alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning techniques can effectively classify medulloblastoma subgroups based on RNA sequencing data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms are effective in classifying medulloblastoma subgroups using gene expression data.
alignments.rationale: Both claims state that ML can classify tumor subgroups; the publication uses the broader term "gene expression" rather than specifically "RNA sequencing", so the claim is clarified and slightly broadened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Reduced feature sets significantly enhance clustering and classification performance for Group 3 and Group 4 medulloblastoma subgroups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Reduced feature sets significantly enhanced clustering and classification performance for medulloblastoma subgroups, particularly for group 3 and group 4.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result, explicitly noting the benefit for groups 3 and 4 as in the preprint, with no substantive change in meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Random Forest, K-nearest Neighbors, and Support Vector Machine classifiers achieve classification accuracies exceeding 90% for certain medulloblastoma subgroups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest, K-nearest neighbors, and support vector machine classifiers consistently outperformed the decision tree and naive Bayes classifiers, achieving classification accuracies exceeding 90% in many scenarios.
alignments.rationale: Both specify the same classifiers and >90% accuracy; the publication adds comparative context but does not alter the core claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of feature selection techniques improves model performance in classifying medulloblastoma subgroups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integration of feature selection techniques substantially improves model performance in classifying medulloblastoma subgroups.
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens wording ("substantially") but retains the same finding that feature selection improves performance, so it is a clarified restatement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed one-step doubly robust and locally efficient estimator called TNDDR improves adjustment for measured confounders when estimating COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness among community-dwelling people aged ≥ 60 years in Québec.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We propose a one-step doubly robust and locally efficient estimator called TNDDR to improve adjustment for measured confounders when estimating COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same proposed estimator and purpose; wording is consistent and the claim is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The efficient influence function (EIF) for the marginal expectation of the outcome under a vaccination intervention has been derived.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We derive the efficient influence function (EIF) for the marginal expectation of the outcome under a vaccination intervention.
alignments.rationale: The content (EIF derivation) is unchanged, but the publication labels the result with lower certainty (medium), so confidence is slightly tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Conditions for consistency, asymptotic normality, and double robustness of TNDDR have been established.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed estimator TNDDR is supported by both theoretical and empirical justifications.
alignments.rationale: The publication summarizes theoretical/empirical support rather than explicitly enumerating mathematical conditions; this clarifies the presentation and slightly tempers the explicitness of the preprint's formal claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed estimator TNDDR is supported by both theoretical and empirical justifications.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed estimator TNDDR is supported by both theoretical and empirical justifications.
alignments.rationale: This claim is carried into the publication verbatim in meaning and emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effective reproductive number R_t is estimated based on serological data using Bayesian inference.
alignments.rationale: No equivalent claim about estimating R_t from serological data appears in the publication; the publication does not report this specific analysis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A forecast of the spread of coronavirus in Mexico is presented based on a contact tracing model using a data-driven Bayesian inference approach.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A projection of the spread of coronavirus in Mexico is based on a contact tracing model using Bayesian inference.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same projection using a contact-tracing Bayesian model; wording is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine Learning is used to analyze comorbidity associated with coronavirus to predict patient care type and survival.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine Learning is used to analyze the comorbidity associated with coronavirus and predict patient care type and survival.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this claim with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two classifiers are implemented to predict the type of care procedure and the survival of patients diagnosed with coronavirus.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two classifiers are implemented to predict the type of care procedure and the survival or mortality of COVID-19 patients with chronic diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the target population (COVID-19 patients with chronic diseases) and mentions mortality explicitly, refining the preprint's description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Techniques are presented to address unbalanced datasets related to outpatient/hospitalized and survived/deceased cases in coronavirus data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Techniques are presented to address unbalanced datasets related to outpatient/hospitalised and survived/deceased cases for better classification performance.
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same methodological contribution addressing class imbalance for those outcome categories.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combined machine learning-based algorithm and mass spectrometry can discriminate COVID-19 in plasma samples within minutes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combined machine learning-based algorithm and mass spectrometry platform can discriminate COVID-19 in plasma samples within minutes.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the exact result and context; wording is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The screening approach demonstrates specificity greater than 97% and sensitivity greater than 83% from blinded data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The diagnostic method demonstrated specificity greater than 96% and sensitivity greater than 83% for COVID-19 diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: Sensitivity is the same, but the reported specificity is slightly lower (>96% vs >97%), so the numerical claim is revised though the diagnostic performance claim remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Twenty-one molecules related to the disease's pathophysiology and twenty-six features related to patients' health outcomes were identified.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified 19 molecules related to the disease's pathophysiology that can assist in patient management and decision-making.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports fewer molecules (19 vs 21) and omits mention of the 26 patient-outcome features, narrowing and slightly tempering the original identification claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This screening approach is understood as a tool with great potential for real-world application.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified 19 molecules related to the disease's pathophysiology that can assist in patient management and decision-making.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes practical utility by linking identified molecules to patient management, which clarifies and grounds the preprint's more speculative statement about real-world potential.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Empirically observed adherence was demonstrated to be the strongest predictor of future adherence/non-adherence.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Empirically observed adherence was demonstrated to be the strongest predictor of future adherence/non-adherence.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this result directly with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification models accurately predicted adherence across the trial with an AUC of 0.83.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification models accurately predicted adherence across the trial with an AUC of 0.83.
alignments.rationale: Identical performance claim retained in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification models accurately predicted adherence for the subsequent week with an AUC of 0.87.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification models accurately predicted adherence for the subsequent week and day with an AUC of 0.87.
alignments.rationale: The publication combines week and day predictions but preserves the same AUC for the subsequent-week claim, so the preprint claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification models accurately predicted adherence for the subsequent day with an AUC of 0.87.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification models accurately predicted adherence for the subsequent week and day with an AUC of 0.87.
alignments.rationale: Although the publication reports week and day together, the day prediction and AUC are preserved, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Real-time measurement of dosing can be utilized to dynamically predict medication adherence with high accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Remote, real-time measurement of medication dosing can facilitate dynamic prediction of risk for medication non-adherence.
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases the conclusion to emphasize facilitating dynamic risk prediction for non-adherence, clarifying the application while keeping the original meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A new structural biomarker for detecting glaucoma progression based on structural transformation of the optic nerve head (ONH) region has been presented.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our validation of the biomarker using ONH sequences from controlled experimental conditions confirms the diagnostic accuracy of the biomarkers observed in the clinical population.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not simply restate presentation of a new biomarker but reports validation and confirmation of diagnostic accuracy in controlled data, which clarifies and strengthens the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning methods trained to estimate generic deformation were able to detect ONH deformation from confocal images and provided a higher diagnostic accuracy compared to classical optical flow and legacy biomarkers of glaucoma progression.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning methods trained to estimate generic deformation were able to estimate ONH deformation from image sequences and provided a higher diagnostic accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the core result about DL methods estimating ONH deformation with higher accuracy but omits explicit mention of the specific comparators, slightly narrowing the framing while keeping confidence similar.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The area under the ROC curves (AUC) for various methods demonstrated diagnostic accuracies ranging from 0.82 to 0.94 for the LEGS study and 0.82 to 0.93 for the DIGS study.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the ROC curve (AUC) for various methods indicates that the POD method achieved the highest diagnostic accuracy of 0.94 for the LEGS study.
alignments.rationale: The publication highlights a specific top-performing method and a single AUC value (0.94) rather than reporting the full ranges given in the preprint, revising and narrowing the numerical emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lower diagnostic accuracy of the learning-based methods for LEG study eyes was attributed to image alignment errors in confocal sequences.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lower diagnostic accuracy of the learning-based methods for LEG study eyes were due to image alignment errors in confocal sequences.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same limitation attributing lower accuracy to image alignment errors with essentially identical meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of deep learning methods can be further improved by fine-tuning these networks using longitudinal ONH sequences.
alignments.rationale: The publication contains no explicit recommendation or claim about fine-tuning with longitudinal ONH sequences, so this suggested improvement is not present in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Support vector machine (SVM) demonstrated a promising detection accuracy after 32 features were detected to be significantly associated with the COVID-19 severeness.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Support vector machine (SVM) demonstrated a promising detection accuracy after 32 features were detected to be significantly associated with the COVID-19 severeness.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim with equivalent certainty and framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The final SVM model was trained using 28 features and achieved an overall accuracy of 0.8148.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The final SVM model was trained using 28 features and achieved an overall accuracy of 0.8148.
alignments.rationale: The numerical result and description are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work may facilitate the risk estimation of whether the COVID-19 patients would develop severe symptoms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work may facilitate the risk estimation of whether the COVID-19 patients would develop severe symptoms.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about potential utility for risk estimation is carried forward without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 28 COVID-19 severeness associated biomarkers may also be investigated for their underlining mechanisms how they were involved in the COVID-19 infections.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 28 COVID-19 severeness associated biomarkers may also be investigated for their underlining mechanisms how they were involved in the COVID-19 infections.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same claim but presents it with higher stated certainty (medium vs low), effectively amplifying confidence while preserving scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning algorithms can predict the therapy response of GEJ patients to neoadjuvant chemotherapy based on histological tissue samples.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning algorithms can predict treatment response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in patients with GEJ adenocarcinoma using histologic tissue samples.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the primary result about DL prediction from histologic samples with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best pre-therapy network achieves an area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) of 0.81 and a balanced accuracy of 0.78.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best pretherapy deep learning network achieves an AUROC of 0.81 and a balanced accuracy of 0.78.
alignments.rationale: The numeric performance (0.81) and balanced accuracy are maintained but the reported metric changed from AUPRC to AUROC in the publication, a substantive revision of the performance metric.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best on-therapy network achieves an AUROC of 0.84 and a balanced accuracy of 0.80.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best on-therapy deep learning network achieves an AUROC of 0.84 and a balanced accuracy of 0.80.
alignments.rationale: This claim is reproduced in the publication with identical metrics and framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest that the networks can predict treatment response using WSI with high accuracy even pre-therapy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results suggest the presence of predictive morphologic tissue biomarkers for treatment response.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the implication from model performance to the existence of morphologic tissue biomarkers, narrowing the emphasis from algorithmic capability to biological interpretability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This predictive capability could lead to earlier therapy intensification compared to the current PET/CT diagnostic system for non-responders.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state the clinical-action recommendation about earlier therapy intensification relative to PET/CT, so this prospective clinical claim was not included.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Differentiating post-vaccination side-effects alone from early COVID-19 was challenging, with a sensitivity in identification of individuals testing positive of 0.6 at best.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Differentiating post-vaccination side-effects alone from early COVID-19 was challenging, with a sensitivity in identification of individuals testing positive of 0.6 at best.
alignments.rationale: The result about low sensitivity is reproduced verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A majority of individuals testing positive for SARS-CoV-2 did not have fever, persistent cough, or anosmia/dysosmia, which are requisite symptoms for accessing UK testing.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most individuals who tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 did not have fever, persistent cough, or anosmia/dysosmia, which are requisite symptoms for accessing UK testing.
alignments.rationale: The publication carries forward the same observation about the absence of the requisite symptoms among positive cases.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Post-vaccination side-effects per se cannot be differentiated from COVID-19 with clinical robustness, either using symptom profiles or machine-derived models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Post-vaccination symptoms per se cannot be differentiated from COVID-19 with clinical robustness, either using symptom profiles or machine-derived models.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about inability to robustly differentiate symptoms is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individuals presenting with systemic symptoms post-vaccination should be tested for SARS-CoV-2 to prevent community spread.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals presenting with systemic symptoms post-vaccination should be tested for SARS-CoV-2 or quarantining, to prevent community spread.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the testing recommendation and adds quarantining as an alternative precaution, clarifying containment measures without changing scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the critical importance of testing symptomatic individuals, even if vaccinated, to ensure early detection of SARS-CoV-2 infection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals presenting with systemic symptoms post-vaccination should be tested for SARS-CoV-2 or quarantining, to prevent community spread.
alignments.rationale: This broader statement about the importance of testing symptomatic (including vaccinated) individuals is represented by the publication's testing/quarantine recommendation with equivalent intent and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI-DP system demonstrates encouraging preliminary results in its diagnostic performance compared to the manual screening method.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Preliminary results indicate that the diagnostic performance of the AI-DP system is encouraging.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports encouraging preliminary performance but omits the explicit comparison to manual screening present in the preprint, narrowing the comparative claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The comprehensive evaluation of the AI-DP will assess its diagnostic performance, repeatability, time-to-result, cost-efficiency, and usability.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The comprehensive evaluation will assess the non-inferiority of the AI-DP system compared to manual screening.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the non-inferiority evaluation versus manual screening rather than enumerating all specific evaluation metrics listed in the preprint, narrowing the stated evaluation scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study aims to verify the non-inferiority of the AI-DP over the manual screening method in two STH endemic countries.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The comprehensive evaluation will assess the non-inferiority of the AI-DP system compared to manual screening.
alignments.rationale: The core objective to evaluate non-inferiority versus manual screening is preserved in the publication with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results of this study will provide evidence-based data to inform health decision-makers about the AI-DP's suitability for large-scale deworming programs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study aims to provide evidence to determine if the AI-DP is a cost-efficient end-to-end diagnostic for large-scale deworming programs.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the intent to inform decision-makers about suitability for large programs but highlights cost-efficiency specifically, clarifying the nature of the evidence sought.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The methodology and statistics from this study may support the development of AI-DP technologies for other neglected tropical diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make an explicit claim about methodological generalizability to other neglected tropical diseases, so this forward-looking contribution is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The adapted 3D StyleGANv2 architecture generated high-quality and diverse synthetic TOF MRA volumes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The adapted 3D StyleGANv2 architecture generated high-quality and diverse synthetic TOF MRA volumes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result claim verbatim about the adapted 3D StyleGANv2 producing high-quality, diverse synthetic TOF MRA volumes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multiclass vessel segmentation models trained on synthetic data alone achieved comparable performance to models trained using real data in most arteries. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multiclass vessel segmentation models trained on synthetic data alone achieved comparable performance to models trained using real data in most arteries. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result that segmentation models trained only on synthetic data achieved comparable performance to real-data-trained models for most arteries.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Generative modeling of the Circle of Willis via synthesis of 3D TOF MRA data paves the way for generalizable deep learning applications in cerebrovascular disease. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Generative modeling of the Circle of Willis via synthesis of 3D TOF MRA data paves the way for generalizable deep learning applications in cerebrovascular disease. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this high-level conclusion about the potential of generative modeling for generalizable deep learning applications in cerebrovascular disease.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed methods produce synthetic data with limited anatomical fidelity or downstream utility in tasks concerning vessel characteristics. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include a corresponding limitation about limited anatomical fidelity or downstream utility; instead it adds a claim about synthetic data improving underrepresented-segment performance, so the limitation claim from the preprint is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Medical students expressed predominantly positive attitudes towards the use of AI in healthcare, with 67.6% supporting its integration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Medical, dental, and veterinary students reported positive attitudes towards AI in healthcare. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication broadens the sampled population to include dental and veterinary students and reports positive attitudes generally, omitting the specific 67.6% figure from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A significant majority of students, 76.1%, expressed a desire for more AI teaching in their curricula. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Students expressed a strong desire for more AI teaching in their education. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report a strong desire for more AI teaching; the publication restates this result without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Despite positive attitudes, 75.3% of students reported limited general knowledge of AI and 76.3% noted the absence of AI-related courses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The majority of students had limited AI knowledge and felt unprepared to use AI in their careers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same finding about limited AI knowledge and extends it to feeling unprepared for future use, summarizing rather than changing the substantive result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study reveals regional differences in perceptions of AI among medical students, although these differences predominantly have small effect sizes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are significant regional differences in attitudes towards AI between the Global North and South. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the regional variation as significant and specifies a Global North vs South difference, which strengthens and narrows the preprint's more cautious description of small effect sizes across regions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study highlights the importance of integrating AI teaching into medical education to better prepare students for its use in their future careers. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identifies a need for integrating AI education into medical curricula worldwide. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same conclusion but frames it as a worldwide need for integrating AI education, broadening the scope relative to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracies of cross-validation methods inflated the performance estimation compared with those of a held-out test, compromising the model generalizability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracies of cross-validation methods inflated performance estimations compared to held-out test methods, which compromised model generalizability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same methodological result about cross-validation overestimating performance relative to held-out tests and the effect on generalizability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Test accuracies have increased with publication year but were not associated with training sample sizes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most studies reported classification accuracies for MRI-based diagnostic classifiers for ADHD, but these accuracies varied significantly due to differences in MRI modalities, ML models, and sample sizes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes that accuracies vary with factors including sample size, which contradicts the preprint's claim of no association with training sample size, indicating a revision of that specific point.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Improved test accuracy over time was likely due to the use of better ML methods along with strategies to deal with data imbalances. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Test accuracies for MRI-based classifiers have increased over time, likely due to the adoption of better ML methods and strategies to address data imbalances. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same causal interpretation for increased test accuracy over time, attributing it to better methods and imbalance-handling strategies.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Large multi-modal imaging datasets, and potentially the combination with other types of data, will be essential to achieve the goal of developing clinically useful imaging classification tools for ADHD in the future. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Large multi-modal imaging datasets, combined with other data types such as cognitive data and genetics, will be essential for developing clinically useful imaging classification tools for ADHD. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions make the same recommendation about the necessity of large multi-modal datasets, but the publication expresses the claim with slightly lower certainty (medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We found differences between PD patients with and without depression in the alpha band globally and in the beta and gamma bands in the central electrodes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Differences in EEG signal features were found between PD patients with and without depression in the alpha, beta, and gamma bands. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports differences across the same frequency bands but omits the electrode-level spatial detail (global vs central), thereby clarifying and narrowing the spatial specifics of the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LEAPD-based machine learning could robustly distinguish between PD patients with and without depression with 97% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LEAPD-based machine learning algorithm could distinguish between PD patients with and without depression with 97% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance figure and claim for the LEAPD algorithm distinguishing PD patients with vs without depression.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LEAPD-based machine learning could distinguish between PD patients with depression and non-PD patients with depression with 100% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LEAPD algorithm achieved 100% accuracy in distinguishing between PD patients with depression and non-PD patients with depression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact performance claim of 100% accuracy for that comparison.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrated the efficacy of the LEAPD algorithm in identifying PD patients with depression from PD patients without depression and controls with depression. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Resting-state EEG power spectral analysis has the potential to accurately distinguish depression in PD. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication couches the conclusion more cautiously as 'potential' and with medium certainty, tempering the stronger efficacy conclusion from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our data provide insight into cortical mechanisms of depression and could lead to novel neurophysiologically-based biomarkers for non-motor symptoms of PD. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings provide insight into cortical mechanisms of depression and could lead to novel neurophysiological markers for non-motor symptoms of PD. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution about insights into cortical mechanisms and the potential for new neurophysiological markers with equivalent framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning models developed demonstrated superior predictive performance for assessing the risk of malignancy in pulmonary nodules. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LASSO model yielded the best predictive performance in cross-validation for assessing the risk of malignancy in pulmonary nodules. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that the LASSO model in particular yielded the best performance, narrowing the preprint's broader claim about 'the machine learning models' overall.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LASSO model was identified as the optimal model due to its superior calibration compared to other machine learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LASSO model yielded the best predictive performance in cross-validation for assessing the risk of malignancy in pulmonary nodules. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication supports LASSO as the best-performing model but removes the specific justification about superior calibration, revising the rationale behind its selection.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The radiomics model achieved a test-set AUC of 0.930, outperforming the established Brock model which had an AUC of 0.868. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our radiomics model achieved a test-set AUC of 0.93, outperforming the established Pan-Canadian Early Detection of Lung Cancer model which had an AUC of 0.87. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports essentially the same AUC figures but clarifies the comparator model name (Pan-Canadian model) and rounds the comparator AUC, preserving the substantive performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed models may be suitable for assessing suspicious, but indeterminate, screen-detected pulmonary nodules for risk of malignancy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We developed highly accurate machine learning models based on radiomic and epidemiological features that may be suitable for assessing indeterminate screen-detected pulmonary nodules. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions conclude that the developed models may be suitable for assessing indeterminate/suspicious screen-detected nodules; the publication adds detail about feature types but preserves the conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We found evidence supporting the existence of three distinct methylation subtypes in thyroid cancer.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The unsupervised algorithm identified that samples could be classified into three subtypes based solely on their methylation profile.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same core result (three methylation subtypes) but adds methodological detail (unsupervised algorithm and methylation-only basis), which clarifies the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Follicular adenomas, follicular carcinomas, oncocytic adenomas, oncocytic carcinomas, and NIFTP samples shared numerous epigenetic alterations, with a predominance of hypermethylation events.
alignments.pub_claim_text: These methylation subtypes were strongly associated with histological diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the specific observation about shared epigenetic alterations as an association between methylation subtypes and histology; this is consistent but presents the finding at a higher-level, clarifying its relation to diagnosis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Follicular variant papillary thyroid carcinomas (FVPTC) were as likely to be classified as follicular-like or PTC-like, indicating a heterogeneous group likely to be formed by at least two distinct diseases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Follicular variant PTC samples were split into two methylation clusters, indicating a heterogeneous group likely to be formed by two distinct diseases.
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the same observation (FVPTC split into two clusters and heterogeneity implying two diseases) with near-identical meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: FVPTC with follicular-like methylation patterns were enriched for RAS mutations, while those with PTC-like methylation patterns were enriched for BRAF and RET alterations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: FVPTC samples with a follicular-like methylation pattern were enriched for RAS mutations, whereas FVPTC with PTC-like methylation patterns were enriched for BRAF V600E mutations and RET fusions.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same mutation-methylation pattern associations, adding specific mutation nomenclature (BRAF V600E, RET fusions) without changing the substantive result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results offer a robust background to support the classification of thyroid neoplasms based on methylation patterns.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification method provides a robust background to support the classification of thyroid neoplasms based on methylation patterns.
alignments.rationale: Both claims make the same concluding statement about the robustness of methylation-based classification; wording differs minimally.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Subjective Cognitive Decline (SCD) is associated with a higher prevalence of Alzheimer’s pathology compared to the general population.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Subjective Cognitive Decline (SCD) is suggested as an early symptomatic phase of Alzheimer's disease (AD).
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the specific epidemiological association as a broader conceptual positioning of SCD as an early symptomatic phase, with slightly lower stated certainty, representing a revision and tempering of the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PREVIEW study aims to identify features and tools to accurately detect SCD patients who will progress to Alzheimer’s disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study aims to identify features from non-invasive assessments that can accurately detect SCD patients who will progress to AD dementia.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same aim but clarifies that it focuses on features from non-invasive assessments and frames progression as to AD dementia; this refines the original contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Previous studies have identified various features that can stratify SCD patients according to their risk of progression to objective cognitive decline.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate this background-summary claim about prior stratification studies, so this specific claim appears omitted in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PREVIEW study will utilize machine learning algorithms to define the risk of progression from SCD to mild cognitive impairment and Alzheimer’s disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This is the first study to investigate the application of machine learning to predict AD in patients with SCD.
alignments.rationale: Both statements concern applying machine learning to predict progression in SCD; the paper emphasizes novelty (first study) which clarifies and frames the original methodological claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a need for cost-effective and easily accessible tools to identify patients at the earliest stages of Alzheimer’s disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The expected results may provide evidence for defining cost-effective and globally scalable tools to estimate the risk of AD.
alignments.rationale: The publication recasts the need as a potential outcome of the study (expected results may provide evidence), which slightly tempers the original normative claim while keeping the same objective.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performing infection risk model achieves a cross-validated AUC of 0.88 at 1-hour before clinical suspicion.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing infection risk model achieves a cross-validated AUC of 0.88 at 1 hour before clinical suspicion.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the identical quantitative performance claim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model maintains an AUC greater than 0.85 for 48-hours before suspicion by aggregating information across demographics and a set of 163 vital signs and laboratory measurements.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model maintains an AUC greater than 0.85 for 48 hours before suspicion by aggregating information across demographics and a set of 163 vital signs and laboratory measurements.
alignments.rationale: Same quantitative and methodological claim is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A second model trained on a reduced feature space can still achieve an AUC of 0.86 at 1-hour before clinical suspicion.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A second model trained on a reduced feature space can still achieve an AUC of 0.86 at 1 hour before clinical suspicion.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same secondary model performance claim without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive model provides a continuous risk score of infection that can be deployed in hospitals to provide advance warning of patient deterioration.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive model provides a continuous risk score of infection that can be deployed in hospitals to provide advance warning of patient deterioration.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same deployment/conclusion but with higher stated certainty (publication labels it as a high-certainty conclusion), representing an amplification of confidence in the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CPFE patients with >10% emphysema have a greater smoking history and lower baseline DLco compared to CPFE patients with <10% emphysema.
alignments.pub_claim_text: CPFE patients with 610% emphysema have a greater smoking history and lower baseline DLCO compared to those with <10% emphysema.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparison and findings; the small notation change (>10% vs 610%) does not change meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In patients with >10% emphysema, 1-year DLco decline is a better indicator of mortality than 1-year FVC decline.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In patients with 610% emphysema, 1-year DLCO decline shows stronger mortality associations than 1-year FVC decline.
alignments.rationale: Both versions convey the same result that DLCO decline has stronger mortality associations than FVC decline in patients with 610% emphysema.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The unsupervised machine learning approach identified CPFE patients in whom FVC decline did not associate strongly with mortality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The unsupervised machine-learning approach identified CPFE patients in whom FVC decline did not associate strongly with mortality.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological/result claim about the subgroup identified by unsupervised learning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In non-CPFE IPF patients, 1-year FVC declines >5% and >10% show comparable mortality associations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In non-CPFE IPF patients, 1-year FVC declines 65% and 10% show strong mortality associations.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes that both thresholds show strong mortality associations (rather than stating they are comparable); this reframes the original wording while preserving the substantive observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DLco decline should be considered in patients with >10% emphysema when assessing disease progression in IPF.
alignments.pub_claim_text: When assessing disease progression in IPF, DLCO decline should be considered in patients with 610% emphysema.
alignments.rationale: The recommendation is preserved in the publication but its stated certainty is slightly lower (publication labels the conclusion with medium rather than high certainty), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models using baseline EEG features can predict phenoconversion time in patients with iRBD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models using baseline EEG features can predict phenoconversion time and subtype in patients with iRBD.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds subtype prediction to the original claim about phenoconversion time prediction, broadening the scope while keeping the original assertion intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The random survival forest model achieved an integrated Brier score of 0.114 and a concordance index of 0.775 for survival prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random survival forest model achieved an integrated Brier score of 0.114 and a concordance index of 0.775 for survival prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact reported performance metrics for the random survival forest model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The K-nearest neighbor model demonstrated an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.901 for subtype prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The K-nearest neighbor model had an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.901 for subtype prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication carries forward the same numeric performance claim for the KNN subtype model without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EEG slowing was identified as an important feature for both survival and subtype prediction models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Slowing of the EEG was identified as an important feature for both survival and subtype prediction models.
alignments.rationale: Both versions identify EEG slowing as an important predictive feature; wording is equivalent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mechanisms behind missing values in Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are often not random with respect to important characteristics of the patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analyzing patterns of missing data and measurement frequencies provides valuable insights into patient monitoring practices and potential systemic disparities in healthcare delivery.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's general claim about non-random missingness as an actionable recommendation to analyze patterns for insights; it is focused more on insights and disparities (narrower ICU/monitoring context) and the certainty is somewhat reduced.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results expose underlying differences in the sampling frequency and missing data patterns of vital sign measurements and laboratory tests between different demographic groups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Significant demographic systematic disparities were observed in the first 24 hours of ICU stays.
alignments.rationale: Both claims report demographic differences in measurement patterns, but the publication specifies the setting (first 24 h of ICU), narrowing the scope while retaining the result's strength.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These measurement patterns can provide significant predictive insights into patient outcomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Measurement patterns were associated with ICU mortality, with models based solely on these patterns achieving an AUC of 0.76.
alignments.rationale: The publication gives a concrete outcome (ICU mortality) and model performance (AUC 0.76), which clarifies and narrows the preprint's general claim about predictive insights.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We urge a reevaluation of the conventional understanding of missing data and sampling frequencies in EHRs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analyzing patterns of missing data and measurement frequencies provides valuable insights into patient monitoring practices and potential systemic disparities in healthcare delivery.
alignments.rationale: The publication echoes the recommendation to analyze missingness and sampling frequencies but frames it in terms of gaining insights into monitoring and disparities, narrowing the context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Nallasamy formula achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.312 Diopters in the testing dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Nallasamy formula demonstrated a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.312 Dioptres in the testing dataset.
alignments.rationale: The numeric MAE is identical in preprint and publication, with no substantive change in claim or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Nallasamy formula performed significantly better than Barrett Universal II and four other existing methods based on the paired Wilcoxon test with Bonferroni correction (p-value < 0.05).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the Nallasamy formula was statistically significant compared to the existing methods based on the paired Wilcoxon test with Bonferroni correction (p<0.05).
alignments.rationale: Both state the same statistical test and significance; the publication omits the specific named comparator in this sentence but preserves the statistical result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Nallasamy formula outperformed the five methods studied on overall MAE and MedAE, as well as the percentage of eyes within 0.5 D of prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Nallasamy formula outperformed seven existing methods in terms of overall MAE and median absolute error (MedAE).
alignments.rationale: The publication changes the count of compared methods (five -> seven) and omits the % within 0.5 D; performance claim is altered (revised) and effectively broadened to more comparators.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Nallasamy formula demonstrated improved prediction accuracy for IOL power selection in cataract patients compared to traditional methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Nallasamy formula outperformed seven existing methods in terms of overall MAE and median absolute error (MedAE).
alignments.rationale: The publication supports improved accuracy versus existing methods but adds context (comparison to more methods) and the paper also introduces a caveat about clinical significance at population level, which tempers the practical implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning approach demonstrated high performance in predicting early good to excellent clinical results.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning approach showed high performance in predicting early good to excellent clinical results.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the conclusion but with slightly lower certainty (medium vs high), so the claim is clarified and the confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model for predicting ODI at 6 months follow up achieved a sensitivity of 74.3% and specificity of 79.4%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model for predicting ODI at 6 months' follow up demonstrated a good balance between sensitivity (74.3%) and specificity (79.4%), with an accuracy of 75.8% and ROC AUC = 0.842.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same sensitivity and specificity but adds accuracy and AUC, clarifying and strengthening the reported performance metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combined target model showed a sensitivity of 74.2% and specificity of 71.8%, with an accuracy of 72.8%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combined target model showed a sensitivity of 74.2%, specificity of 71.8%, accuracy of 72.8%, and ROC AUC = 0.808.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same sensitivity, specificity and accuracy while adding ROC AUC, clarifying the model's performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ROC AUC for the ODI prediction model was 0.842, indicating good predictive performance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model for predicting ODI at 6 months' follow up demonstrated a good balance between sensitivity (74.3%) and specificity (79.4%), with an accuracy of 75.8% and ROC AUC = 0.842.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same AUC and frames it within the full set of performance metrics; the numeric claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ROC AUC for the combined target model was 0.808, suggesting reasonable predictive capability.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combined target model showed a sensitivity of 74.2%, specificity of 71.8%, accuracy of 72.8%, and ROC AUC = 0.808.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same ROC AUC and associated performance metrics, so the claim is unchanged and clarified by additional context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Body-worn sensor systems are technically feasible and well-tolerated in subjects with recent stroke.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Body-worn sensor systems are technically feasible and well tolerated in subjects with recent stroke.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates feasibility and tolerability but with slightly lower certainty (medium vs high), so the claim is clarified and confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine-learning algorithm achieved a repetition counting accuracy of 95.6 ± 2.4 % overall.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study achieved a repetition counting accuracy of 95.6% overall using body-worn sensors.
alignments.rationale: The reported overall repetition counting accuracy is the same (95.6%), with the publication omitting the reported SD; the core numeric result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Accuracy in counting exercise repetitions decreased when using fewer sensors or using accelerometry alone.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report a general claim that accuracy decreased with fewer sensors or with accelerometry alone; instead it reports subgroup accuracies, so the specific preprint claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study may ultimately be useful for developing a system to measure total exercise 'dose' in post-stroke patients during clinical rehabilitation or clinical trials.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study indicates that body-worn sensors may be useful for measuring total exercise 'dose' in poststroke patients during rehabilitation.
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same contribution claim but with higher certainty (medium vs low), thereby amplifying confidence in potential usefulness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Proposed models can predict COVID-19 qRT-PCR results in symptomatic individuals with high accuracy, sensitivity and specificity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Proposed models can predict COVID-19 qRT-PCR results in symptomatic individuals with high accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's core claim about high predictive performance with similar certainty and wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Resource distribution is significantly optimized when model-based patient selection is observed, compared to random choice.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Resource allocation can be significantly improved when model-based patient selection is observed, compared to random choice.
alignments.rationale: The publication echoes the preprint's resource allocation benefit but frames it with slightly lower certainty (medium) and uses somewhat different wording (allocation vs distribution).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models can be derived from widely available, quick, and inexpensive exam data in order to predict qRT-PCR results used in COVID-19 diagnosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models can be derived from widely available, quick, and inexpensive exam data to predict qRT-PCR results used in COVID-19 diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological/feasibility claim with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These models can be used to assist strategic decision-making in resource scarcity scenarios, including personnel shortage, lack of medical resources, and testing insufficiency.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Resource allocation can be significantly improved when model-based patient selection is observed, compared to random choice.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents improved allocation as an outcome of model use, which aligns with the preprint's claim about assisting strategic decision-making in scarcity scenarios, though the publication does not enumerate specific scarcity contexts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification model trained using chronic medicated SSD classified SSD from healthy controls with 69% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classifier achieved 69% accuracy when tested on an independent dataset of SSD patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim reports the same accuracy on an independent dataset with equivalent certainty and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model can identify chronic medicated SSD with 71% sensitivity but cannot be generalized to first episode unmedicated SSD or first episode medicated SSD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Subgroup analysis showed 71% sensitivity for chronic medicated SSD, but poor generalization to first-episode unmedicated SSD with a sensitivity of 48%. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the 71% sensitivity but provides a quantified poor generalization (48%) for first-episode unmedicated cases and omits the explicit statement about first-episode medicated cases, narrowing and clarifying the original claim while lowering stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Medication usage has a significant effect on functional connectivity, while disease duration does not. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a significant association between functional connectivity and medication usage, but not with disease duration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same association and non-association with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification model may primarily identify the pattern of chronic medication usage state rather than the trait biomarker of SSD. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classifiers developed on chronic medicated SSD may predominantly capture state features, overshadowing potential SSD traits. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements convey the same conclusion that classifiers capture medication-related state features rather than SSD trait biomarkers with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current machine learning studies in chronic medicated SSD should be reconsidered more cautiously in terms of clinical application. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classifiers developed on chronic medicated SSD may predominantly capture state features, overshadowing potential SSD traits. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same concern about clinical applicability by emphasizing that classifiers capture state features; this clarifies the basis for the suggested caution though it is not an identical wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine-learning model predicts risk of severe disease progression in Covid-19 patients at hospital admission. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine-learning model predicts risk of severe disease progression in Covid-19 patients at hospital admission. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-level conclusion with identical scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In the development cohort, the model achieved an AUC of 0.85 for predicting severity of disease progression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the development cohort, the model achieved an AUC of 0.85 for predicting severity of disease progression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact same performance result reported for the development cohort.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In the external testing cohort, the model performed with an AUC of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the external testing cohort, the model achieved an AUC of 0.83 for predicting severity of disease progression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical external-validation performance reported with same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model identifies patients who will die or require mechanical ventilation during hospitalization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model identifies patients who will die or require mechanical ventilation during hospitalization based on clinical and laboratory features obtained at admission. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds that predictions are based on admission clinical and laboratory features and increases the certainty/impact framing, thus clarifying and amplifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Logistic regression models achieved the best performance on clinical features with an AUC of 0.848, sensitivity of 0.455, and specificity of 0.906. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Logistic regression models achieved the best performance for differentiating between mild and severe COVID-19 cases, with an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.950 for combined features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication attributes the best performance to logistic regression but reports the AUC for combined features (0.950), which revises and broadens the original claim that tied logistic regression best performance specifically to clinical features with AUC 0.848.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Imaging features achieved an AUC of 0.926, sensitivity of 0.818, and specificity of 0.901, indicating their strong predictive power. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Imaging features had the strongest impact on the model output for automated severity assessment of COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the dominant role of imaging features (impact on model output) rather than restating exact metrics, clarifying their relative importance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of clinical and imaging features yielded the best performance overall with an AUC of 0.950, sensitivity of 0.764, and specificity of 0.919. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A combination of clinical and imaging features yielded the best performance overall in predicting COVID-19 severity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert that combined features produced the best overall performance; the publication restates this result at the same level.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SMOTE oversampling method improved the performance of the combined features to an AUC of 0.960, with sensitivity of 0.845 and specificity of 0.929. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The synthetic minority oversampling method further improved the performance of the model using combined features, achieving an AUC of 0.960. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same SMOTE performance improvement and metric.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study indicates that clinical and imaging features can be used for automated severity assessment of COVID-19 patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clinical and imaging features can potentially help triage patients with COVID-19 and prioritize care delivery to those at a higher risk of severe disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication extends the conclusion by specifying a clinical application (triage and prioritization), broadening the stated utility of combined features.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: None of the 5 ML algorithms predict OUD better than chance when ancestry was balanced but were confounded with ancestry in an out-of-sample test. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: None of the 5 ML algorithms predict OUD better than chance when ancestry was balanced but were confounded with ancestry in an out-of-sample test. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the identical result and interpretation with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithms preferentially predicted admixed subpopulations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithms preferentially predicted admixed subpopulations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is the same but the publication reports a lower certainty level, so the substantive claim is unchanged though confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Genome-wide significant tobacco smoking variants were also confounded by ancestry. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Genome-wide significant tobacco smoking variants were also confounded by ancestry. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same finding reported in the publication but with reduced stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The bias attributable to ancestral confounding could impact any ML-based genetic prediction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The bias attributable to ancestral confounding could impact any ML-based genetic prediction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same cautionary conclusion with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Researchers and clinicians are encouraged to be skeptical of claims of high prediction accuracy from ML-derived genetic algorithms for polygenic traits like addiction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Researchers and clinicians are encouraged to be skeptical of claims of high prediction accuracy from ML-derived genetic algorithms for polygenic traits like addiction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same admonition but reports it with higher stated certainty, amplifying the confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 4CE severity algorithm performed with pooled sensitivity of 0.73 and specificity 0.83 for the combined outcome of ICU admission and/or death. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The full 4CE severity phenotype had pooled sensitivity of 0.73 and specificity 0.83 for the combined outcome of ICU admission and/or death. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim reports the same pooled sensitivity and specificity for the same combined outcome.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A multivariate machine learning approach identified codes resulting in mean AUC 0.956 compared to 0.903 using expert-derived codes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At one pilot site, the expert-derived phenotype had a mean area under the curve of 0.903, compared with an area under the curve of 0.956 for the machine learning approach. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same comparative AUC results between ML and expert-derived phenotypes, matching the preprint's metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Billing codes were poor proxies of ICU admission, with 49% precision and recall compared against chart review at one partner institution. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Billing codes were poor proxies of ICU admission, with as low as 49% precision and recall compared with chart review. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same poor proxy performance (49% precision/recall), matching the preprint's single-site finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed a proxy measure of severity that proved resilient to coding variability internationally by using a set of 6 code classes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We developed an EHR-based severity phenotype for COVID-19 in hospitalized patients and validated it at 12 international sites. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies and strengthens the preprint's claim by reporting validation across 12 international sites and raising certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Miro Health variables show significant correlations with legacy neuropsychological test variables. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Miro Health Mobile Assessment Platform demonstrates concurrent validity with legacy neuropsychological test scores, with correlations ranging from 0.27 to 0.68. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same concurrent validity but adds numeric correlation ranges, clarifying the preprint's general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Miro Health demonstrates minimal learning effects and agreement of subsequent measurements in longitudinal studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The test-retest reliability of the Miro Health Mobile Assessment Platform ranges from 0.25 to 0.79, indicating minimal learning effects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication reports the same conclusion and quantifies test-retest reliability, supporting the preprint's statement about minimal learning effects.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Risk Score effectively distinguishes amnestic MCI from healthy controls with an AUROC of 0.97. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Risk score from the Miro Health Mobile Assessment Platform effectively differentiates individuals with aMCI from HC with an AUROC of 0.97. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result verbatim, including the AUROC value.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Risk Score separates non-amnestic MCI participants from healthy controls with an AUROC of 0.80. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this naMCI vs healthy control comparison; instead it reports a different comparison (aMCI vs naMCI), so the original preprint claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Miro Health provides valid and reliable variable scores that are analogous to legacy neuropsychological variable scores. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Miro Health Mobile Assessment Platform provides valid and reliable assessment of neurobehavioral and cognitive status. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same overall conclusion using slightly different wording, reinforcing validity and reliability.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The Miro Health Mobile Assessment Platform differentiates aMCI from naMCI with an AUROC of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a new result (aMCI vs naMCI AUROC) that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Over 76 genetic loci (comprising 90 SNPs) have been associated with Parkinson's disease by the most recent GWAS meta-analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Over 76 genetic loci (comprising 90 SNPs) have been associated with Parkinson's disease by the most recent GWAS meta-analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same GWAS meta-analysis result without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PD-associated genetic variants modulate disease risk through tissue-specific expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) effects. (certainty=medium, type=hypothesis)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis ranked the tissue-specific transcription effects for PD-associated genetic variants and estimated their relative contributions to PD risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides an explicit analysis and ranking that moves the preprint's hypothesis toward a demonstrated result, increasing confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis ranked the tissue-specific transcription effects for PD-associated genetic variants and estimated their relative contributions to PD risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis ranked the tissue-specific transcription effects for PD-associated genetic variants and estimated their relative contributions to PD risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This claim is carried forward directly from preprint to publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two variants (rs7617877 and rs6808178) and eQTL associated transcriptional changes of EAF1-AS1 within the heart atrial appendage play a key role in the risk of developing Parkinson's disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two variants (rs7617877 and rs6808178) and eQTL associated transcriptional changes of EAF1-AS1 within the heart atrial appendage play a key role in the risk of developing Parkinson's disease. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same finding but presents it with reduced certainty (medium), so the claim is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Effects associated with eQTLs located within the brain cerebellum confer major Parkinson's disease risk. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Effects associated with eQTLs located within the Brain Cerebellum confer major Parkinson's disease risk. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication carries forward the same result regarding cerebellum eQTL effects with similar certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings were replicated in two additional, independent cohorts (the UK Biobank, and NeuroX), warranting further mechanistic investigations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The replication in independent cohorts is reported in the publication but was not present in the preprint, so this is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The random forest model demonstrated higher predictive power than logistic regression for predicting metabolic syndrome onset. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest models have the highest predictive power for metabolic syndrome onset compared to logistic regression models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same model superiority and presents it with higher certainty, amplifying confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Diastolic blood pressure is the most important predictor of metabolic syndrome onset for men aged 30 and 35 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Diastolic blood pressure is the most important predictor of metabolic syndrome onset for males, while body mass index is the most important predictor for females. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes the age-specific preprint finding to males overall and pairs it with the female result, broadening the scope beyond the specified ages.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Body mass index is the most important predictor of metabolic syndrome onset for women aged 30 and 35 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Diastolic blood pressure is the most important predictor of metabolic syndrome onset for males, while body mass index is the most important predictor for females. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the sex-specific predictor finding without the preprint's explicit age restriction, broadening applicability to females generally.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning model was created to predict metabolic syndrome onset at age 40 with high accuracy from health examination data obtained at ages 30 or 35. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models were created to predict metabolic syndrome onset at the age of 40 years with high accuracy from health examination data obtained at the age of 30 or 35 years. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the model development and predictive performance unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study indicates sex differences in important predictors of metabolic syndrome onset. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sex differences in important predictors of metabolic syndrome onset were identified through the variable importance indices of the random forest. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the conclusion that sex differences in predictors were observed, in similar terms.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Applying the predictive model in routine healthcare management could provide early health interventions to prevent metabolic syndrome onset. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an application-oriented recommendation for clinical implementation that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CT radiomics models based on 6 second-order features were effective in discriminating short- and long-term hospital stay in patients with pneumonia associated with SARS-CoV-2 infection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CT radiomics models based on 6 second-order features were effective in discriminating short- and long-term hospital stay in patients with COVID-19 pneumonia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result; terminology changed from SARS-CoV-2 infection to COVID-19 pneumonia without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The areas under the curves for the logistic regression and random forest models were 0.97 and 0.92, respectively, in the test dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The areas under the curves for the logistic regression and random forest models were 0.97 and 0.92, respectively, indicating high predictive performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the exact AUC results and interpretation from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The logistic regression model demonstrated a sensitivity and specificity of 1.0 and 0.89, respectively, while the random forest model showed a sensitivity and specificity of 0.75 and 1.0. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The logistic regression and random forest models demonstrated a sensitivity and specificity of 1.0 and 0.89, 0.75 and 1.0 in the test dataset, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
