alignments.rationale: Sensitivity and specificity values are identical between preprint and publication, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning-based CT radiomics models showed feasibility and accuracy for predicting hospital stay in patients with pneumonia associated with SARS-CoV-2 infection. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning-based CT radiomics features and models showed feasibility and accuracy for predicting hospital stay in patients with COVID-19 pneumonia. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The overall conclusion is preserved, with only minor terminology changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pullback pressure gradient index (PPGi) cut-off of 0.78 achieved 70% accuracy for focal vs. diffuse and 77% accuracy for focal vs. non-focal classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pullback pressure gradient index (PPGi) with a cut-off value of 0.78 distinguishes focal from diffuse and non-focal coronary artery disease (CAD). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same cutoff-based distinction but omits the specific accuracy percentages reported in the preprint, clarifying to a more general statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The penalized logistic regression model, including PPGi as a feature, provided superior accuracy of 95% for focal vs. diffuse and 84% for focal vs. non-focal classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The penalized logistic regression model, including PPGi as a feature, achieved an accuracy of 88% for focal vs. diffuse classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different (lower) accuracy and only the focal vs. diffuse comparison, indicating the preprint's numeric results and broader comparison were revised and narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning method with penalized logistic regression outperformed the PPGi cut-off values, providing robust and generalizable classification across different study populations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning models for physiological patterns of CAD classification outperformed the binary PPGi method. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the main conclusion that ML models outperform the binary PPGi approach but omits the stronger wording about robustness/generalizability, providing a clearer, slightly less strong statement.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest model with PPGi as one of the features provided an accuracy of 73% for multiclass classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a new result about a random forest multiclass accuracy that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model achieved high performance with 74% sensitivity, 75% specificity, 74% overall accuracy, and 77% AUC.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model developed using histogram-based features of diffusion MRI tract profiles achieved high performance with 74% sensitivity and 75% specificity.
alignments.rationale: The performance numbers for sensitivity and specificity match exactly; the publication omits the overall accuracy and AUC but conveys the same core performance result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating patients' demographics and clinical features into the models demonstrated that these features were not as effective as microstructural measures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating patient demographics and clinical features did not improve the model's performance, indicating that microstructural measures are more effective for prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion but more directly (saying demographics did not improve performance) and presents it with lower stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ADC, FA, and RD contributed more to the model than other features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Apparent diffusion coefficient, fractional anisotropy, and radial diffusivity were found to contribute more to the predictive model than other features.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same feature-importance finding using equivalent terminology and emphasis.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Diffusion MRI metrics can reveal detailed insights into white matter microstructural changes associated with development, pathology, and function.
alignments.rationale: This is a broader, general conclusion about diffusion MRI not present in the preprint claims and appears as added contextual/background material in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Tree-based machine learning algorithms can effectively discriminate late onset Alzheimer's disease from age-matched unaffected subjects using single nucleotide variants.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Tree-based machine learning algorithms effectively discriminate late onset Alzheimer's disease from age-matched unaffected subjects using single nucleotide variants.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result claim without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning approach prioritized a set of single nucleotide variants located near the genes PVRL2, TOMM40, APOE, and APOC1.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning approach prioritized specific single nucleotide variants located in the genes PVRL2, TOMM40, APOE, and APOC1, which influence gene expression and splicing.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds functional detail (influence on expression/splicing) to the prioritization result, clarifying the biological implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a clear interaction between the single nucleotide variants rs405509 and rs1160985 in the genomic profiles captured.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Genomic profiles in the identified region show interaction patterns among specific single nucleotide variants, including rs405509 and rs1160985.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same observation about interaction patterns involving those variants.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interaction of rs405509 located in the APOE promoter with rs429358 appears to neutralize their predisposing effect.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The interaction of rs405509 located in the APOE promoter with rs429358 appears to neutralize their predisposing effect for Alzheimer's disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same conclusion about the interaction and its apparent neutralizing effect.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Interactions among single nucleotide variants are associated with specific comorbidities and the presence of eQTL and sQTLs.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the association with specific comorbidities; while it notes effects on expression/splicing for variants, it does not support the broader claim about comorbidity associations, so the claim is not present in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models were developed to predict mortality and cytomegalovirus (CMV) reactivation after allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT) using both baseline patient data and time-dependent laboratory measurements.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models were developed to predict mortality and cytomegalovirus (CMV) reactivation after allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT) using both baseline patient data and time-dependent laboratory measurements.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the development and data modalities of the models as stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gradient boosting machine models achieved areas under the receiver-operating characteristic of 0.92 for mortality prediction and 0.83 for CMV reactivation prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The gradient boosting machine models achieved areas under the receiver-operating characteristic of 0.92 for mortality prediction and 0.83 for CMV reactivation prediction.
alignments.rationale: Identical performance metrics are reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models provided well-calibrated, time-dependent risk predictions within a 21-day time window.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models provided well-calibrated, time-dependent risk predictions within a 21-day time window.
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the same claim about calibration and the 21-day window.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both models were validated in a prospective, non-interventional study and performed comparably to expert hematologists in a pilot comparison.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both models were validated in a prospective, non-interventional study and performed comparably to expert hematologists in a pilot comparison.
alignments.rationale: Validation details and the pilot comparison result are unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning model was trained to predict mortality within 12 weeks of a first positive SARS-CoV-2 test.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model was trained to predict mortality within 12 weeks of a first positive SARS-CoV-2 test.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same model training objective.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model achieved a weighted concordance index of 0.95 and a precision-recall area under the curve of 0.71 on the test set.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved a weighted concordance index of 0.95 and an area under the curve for precision-recall of 0.71 on the test set.
alignments.rationale: Performance metrics are reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Age, sex, number of medications, previous hospitalizations, and lymphocyte counts were identified as top mortality risk factors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Age, sex, number of medications, previous hospitalizations, and lymphocyte counts were identified as top mortality risk factors.
alignments.rationale: The publication lists the same top risk factors.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The explainable survival model revealed temporal dynamics of the 22 selected risk factors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The explainable survival model revealed temporal dynamics of the 22 selected risk factors.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the claim about temporal dynamics from the explainable model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Upon further validation, the model may allow direct reporting of personalized survival probabilities in routine care.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Upon further validation, the model may allow direct reporting of personalized survival probabilities in routine care.
alignments.rationale: The content of the claim is the same but the publication reports a lower certainty (conclusion downgraded from medium to low), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates large area, label-free, and rapid testing sensor platforms for fast and accurate detection of SARS-CoV-2.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The testing platform combines surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy and machine learning for the rapid and accurate detection of SARS-CoV-2.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the SERS+ML implementation behind the rapid/accurate detection claim, providing a more specific technical framing than the preprint's broader statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach can obtain test results typically within 25 minutes with a detection accuracy of at least 83% for viral samples.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Spectroscopic signatures can provide test results within 25 minutes.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the 25-minute timeframe but omits the explicit 83% accuracy figure, narrowing the claim and reducing stated confidence about the numeric accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of SERS enhancing metal insulator metal (MIM) nanostructures enables effective detection of viral samples.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The testing platform combines surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy and machine learning for the rapid and accurate detection of SARS-CoV-2.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms SERS-based detection but does not specifically mention MIM nanostructures; the claim is retained in spirit but the specific nanostructure detail is not emphasized.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach features large area nanopatterning and fabrication in both rigid and flexible formats for wearables.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The large area nanopatterning approach allows sensors to be fabricated on flexible surfaces for versatile applications.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the key contribution about large-area nanopatterning and fabrication on flexible substrates, matching the preprint's claim about flexible/wearable formats.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The technique can accurately distinguish between different respiratory and nonrespiratory viruses.
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a capability (distinguishing multiple virus types) that was not claimed in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The platform can detect virus signatures in human saliva without any additional sample preparation.
alignments.rationale: Detection in unprocessed saliva is an added experimental claim in the publication not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Thermalytix demonstrated an overall sensitivity of 95.24% and specificity of 88.58% compared to conventional mammography.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The overall sensitivity of Thermalytix was 95.24% and the specificity was 88.58%.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same sensitivity and specificity values with the same comparison, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In women with dense breasts, Thermalytix achieved a sensitivity of 100% and a specificity of 81.65%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In women with dense breasts, the sensitivity of Thermalytix was 100% and the specificity was 81.65%.
alignments.rationale: Values and subgroup (dense breasts) are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Thermalytix outperformed mammography in identifying all malignancies among women reported as BI-RADS 0.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Thermalytix outperformed mammography in women with dense breasts and those reported as BI-RADS 0.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the BI-RADS 0 finding but adds dense-breast patients, broadening the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Thermalytix identified all malignancies among premenopausal women and women younger than 50 years of age.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the age/premenopausal-specific "identified all malignancies" claim present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: A total of 21 malignancies were detected among the 459 women evaluated.
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces an overall malignancy count that was not stated as a separate claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ICD-10-CM codes cover only a fraction of known monogenic diseases, and code-based patient retrieval offers 0.12 precision.
alignments.pub_claim_text: ICD-10-CM codes cover only a fraction of monogenic diseases and offer 0.14 precision for patient retrieval.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same point but reports a slightly different precision estimate (0.14 vs 0.12).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Searching by causal gene symbol offers great recall but an even worse 0.09 precision.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Searching by causal gene symbol has a recall rate but an even worse precision of 0.07.
alignments.rationale: Same qualitative finding but a different numeric precision (0.07 vs 0.09) in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MonoMiner achieves 7-9 times higher precision (0.82), with 0.88 precision on disease diagnosis alone.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MonoMiner achieves 6 to 11 times higher precision (0.80) compared to other methods, with 0.87 precision on disease diagnosis alone.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports slightly different ranges and precision estimates but conveys the same comparative improvement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MonoMiner tags 4,259 patients with 560 monogenic diseases and 534 causal genes, at 0.48 recall.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MonoMiner tags 4259 patients with 560 monogenic diseases and 534 causal genes, achieving a recall of 0.48.
alignments.rationale: Numbers and recall are effectively identical between the preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MonoMiner enables the discovery of a large, high-precision cohort of monogenic disease patients with an established molecular diagnosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MonoMiner enables the discovery of a large, high-precision cohort of patients with monogenic diseases with an established molecular diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same concluding claim with equivalent wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Natural language processing can be employed to reveal the latent patterns or context of genomic elements in SARS-CoV-2 genomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NLP algorithms can process SARS-CoV-2 genomes and mutations to reveal the characteristics and evolution of the virus.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the core capability of NLP on viral genomes with slightly more explicit wording about characteristics and evolution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Dynamic topic modeling and temporal Word2Vec models can delineate mutation signatures corresponding to different Variants-of-Concerns.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NLP is applicable in probing the temporal mutational signatures through dynamic topic modelling.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes dynamic topic modelling for temporal signatures; the preprint additionally mentioned Word2Vec, so the published phrasing narrows methodological emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Characteristic mutations affiliated with Covid-infection severity have been identified and their relationship with Mutations-of-Concern has been tracked.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach yields promising results in identifying putative signatures linked to known/highly speculated mutations of concern.
alignments.rationale: The publication is more cautious and focuses on putative signatures linked to mutations of concern, omitting the stronger claim about association with infection severity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach presented could supplement ongoing efforts in understanding the Covid pandemic and provide alternative strategies in studying dynamic phenomena in biological sciences.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach yields promising results in identifying putative signatures linked to known/highly speculated mutations of concern.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the work as yielding promising results (more cautious) rather than the broader, more prescriptive framing in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Three MRI-driven subtypes of multiple sclerosis were identified based on how they accumulated MRI abnormality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MS subtypes can be classified as cortex-led, normal-appearing white matter-led, and lesion-led based on the earliest abnormalities.
alignments.rationale: The publication identifies the same three MRI-driven subtypes and the basis for classification, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The lesion-led subtype showed a faster disability progression and higher disease activity than the cortex-led subtype.
alignments.pub_claim_text: People with the lesion-led subtype have the highest risk of confirmed disability progression (CDP) and the highest relapse rate.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same pattern of worse progression and higher activity for the lesion-led subtype.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MRI-driven subtypes were associated with disability progression, while clinical phenotypes and baseline disability were not.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MRI-based subtypes predict MS disability progression and response to treatment.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes prediction of progression and treatment response but does not explicitly restate the preprint's negative finding about clinical phenotypes and baseline disability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only the lesion-led subtype showed a significant treatment response in multiple sclerosis clinical trials.
alignments.pub_claim_text: People with the lesion-led MS subtype show positive treatment response in selected clinical trials.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports lesion-led subjects show positive responses in selected trials but does not assert exclusivity ('only'), softening the preprint's stronger claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MRI-driven subtyping using machine learning can prospectively enrich clinical trials with patients who are most likely to respond to treatments.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MRI-based subtypes predict MS disability progression and response to treatment.
alignments.rationale: The publication supports the predictive relevance of subtypes (which underpins enrichment) but does not explicitly claim prospective trial enrichment, and its overall conclusion confidence is lower.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study prospectively and externally validated a machine learning model that predicts in-hospital mortality for all adult patients at the time of hospital admission.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model was prospectively and externally validated to predict in-hospital mortality for all adult patients at the time of hospital admission.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same validation claim with equivalent wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The in-hospital mortality rates for the validation cohorts ranged from 1.6% to 3.0%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The in-hospital mortality rates for the training and validation cohorts were 3.0%, 2.7%, 1.8%, 2.1%, and 1.6% respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the detailed per-cohort rates, expanding on the preprint's reported range.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curves (AUROCs) for the model ranged from 0.84 to 0.89 across different validation cohorts.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curves for the model ranged from 0.84 to 0.89 across different validation cohorts.
alignments.rationale: Identical AUROC range reported in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrated accurate prediction of in-hospital mortality for adult patients at the time of admission.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated good discrimination of in-hospital mortality for adult patients at the time of admission.
alignments.rationale: The publication uses 'good discrimination' rather than 'accurate prediction,' a modest wording change but the same general conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The data elements, methods, and patient selection make the model implementable at a system-level.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The data elements, methods, and patient selection make the model implementable at a system level.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same implementability claim with equivalent wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multivariate machine learning models using clinical variables performed best in predicting cognitive impairment outcomes in newly diagnosed Parkinson's disease cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models consisting of clinical variables performed best in predicting cognitive impairment outcomes compared to dementia conversion. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim retains the core finding that clinical-variable models performed best for cognitive impairment but adds a specific comparison to dementia conversion, which narrows and clarifies the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was a marginal improvement in prediction performance when clinical, biofluid, and genetic/epigenetic variables were combined in one model. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Including clinical, biofluid, and genetic/epigenetic variables in one model resulted in a marginal improvement in prediction performance. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with consistent wording and certainty, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Several cerebrospinal fluid measures and an epigenetic marker demonstrated high predictive weighting in multiple models when included with clinical variables. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Several cerebrospinal fluid measures and an epigenetic marker showed high predictive weighting in multiple models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same finding about CSF measures and an epigenetic marker having high predictive weight, with equivalent certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The multivariate machine learning model accurately predicts cognitive outcomes in newly diagnosed Parkinson's disease cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication-level statement about overall model accuracy was not present in the preprint and appears as an added, more general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All tested bots passed the multiple-choice medical licensing exam, with Bing and GPT-4 achieving 76% correct answers, outperforming the other bots and students. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All bots passed the multiple-choice medical licensing exam, with Bing and GPT-4 achieving 76% correct answers, outperforming the other bots and students. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the identical performance result with the same specifics and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Bots performed worse on difficult questions, scoring 62%, but still outperformed students who scored 32% on those questions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Bots performed worse on difficult questions, scoring 62%, but still outperformed students who scored 32% on those questions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same comparative performance on difficult questions with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hallucinations were found in 7% of Bing’s and GPT-4’s answers, which is significantly lower than Bard (22%) and Claude Instant (19%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Hallucinations were found in 7% of Bing's and GPT-4's answers, which is significantly lower than Bard's 22% and Claude Instant's 19%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The hallucination rates and comparative statement are carried forward intact into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: None of the tested bots succeeded in refusing to answer all clinical case questions, indicating they are not suitable for use as medical doctors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: None of the tested bots succeeded in refusing to answer all clinical case questions, indicating a limitation in their design. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens the strong preprint conclusion (unsuitability as doctors) to a more circumspect description of a design limitation, reducing the asserted implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Microsoft Bing was able to detect weak or ambiguous exam questions, suggesting it could improve the quality of a multiple-choice exam. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Bots could be used as a time-efficient tool to improve the quality of a multiple-choice exam. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes the preprint's Bing-specific observation to a broader claim about bots in general being useful for exam improvement, thereby broadening the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Temporal dataset shift significantly impacts clinical prediction models, particularly evident in sepsis prediction with a maximum AUROC drop of 0.090. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Temporal dataset shift significantly impacts clinical prediction models, particularly evident in sepsis prediction with a maximum AUROC drop of 0.090. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same quantitative finding about temporal shift and AUROC drop with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Domain generalization (DG) and unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) algorithms did not produce more robust models compared to empirical risk minimization (ERM) in the context of temporal dataset shift. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Domain generalization (DG) and unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) algorithms did not produce more robust models compared to ERM in the context of temporal dataset shift. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the negative result regarding DG/UDA relative to ERM; wording and implication remain consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of ERM[08-10] to 2017-2019 resulted in one additional false-negative among 11 patients with sepsis compared to the model applied to 2008-2010. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using empirical risk minimization (ERM) on data from 2008–2010 resulted in one additional false-negative among 11 patients with sepsis when applied to 2017–2019. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same specific patient-level impact observation; the content is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings indicate that purely data-driven techniques like DG and UDA struggle to improve robustness to dataset shift without leveraging prior knowledge of the nature of the shift. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Alternate approaches are required to preserve model performance over time in clinical medicine due to the limitations of DG and UDA. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication draws a clearer, policy-oriented implication (need for alternate approaches) from the preprint's finding that data-driven DG/UDA methods struggle, thus broadening the practical implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model achieved an accuracy of 0.85, sensitivity of 0.96, and AUC of 0.86 in per-patient level stenosis classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model achieved an accuracy of 0.85, sensitivity of 0.96, and AUC of 0.86 in per-patient level stenosis classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact performance metrics and result without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Redundancy training was effective to improve classification performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Redundancy training was effective to improve classification performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The effectiveness of redundancy training is reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Stenosis position localization was adequate with better quantitative results in the anchor-based FPN model. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Stenosis position localization was adequate with better quantitative results in the anchor-based FPN model, achieving global-sensitivity for left coronary artery (LCA) and right coronary artery (RCA) of 0.68 and 0.70. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication supplies specific sensitivity values, clarifying and slightly strengthening the original localization performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A fully-automatic end-to-end deep learning-based workflow that eliminates the vessel extraction and segmentation step was feasible in coronary artery stenosis classification and localization on CAG images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrated a fully automatic end-to-end deep learning-based workflow that eliminates the vessel extraction and segmentation step in coronary artery stenosis classification and localization on CAG images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication moves from 'feasible' to 'demonstrated,' making the claim more definitive and increasing confidence in the result.

alignments.pub_claim_text: This tool may be useful to facilitate safety screening in high-volume centers and in clinical trial settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a new implication about potential clinical utility in specific settings that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Statistically significant differences were found for normalized abundances of 374 of the 597 peptides and glycopeptides interrogated, between symptomatic and asymptomatic COVID-19 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found statistically significant differences for normalized abundances of 374 of the 597 peptides and glycopeptides interrogated between symptomatic and asymptomatic COVID-19 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the specific peptide/glycopeptide differential-abundance result verbatim in substance and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Specific glycoprotein profiles were found to be strongly predictive of symptomatic COVID-19 infection, achieving accuracies of 100% in an independent test set and 96% overall. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Specific glycoprotein profiles were found to be strongly predictive of symptomatic COVID-19 infection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the predictive-profile claim but omits the specific high accuracy numbers, which constitutes a revision that tempers the strength of the numeric result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A majority of glycoprotein modifications observed in symptomatic COVID-19 and sepsis patients likely represent a generic consequence of a severe systemic immune and inflammatory state. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings support the potential value of glycoproteomic biomarkers in the biomedical understanding and clinical management of serious acute infectious conditions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts the emphasis from an etiological interpretation (generic consequence) to broader translational value (biomarker utility), representing a substantive reframing and broadening of implications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are glyco-isoform changes that are specific to severe COVID-19 infection, which may represent either COVID-19-specific consequences or susceptibility to a severe course of the disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are glycoisoform changes that are specific and particular to severe COVID-19 infection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the existence of COVID-19-specific glycoisoform changes but removes the speculative alternative interpretation about susceptibility, narrowing and clarifying the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No COVID-19 prediction model in the existing literature has been found to be reliable.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state that no COVID-19 model is reliable and instead focuses on providing a pipeline; the original negative claim about all existing models is absent from the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The OHDSI analytics pipeline enables the rapid development of reliable prediction models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The OHDSI analytics pipeline enables the rapid development of reliable prediction models for COVID-19 outcomes.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the pipeline's ability to produce reliable models but explicitly limits the claim to COVID-19 outcomes, narrowing the original general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: L1-regularized logistic regression models were well calibrated compared to other machine learning methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: L1-regularized logistic regression models were well calibrated when predicting death in patients hospitalized with COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the calibration claim but specifies the target (death in hospitalized COVID-19 patients) and reports it with higher certainty, increasing confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The open-source tools developed allow for efficient end-to-end model development and evaluation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The OHDSI software tools and pipeline are open source and available to researchers worldwide.
alignments.rationale: Both claims describe the open-source OHDSI tools; the publication emphasizes availability worldwide rather than the explicit "efficient end-to-end" phrasing in the preprint, shifting emphasis but preserving the contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The MLP neural network consistently resulted in lower discrimination performance compared to other methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the MLP-specific finding; instead it compares tree-based methods to L1-regularized logistic regression, so the MLP claim is absent.

alignments.pub_claim_text: AdaBoost, random forest, gradient boosting machine, and decision tree yielded similar or lower internal and external validation discrimination performance compared to L1-regularized logistic regression.
alignments.rationale: This publication-level comparison of several tree-based methods to L1-regularized logistic regression does not have a clear counterpart in the preprint (the preprint mentioned MLP instead), so it is treated as an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The transformation method developed in this study allows for the conversion of standard clinical images to ultra-low-field (ULF) quality images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates a method for virtually evaluating novel imaging devices using machine learning and open-access datasets.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the technical transformation as a broader virtual evaluation approach for devices using ML and datasets, shifting the emphasis from pure image conversion to device evaluation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pathology detection performance was similar between 3T and simulated 64mT images across all cohorts.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pathology detection was similar between 3 T and simulated 64mT images across all cohorts.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this core result verbatim and with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Simulated images showed decreasing sensitivity for lesions smaller than 4 cm².
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simulated images showed decreasing sensitivity for lesions smaller than 4 cm².
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this quantitative performance finding exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Simulated data derived from open-source imaging databases may facilitate testing and validation of new imaging devices.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simulated data derived from open-source imaging databases may facilitate testing and validation of new imaging devices.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this conclusion about the utility of simulated open-source data for device testing and validation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ultra-low-field, point-of-care MRI has potential to detect a range of pathologies including brain tumors, strokes, and multiple sclerosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make a direct claim about the clinical detection potential across specific pathologies; instead it focuses on simulation/evaluation methodology and limitations, so the specific clinical-potential claim is absent.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Simulations cannot replace prospective trials during the evaluation of medical devices, but they can provide guidance and justification for prospective studies.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly adds a limitation about simulations versus prospective trials that was not stated as a claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The points-based model had good discrimination with a c-statistic of 0.66.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The points-based model had good discrimination with a c-statistic of 0.66.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this numeric discrimination result identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was a 31% relative reduction in risk of all-cause mortality per decile of increasing C-Score.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There was a 31% relative reduction in risk of all-cause mortality per decile of increasing C-Score.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this risk reduction result exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A Cox model integrating age and C-Score had improved discrimination with a c-statistic of 0.74.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A Cox model integrating age and C-Score had improved discrimination with a c-statistic of 0.74.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the Cox model discrimination finding verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning approaches did not offer improved discrimination over statistical modelling.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include an explicit statement comparing machine learning to statistical modelling in this negative form, so this preprint claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel health metric ('C-Score') has good predictive capabilities for all-cause mortality within 10 years.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel health metric ('C-Score') has good predictive capabilities for all-cause mortality within 10 years.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this concluding claim about the C-Score's predictive capability.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Embedding the C-Score within a smartphone app may represent a useful tool for democratized, individualized health risk prediction.
alignments.rationale: The smartphone-app application of the C-Score is a pragmatic extension introduced in the publication that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models based on exposome data can reliably identify individuals that will be diagnosed with dementia.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models utilizing exposome data can reliably identify individuals at risk of dementia.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core conclusion that exposome-based ML models can identify individuals at risk/likely to be diagnosed with dementia.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost based model outperforms the logistic regression model, achieving a mean AUC of 0.88 in the external validation tests.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The XGBoost model outperformed the logistic regression model, achieving a mean AUC of 0.88 in external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this model comparison and the AUC result identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Novel exposome factors such as facial aging, the frequency of use of sun/ultraviolet light protection, and the length of mobile phone use may serve as potential markers for dementia.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Novel exposome factors such as facial aging, the frequency of use of sun/ultraviolet light protection, and the length of mobile phone use may serve as potential markers for dementia.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains these proposed exposome marker candidates with similar framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A novel neurocognitive assessment test is proposed as an online tool to screen individuals at risk of dementia for enrolment in preventive interventions and future clinical trials.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not mention proposing a neurocognitive online screening test; this specific contribution is absent.

alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach highlights the potential of low-cost, readily available exposome factors as markers for dementia.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a broader conclusion about the potential of low-cost exposome factors as markers, which is an extension of the preprint's contributions and not explicitly stated there.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pramipexole specifically increased choice accuracy in the reward condition, with no effect in the loss condition.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pramipexole increased choice accuracy in the reward condition with no effect on losses.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this behavioral result with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pramipexole increased the BOLD response in the orbital frontal cortex during the expectation of win trials but decreased the BOLD response to reward prediction errors in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants who received pramipexole had increased blood oxygen level-dependent response in the orbital frontal cortex during the expectation of win trials.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the increased OFC BOLD response but omits the reported decrease in vmPFC RPE response from the preprint, indicating a revision/reduction of the neuroimaging claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pramipexole enhances reward learning by preserving learned values.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pramipexole enhances reward learning by preserving learned values.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this mechanistic conclusion identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The observed effects of pramipexole may provide a plausible candidate mechanism for its anti-depressant effect.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make an explicit claim connecting the observed effects to pramipexole's antidepressant mechanism; instead it gives a more specific computational explanation of value decay reduction, so the broader antidepressant-mechanism claim is absent.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The pattern of results indicates that pramipexole enhances choice accuracy by reducing the decay of estimated values during reward learning.
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a more specific mechanistic interpretation (reduced decay of estimated values) that was not explicitly stated in the preprint, so it is treated as an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A total of 56 out of 163 STM academic publishers had a publicly available policy guiding the authors’ use of AI chatbots.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only 34.6% of STM academic publishers had a publicly available policy guiding the authors' use of AI chatbots.
alignments.rationale: The numeric values are equivalent (56/163 ≈ 34.4%), so the publication restates the same result in percent form.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No policy allowed authorship accreditations for AI chatbots or other generative technology.
alignments.pub_claim_text: No policy allowed authorship for AI chatbots or other AI tools.
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert the same negative finding about authorship credit for AI tools.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most (49 out of 56 or 87.5%) of the publishers required specific disclosure of AI chatbot use.
alignments.pub_claim_text: 87.5% of the publishers with policies required specific disclosure of AI chatbot use.
alignments.rationale: Identical statistic and meaning about disclosure requirements.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Four policies/publishers placed a complete ban on the use of AI tools by authors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Four policies placed a complete ban on the use of AI chatbots by authors.
alignments.rationale: Same count and conclusion about complete bans on AI chatbot use by authors.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only a third of STM academic publishers had publicly available policies as of December 2023.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only 34.6% of STM academic publishers had a publicly available policy guiding the authors' use of AI chatbots.
alignments.rationale: This conclusion reiterates the same statistic expressed in Q0; publication frames it as the measured percentage rather than the dated phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning-based system HCC-SurvNet achieved a concordance index of 0.724 on an internal test set and 0.683 on an external test set.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning-based system HCC-SurvNet achieved concordance indices of 0.724 and 0.683 on the internal and external test cohorts, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same concordance indices for the same cohorts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HCC-SurvNet outperformed the standard AJCC/UICC TNM classification system in predicting tumor recurrence.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state or present a claim that the model outperformed the TNM system, so the specific claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The risk score predicted by HCC-SurvNet was an independent risk factor for post-surgical recurrence in both internal and external test sets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model was an independent risk factor for post-surgical recurrence in both test cohorts.
alignments.rationale: Publication restates the same finding that the model's score was an independent risk factor in both cohorts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Statistically significant differences in survival distributions were observed between low- and high-risk subgroups as stratified by the risk scores from HCC-SurvNet.
alignments.pub_claim_text: HCC-SurvNet's risk score stratified patients into low- and high-risk subgroups with statistically significant differences in their survival distributions.
alignments.rationale: Both claims describe the same stratification result with significant survival differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning-based models like HCC-SurvNet may augment current patient stratification methods and refine clinical management for HCC patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning-based models can provide recurrence risk scores which may augment current patient stratification methods.
alignments.rationale: Publication restates the same conclusion more narrowly (focus on recurrence risk scores) and with slightly different wording, clarifying the mechanism of augmentation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model for laser treatment detection achieved an AUC of 0.981 at the patient level.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model for laser treatment detection achieved AUCs of 0.981, 0.95, and 0.979 at the patient, image, and eye levels, respectively.
alignments.rationale: Publication includes the same patient-level AUC and additionally reports image- and eye-level results, broadening the reported evaluation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrated an AUC of 0.950 at the image level for laser photocoagulation detection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model for laser treatment detection achieved AUCs of 0.981, 0.95, and 0.979 at the patient, image, and eye levels, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same image-level AUC (0.95) as reported in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model positively affects the efficacy of different AI models in analyzing retinal images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model for laser treatment detection has been demonstrated to positively affect the efficacy of different AI models.
alignments.rationale: Both statements make the same claim that the laser-detection model improves downstream model efficacy; the publication explicitly confirms this.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DME detection on images without artifacts achieved a higher AUC of 0.955 compared to 0.932 on images with artifacts.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Diabetic macular oedema detection showed an AUC of 0.932 on images with artefacts compared to 0.955 on those without.
alignments.rationale: Identical comparison and numeric AUC values are reported in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participant age detection on images without artifacts had a lower MAE of 3.81 compared to 5.33 on images with artifacts.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the age-detection MAE comparison present in the preprint, indicating the claim was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trained model correctly classified 123 of 128 examinations as positive for pulmonary emboli with a sensitivity of 96.1%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained model correctly classified 123 of 128 examinations as positive for pulmonary emboli, achieving a sensitivity of 96.1%.
alignments.rationale: Exact same case count and sensitivity are reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In the first external test dataset, the trained model achieved a sensitivity of 96.9% and a specificity of 100%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the first external test dataset, the model achieved a sensitivity of 96.9% and an AUROC of 98.6%.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the sensitivity but replaces the reported specificity with an AUROC metric, changing the performance characterization and removing the 100% specificity claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed algorithm demonstrated outstanding performance on both internal and two publicly available external testing datasets with an AUC of 98.3%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUROC) of 96.4% in internal validation.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's single aggregated AUC (98.3%) across datasets is not supported; the publication reports a lower internal AUROC (96.4%) and different external AUROCs, indicating the original uniform AUC claim was revised and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automatic pipeline achieved beyond state-of-the-art diagnostic performance of pulmonary emboli in CTPA.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The automatic pipeline achieved beyond state-of-the-art diagnostic performance for pulmonary emboli in CTPA.
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert the same conclusion about achieving beyond state-of-the-art diagnostic performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Following tDCS treatment, 11 participants attained clinical remission.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Following tDCS treatment, 11 participants attained clinical remission.
alignments.rationale: Identical result reported in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A significant positive correlation was observed between improvements in depressive symptoms and delta band PLV in frontal and temporoparietal regions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A significant positive correlation was observed between improvements in depressive symptoms and delta band PLV in frontal and temporoparietal regional channel pairs.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same correlation with slightly more specific channel-pair wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: tDCS treatment enhances network synchronisation, potentially increasing inhibitory control, which underscores improvement in depressive symptoms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: tDCS treatment enhances network synchronization, potentially increasing inhibitory control, which underscores improvement in depressive symptoms.
alignments.rationale: Same mechanistic conclusion is presented in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Baseline EEG-based measures might aid in predicting clinical response to tDCS treatment.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Baseline EEG-based measures might aid in predicting clinical response.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same tentative conclusion about baseline EEG measures predicting response.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The highest deep learning prediction accuracy of 69.45% was obtained from PLV features combined from theta, beta, and gamma bands.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest deep learning prediction accuracy of 69.45% was obtained from PLV features combined from theta, beta, and gamma bands.
alignments.rationale: Identical reported accuracy and feature combination in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Parkinson's disease patients on medication exhibit a small-to-medium impairment in reward processing compared to healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients with Parkinson's disease on medication exhibit a small-to-medium impairment in reward processing compared to healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim wording and meaning are effectively identical to the preprint claim, retaining magnitude, comparator, and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Greater impairments in reward processing are observed in Parkinson's disease patients off dopaminergic medication. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Greater impairments in reward processing are observed in Parkinson's disease patients off dopaminergic medication. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result without substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Impaired processing off medication is specifically noted in the Option Valuation and Reward Response Vigour tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Impaired processing off medication is specifically noted in option valuation and reward response vigour tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The task-specific impairment is preserved verbatim between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Reinforcement Learning is impaired in Parkinson's disease patients on medication but not off medication. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Reinforcement learning in Parkinson's disease patients is impaired on medication but not off medication compared to healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the explicit comparator (“compared to healthy controls”), clarifying the context but preserving the core finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No significant impairment in reward processing was identified in impulse control disorder patients compared to non-ICD patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: No significant impairment in reward processing was identified in patients with impulse control disorders compared to non-ICD patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim matches the preprint claim in meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Radiomic features are sensitive to the variability of imaging parameters, such as scanner model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Radiomic features are sensitive to the variability of imaging parameters, such as scanner models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same sensitivity of radiomic features to scanner variability with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is strong scanner dependency in the classification of HPV status from radiological images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is strong scanner dependency in the classification of HPV status from radiological images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The scanner dependency finding is preserved verbatim between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effect of scanner manufacturers impacts the robustness of radiomic features and the performance of HPV prediction models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of HPV prediction models is affected by the scanner manufacturer. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the claim to model performance affected by manufacturer and removes the explicit statement about feature robustness, so content has been revised and scope narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An appropriate approach is necessary to reduce the impact of imaging parameters on radiomic features and machine learning models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An appropriate approach is necessary to reduce the impact of imaging parameters on radiomic features and machine learning models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation/conclusion is repeated unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning-based clinical decision support tool identified 82% of sepsis cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The early detection tool achieved high sensitivity, identifying 82% of sepsis cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements convey the same sensitivity result (82%) with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tool had an adoption rate of 89% for alerts evaluated by a physician or advanced practice provider. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 89% of all alerts generated by the system were evaluated by a physician or advanced practice provider. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same adoption/evaluation proportion with the same meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was a 1.85 hour reduction in median time to first antibiotics order associated with the use of the tool. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients with sepsis whose alert was confirmed by a provider within 3 hours had a 1.85-hour reduction in median time to first antibiotic order compared to those whose alerts were dismissed or confirmed later. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the subgroup (alerts confirmed within 3 hours) and comparator, clarifying the context and effectively narrowing the original, broader association.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Provider-related factors were strongly associated with the adoption of the sepsis early detection tool. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Emergency department providers and those with previous interactions with alerts were more likely to interact with and confirm alerts. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication replaces the general statement with specific provider-related factors (ED providers, prior interactions), clarifying which factors were associated with adoption.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Efforts to improve adoption should focus on provider knowledge, experience, and perceptions of the system. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Efforts to improve adoption of early warning systems are essential to their clinical impact. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the specific recommendation into a broader statement about the importance of improving adoption for clinical impact, altering the focus and broadening scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed workflow using Raman spectroscopy can discriminate between COVID-positive and negative individuals with an AUC of 0.8. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The innovative multiple instance learning-based ML approach successfully discriminated between COVID-positive and COVID-negative individuals with an AUC of 0.8. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication attributes the result specifically to a multiple-instance-learning ML approach, clarifying the method while preserving the reported performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity of the method are 79% and 75% for males, and 84% and 64% for females, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not restate the sex-specific sensitivity/specificity numbers; those detailed metrics appear omitted from the high-level publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Considering the sex of the saliva donor increased the AUC by 5%. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Considering the sex of the saliva donor increased the AUC by 5% in the discrimination of COVID-19 status. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same effect (5% AUC increase) when sex is considered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings may pave the way for new rapid Raman spectroscopic screening tools for COVID-19 and other infectious diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings may lead to the development of new rapid Raman spectroscopic screening tools for COVID-19 and other infectious diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same prospective conclusion about potential development of screening tools.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CBioProfiler is a web server and standalone application designed for analyzing cancer biomarkers and subtypes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CBioProfiler is a web server and standalone application designed for analyzing cancer biomarkers and subtypes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same description of CBioProfiler with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The cancer biomarker pipeline in CBioProfiler includes five modules for identifying and annotating cancer survival-related biomarkers using multiple machine learning survival algorithms. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The cancer biomarker pipeline in CBioProfiler includes five modules that utilize multiple survival-related machine learning algorithms to identify and annotate cancer survival-related biomarkers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same content about the five-module biomarker pipeline and its use of multiple ML survival algorithms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CBioProfiler's subtype pipeline consists of three modules for data preprocessing, subtype identification using multiple unsupervised machine learning methods, and subtype evaluation and validation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CBioProfiler's cancer subtype pipeline consists of three modules for data preprocessing, subtype identification, and subtype evaluation and validation using multiple unsupervised machine learning methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same three-module description and methods for subtype analysis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The R package CuratedCancerPrognosisData within CBioProfiler integrates gene expression data and clinical data from 268 gene expression studies of 43 common blood and solid tumors. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CuratedCancerPrognosisData is a novel R package included in CBioProfiler that integrates reviewed and curated gene expression and clinical data from 268 studies covering 43 common blood and solid tumors. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication slightly rephrases the package description, adding that data are reviewed/curated and calling the package novel, which clarifies but does not change scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All methods achieve clinically-effective performances (NPV >0.959) in the case study of COVID-19 diagnosis across four NHS Hospital Trusts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All methods achieve clinically-effective performances (NPV > 0.959) in the case study of COVID-19 diagnosis across four NHS Hospital Trusts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication statements are textually and substantively the same, with identical metrics and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transfer learning achieves the best results with mean AUROCs between 0.870-0.925. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transfer learning achieves the best results with mean AUROCs between 0.870 and 0.925. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is equivalent in both versions, only minor formatting of the AUROC range differs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Site-specific customization improves predictive performance when compared to other ready-made approaches. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Site-specific customization improves predictive performance when compared to other ready-made approaches. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is unchanged between preprint and publication with matching wording and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Unsupervised clustering revealed cancer-type-specific sub-grouping. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Unsupervised clustering revealed cancer type-specific sub-grouping. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The clustering result is stated identically in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classification using supervised machine learning model yielded an overall accuracy of 81.62% in discriminating malignant from control samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classification using a supervised machine learning model yielded accuracies of 96% and 65% in discriminating hematological and solid malignancies from healthy controls, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication splits the single overall accuracy into separate accuracies for hematological and solid malignancies, changing the reported performance breakdown and narrowing the claim to subtype-specific results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy of disease type prediction was 85% and 70% for the hematological and solid cancers, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of disease type prediction was 85% and 70% for the hematological and solid cancers, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This numeric disease-type prediction claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrate the clinical utility of our approach by classifying benign from invasive and borderline adnexal masses with an AUC of 0.8656 and 0.7388, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The potential utility of managing a specific cancer was demonstrated by classifying benign from invasive and borderline adnexal masses with an area under the curve of 0.87 and 0.74, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same results with rounded AUC values and slightly different framing ('potential utility'), which clarifies presentation without changing substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This approach provides a generic and cost-effective strategy for non-invasive pan-cancer detection. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach provides a generic analytical strategy for non-invasive pan-cancer detection and cancer type prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes wording—dropping 'cost-effective' and adding explicit mention of 'cancer type prediction'—which broadens the stated applications while tempering the economic claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prediction pipeline using live electronic health records generated short-term, probabilistic forecasts of emergency admissions with AUROCs ranging from 0.82 to 0.90. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prediction pipeline using live electronic health records generated short-term, probabilistic forecasts of emergency admissions with AUROCs ranging from 0.82 to 0.90. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The AUROC range and the nature of the forecasts are stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pipeline achieved a mean absolute error of 4.0 admissions compared to a benchmark metric of 6.5 admissions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pipeline achieved a mean absolute error of 4.0 admissions, which is an improvement compared to the benchmark metric's mean absolute error of 6.5. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same MAE and explicitly notes it as an improvement, but the numeric claim and comparison remain the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models developed with data from the Covid-19 pandemic yielded AUROCs of 0.68-0.90 and a mean absolute error of 4.2 admissions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models developed with data from the Covid-19 pandemic yielded AUROCs of 0.68–0.90 and a mean absolute error of 4.2, outperforming the benchmark of 4.9. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a comparison to a specific benchmark (4.9) and presents the claim with higher certainty, clarifying and strengthening the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study discusses the challenges faced in designing and implementing models for real-time use in hospital operations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study discusses the challenges faced in designing and implementing models for real-time use in hospital operations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The discussion of implementation challenges is present and unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Infrared (IR) based histopathology provides a complementary information source for classical histopathology. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Infrared (IR) based histopathology provides a complementary information source for classical histopathology. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A pancreatic cancer classification model was developed using data from over 600 biopsies with AUC values above 0.95. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A robust, pixel level machine learning model for pancreatic cancer detection was built using IR imaging data from over 600 biopsies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms the model and data source but omits the explicit AUC >0.95 statement in that specific claim, which tempers the performance assertion while keeping the core result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study analyzed one of the largest IR datasets to date, comprising almost 700 million spectra of different tissue types. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study analyzed one of the largest IR datasets to date, consisting of almost 700 million spectra of different tissue types. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The dataset-size contribution is stated equivalently in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A comprehensive histopathology digital staining model for pancreatic tissues was successfully developed based on biochemical information from IR spectra. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The first six-class model for comprehensive histopathology achieved pixel level AUC values above 0.95. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the comprehensive model as a first six-class model and provides pixel-level AUCs >0.95, giving a more specific description of the achievement consistent with the preprint's claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study presents mass testing strategies developed through machine learning models that predict the risk of COVID-19 contagion based on health determinants. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Four mass testing strategies were evaluated, including antigen testing without low-risk attention, antigen testing with pooling, antigen testing with multiple testing, and a hybrid strategy with targeted testing according to risk. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication describes the evaluated strategies and includes a hybrid targeted-by-risk strategy, which clarifies how ML-derived risk stratification was operationalized without changing the contribution's essence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classifier achieved an AUC-ROC of 0.80 and an AUC-PR of 0.59, indicating its effectiveness in classifying individuals by contagion risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classifier achieved an area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC) of 80.48 % and an area under the Precision-Recall curve (AUC-PR) of 58.98 %. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same classifier performance with slightly more precise percentages; the substantive performance claim is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The fourth strategy, which integrates RT-qPCR, antigen, and RT-LAMP tests, is identified as a cost-effective approach for mass testing. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model to triage the population combined with a targeted mass testing strategy is a cost-effective option. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a more general cost-effectiveness conclusion (ML triage + targeted testing) rather than naming the specific fourth-test-combination, broadening the claim's framing while keeping the cost-effectiveness conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study introduces the metrics of efficiency and stock capacity to assess the performance of different mass testing strategies. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study introduced the metrics efficiency and stock capacity to assess the performance of different testing strategies. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The metrics introduction is stated equivalently in both the preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proprietary large language model (LLM) achieved a sensitivity of 97% in classifying whether adolescent progress notes contained confidential content. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The language learning model can classify whether a progress note contains confidential information. (certainty=high, type=result)
