alignments.rationale: Both claims state the model can detect whether a note contains confidential content; the publication omits the numeric sensitivity, presenting a more general formulation of the same result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LLM demonstrated a specificity of 18% and a positive predictive value of 34% in identifying confidential content. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains no matching numeric performance claim for specificity/PPV; the specific low performance metrics from the preprint do not appear in the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only 13% of the manually reviewed excerpts were accurately derived from the original note, indicating a significant issue with hallucinations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The language learning model can identify the specific confidential content in a progress note. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint reports high hallucination rates undermining content identification, while the publication asserts the model can identify specific confidential content; this is a substantive revision in the claimed capability and confidence in identification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The low specificity and poor positive predictive value of the LLM limit its usefulness in identifying confidential content. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation about poor specificity/PPV limiting usefulness is not reflected in the publication claims, which instead emphasize classification capability; thus the limitation appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In its current form, GPT-3.5 cannot reliably identify confidential content in free-text adolescent progress notes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The language learning model can classify whether a progress note contains confidential information. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint concludes the model is not reliable, whereas the publication asserts the model can perform classification; this reverses the interpretation of reliability and thus is a revision of the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Active surveillance is a recommended risk management strategy for localized prostate cancer to avoid unnecessary treatments. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state the clinical recommendation about active surveillance; this conclusion from the preprint is not present in the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning model was developed to classify prostate adenocarcinoma into indolent and aggressive categories based on core needle biopsy whole slide images. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model achieved ROC-AUCs of 0.846 for indolent prostate adenocarcinoma and 0.980 for aggressive prostate adenocarcinoma. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents concrete performance metrics for the same developed model; this clarifies and augments the development claim with quantitative results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning models achieved ROC-AUCs of 0.846 for indolent and 0.980 for aggressive prostate adenocarcinoma. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model achieved ROC-AUCs of 0.846 for indolent prostate adenocarcinoma and 0.980 for aggressive prostate adenocarcinoma. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the identical ROC-AUC results reported in the preprint, with no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results indicate the promising potential of the deep learning model for integration into prostate adenocarcinoma diagnostic workflows. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results demonstrate the promising potential of the deep learning model for deployment in a practical prostate adenocarcinoma histopathological diagnostic workflow system. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both claims express the model's potential for clinical workflow use; the publication strengthens and specifies this claim toward practical deployment, increasing confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Ensembling Attention-based Multi-scaled Convolution network (EAMC) exhibits high sensitivity and precision in outlining infected regions and assessing severity of COVID-19 infection in lungs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Ensembling of selective Focus-based Multi-resolution Convolution network (E F M C) exhibits high sensitivity and precision in outlining infected regions and assessing severity of COVID-19 in lungs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same high sensitivity/precision result for what appears to be the same ensemble multi-resolution convolution approach, with slightly different method naming/terminology; core result unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Attention module in EAMC combines contextual with local information at multiple scales for accurate segmentation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The selective focus mechanism combines contextual with local information at multiple resolutions for accurate segmentation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both describe a module that fuses contextual and local information across scales/resolutions for segmentation; the publication rephrases the mechanism without changing the substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The superiority of EAMC is established through comparison with existing state-of-the-art learning models over four publicly-available COVID-19 datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The superiority of E F M C, even with severe class imbalance, is established through comparison with existing state-of-the-art learning models over four publicly-available COVID-19 datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims state superiority over SOTA across the same four datasets; the publication adds the robustness note about class imbalance but does not change the core comparative result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results suggest the relevance of deep learning in providing assistive intelligence to medical practitioners during pandemics. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results are suggestive of the relevance of deep learning in providing assistive intelligence to medical practitioners during pandemics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same high-level conclusion but with increased certainty (low -> medium), so the claim is clarified and confidence amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EAMC provides low-cost decision-making for patients lacking specialized healthcare at remote locations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make the specific claim about providing low-cost decision-making or remote-care applicability; that application-oriented claim was dropped.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning and knowledge engineering applied to EHR data can effectively identify patients who should receive a diagnostic workup for acute hepatic porphyria (AHP). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained model demonstrated the ability to rank patients by margin distance, leading to the identification of patients who may require further diagnostic workup for AHP. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the core result in more technical terms (ranking by margin distance) that supports identification of patients for further AHP workup; the substantive claim is consistent and clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified four patients for whom AHP diagnostic testing was possibly indicated and had not been previously performed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified four patients for whom AHP diagnostic testing was possibly indicated and had not been previously performed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this specific result verbatim; there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Based on the reported prevalence of AHP, only 0.002 cases out of the 200 patients manually reviewed would have been expected. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific prevalence-based expectation statistic from the preprint is not present in the publication claims and appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The methodology used in this study may facilitate the diagnosis of other rare diseases beyond AHP. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of machine learning and knowledge engineering to EHR data may facilitate the diagnosis of rare diseases such as acute hepatic porphyria (AHP). (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both claims suggest the method's potential to facilitate diagnosis of rare diseases; the publication frames it as a broader possibility but retains comparable certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The artificial intelligence model built for real-time risk assessment of primary immunodeficiency demonstrates high accuracy in classifying immunodeficiency patients from controls with an AUROC of 0.945. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Bayesian network model (PI Prob) was accurate in classifying immunodeficiency patients from controls with an AUROC of 0.945. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same AUROC for the same classification task; the result is unchanged aside from specifying the model type.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model categorizes validation cohort members into appropriate International Union of Immunological Societies diagnostic categories with 89% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved 89% accuracy for categorizing validation cohort members into appropriate International Union of Immunological Societies diagnostic categories. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same accuracy result for diagnostic categorization with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial intelligence methods can effectively guide management and decision making regarding the diagnostic evaluation for patients with recurrent infections. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Artificial intelligence methods can classify risk for primary immunodeficiency and guide management. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same high-level conclusion but frames it specifically as risk classification for primary immunodeficiency that can inform management, a minor clarification of scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A total of 227 species, 1047 microbial genes and 21877 microbial SNVs were identified that differed between CD and controls.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A total of 227 species, 1047 microbial genes, and 21,877 microbial SNVs were identified that differed between CD and controls.
alignments.rationale: The numbers and wording are effectively identical between preprint and publication, indicating the result was carried over without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The species-, gene- and SNV- models achieved an average AUC of 0.97, 0.95 and 0.77, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The species, gene, and SNV models achieved an average AUC of 0.97, 0.95, and 0.77, respectively.
alignments.rationale: AUC values and phrasing match exactly, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gene model exhibited superior diagnostic capability, achieving average AUCs of 0.89 and 0.91 in internal and external validations, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The gene model exhibited superior diagnostic capability, achieving an average AUC of 0.89 and 0.91 for internal and external validations, respectively.
alignments.rationale: Validation AUCs and conclusion are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gene model was specific for CD against other microbiome-related diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include an assertion about the gene model's specificity against other microbiome-related diseases; this claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The outstanding performance of PTS was mainly explained by genes celB and manY, which demonstrated high predictabilities for CD with the metagenomic datasets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The outstanding performance of PTS was mainly explained by genes celB and manY, which demonstrated high predictabilities for CD with metagenomic datasets.
alignments.rationale: The explanatory attribution to celB and manY appears verbatim in the publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT using the GPT-4 model offered high diagnostic and triage accuracy that was comparable to the physician respondents.
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT using the GPT-4 model offered high diagnostic and triage accuracy that was comparable with that of ophthalmology trainees.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the central claim about ChatGPT's comparable performance but specifies the comparator as ophthalmology trainees rather than the broader term 'physician respondents', narrowing the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Bing Chat had lower accuracy, some instances of grossly inaccurate statements, and a tendency to overestimate triage urgency.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Bing Chat had lower accuracy and a tendency to overestimate triage urgency.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the lower accuracy and overtriage points but omits mention of 'grossly inaccurate statements', so the claim is revised (slightly attenuated).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The physician respondents, ChatGPT, Bing Chat, and WebMD listed the appropriate diagnosis among the top three suggestions in 95%, 93%, 77%, and 33% of cases, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proportion of responses with the correct diagnosis listed in the top 3 possible diagnoses was 95% for ophthalmology trainees and 93% for ChatGPT.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the 95% and 93% figures (with the comparator specified as ophthalmology trainees) but omits the Bing and WebMD percentages, narrowing the reported comparisons.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Triage urgency was appropriate in 86%, 98%, and 84% of cases for the physicians, ChatGPT, and Bing Chat, correspondingly.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Triage urgency was appropriate in 98% of cases for ChatGPT.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the ChatGPT triage accuracy number but omits the physician and Bing figures, so the multi-group claim was narrowed to the ChatGPT result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Genetic information alone is insufficient for accurate T2D prediction, achieving an AUC of 0.73.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim reports the genetic-only AUC or explicitly states that genetic information alone is insufficient; this specific result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Medical imaging data significantly improves prediction accuracy for T2D, resulting in an AUC of 0.89.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not present the standalone imaging-only AUC of 0.89, so this specific claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best-performing model, which integrates genetic, medical imaging, and demographic variables, achieves an AUC of 0.94.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our models achieve an Area Under the Receiver Operating Curve (AUC) of 0.94, effectively identifying high-risk T2D subgroups.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication report the AUC=0.94 for the integrated best-performing model; the publication frames it as effective at identifying high-risk subgroups but the core numeric result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This research represents the first integration of genetic and medical imaging data for T2D risk assessment.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not claim novelty as the 'first' integration; that contribution statement is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study contributes to early detection and precision health of T2D by utilizing artificial intelligence.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This high accuracy for T2D risk assessment promises to catalyze early detection and preventive strategies.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses a similar contribution regarding early detection/prevention, phrased as a promise to catalyze such strategies, which clarifies the impact claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ROC-AUC for the best AI model to discriminate teeth worthy of preservation was 0.901.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ROC-AUC for the best AI model to discriminate teeth worthy of preservation was 0.901 with a 2% margin on dental images.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same AUC but adds a detail about a 2% margin on images, which clarifies the result without changing its substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The average ROC-AUC for dentists was only 0.797.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The average ROC-AUC for dentists was only 0.797.
alignments.rationale: The dentist AUC is reported identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model’s PR-AUC was 0.749, while the human evaluation only reached 0.589.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model's PR-AUC was 0.749, while the dentist evaluation only reached 0.589.
alignments.rationale: PR-AUC values and comparison to human/dentist evaluation are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI models outperform dentists/specialists in predicting tooth extraction based solely on X-ray images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI models outperform dentists/specialists in predicting tooth extraction based solely on X-ray images.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion that AI outperforms clinicians is preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI could help monitor at-risk teeth and reduce errors in indications for extractions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI could help monitor at-risk teeth and reduce errors in indications for extractions.
alignments.rationale: The contribution/implication statement is present and unchanged in the publication (though labeled as a conclusion).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed automated image transcription technique effectively transcribes readings from BP devices.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed automated image transcription technique enhances the accessibility and usability of blood pressure data for monitoring and managing blood pressure during pregnancy and the postpartum period.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core claim of effective transcription but situates it specifically in pregnancy/postpartum monitoring, broadening the stated application and clarifying impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The device-specific model with transfer learning and the device independent model outperformed the device-specific model without transfer learning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The device-specific model with transfer learning and the device independent model outperformed the device-specific model without transfer learning.
alignments.rationale: The comparative model performance claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean absolute error (MAE) of image transcription on held-out test datasets was 1.1 mmHg for systolic and diastolic BP in the Georgia IMPROVE dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean absolute error (MAE) of image transcription on held-out test datasets was 1.2 and 0.8 mmHg for systolic and diastolic blood pressure in the Georgia IMPROVE dataset, and 0.9 and 0.5 mmHg in the Guatemala Set 2 datasets.
alignments.rationale: The numeric MAE values for Georgia differ between preprint and publication and the publication additionally reports Guatemala results, so the result was revised and the reported scope broadened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The MAE of 1.1 mmHg in Georgia and 1 and 0.6 mmHg in Guatemala Set 2 datasets is far below the FDA requirement of 5 mmHg.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The MAE of the proposed automatic image transcription model is far below the FDA recommendation of 5 mmHg, making it suitable for general use with appropriate low-error blood pressure devices.
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert MAE is well below the 5 mmHg recommendation; the publication clarifies suitability for general use with appropriate devices, broadening the practical implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A white matter specific brain age was developed using a three-dimensional convolutional neural network deep learning model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: White matter specific brain age can be successfully targeted for the examination of the most relevant risk factors and cognition.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the contribution toward using white-matter brain age as a target for examining risk factors and cognition rather than describing the specific 3D-CNN development; this broadens the claim and lowers methodological detail and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean absolute error of white matter brain age prediction was 2.84 years with a Pearson’s r of 0.902 with chronological age.
alignments.rationale: The specific performance metrics (MAE and Pearson r) reported in the preprint do not appear in the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individual vascular risk factors and the cumulative vascular risk score were significantly correlated with greater white matter brain age gap.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants with one, two, and three or more vascular risk factors showed an elevated white matter brain age gap (WMBAG) of 0.54, 1.23, and 1.94 years, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides quantified increments of WMBAG by number of vascular risk factors, which clarifies and substantiates the preprint's general correlation claim without changing its scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Baseline white matter brain age gap was significantly associated with processing speed, executive, and global cognition.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Baseline WMBAG was significantly associated with processing speed, executive, and global cognition.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same association with identical cognitive domains and similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The white matter brain age gap can serve as a potential neuroimaging biomarker for an individual’s cerebrovascular ageing process.
alignments.pub_claim_text: White matter specific brain age can be successfully targeted for the examination of the most relevant risk factors and cognition.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames WMBAG as a usable target for examining risk factors and cognition, which clarifies and operationalizes the preprint's biomarker claim without substantially changing its scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High-risk subjects have a greater risk of AD progression compared to other subtypes at 2- and 4-years in both the discovery and validation cohorts.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific longitudinal progression rates for the high-risk subtype at 2- and 4-year follow-up as stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computational framework presented facilitates the identification of subtypes based on their risk of progression to AD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework developed provides a strategy for risk-based stratification of individuals with mild cognitive impairment (MCI).
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the computational framework's role as a risk-based stratification strategy for MCI, a clearer formulation of the preprint's contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Subtype-specific regions demonstrate the largest levels of atrophy-related importance with minimal overlap among the top 5 subtypes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Regions important for model prediction were verified to be classically associated with Alzheimer's disease using modern interpretability techniques.
alignments.rationale: The publication verifies that important regions align with classic AD regions (clarifying biological relevance) but does not emphasize the preprint's point about minimal overlap among top subtypes, effectively narrowing the regional-uniqueness claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neuropathologic changes characteristic of AD are present across all subtypes in comparable proportions.
alignments.rationale: The publication only indicates postmortem confirmation of AD diagnosis labels but does not report the distribution of neuropathologic changes across identified subtypes, so this specific preprint claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4o demonstrated the highest level of agreement with board-certified radiologists among the LLMs tested.
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4o demonstrated the highest level of agreement with board-certified radiologists among the three LLMs evaluated.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative performance result with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant improvements were noted in Criteria 1–3 when comparing reports from the first and last terms using GPT-4o.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Significant improvements were noted in Criteria 1–3 when comparing reports from the first and last terms using GPT-4o.
alignments.rationale: Identical claim and evidence reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All criteria except for Criterion 6 showed progressive enhancement over time.
alignments.pub_claim_text: All criteria except for Criteria 6 showed progressive enhancement over time.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same pattern of improvements across criteria with comparable certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs can effectively provide feedback on commonly corrected areas in radiology reports.
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs can effectively provide feedback on commonly corrected areas in radiology reports, enabling residents to objectively identify and improve their weaknesses.
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the implication (training benefit for residents) and reports this with slightly lower certainty (medium vs high), which clarifies and somewhat tempers the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs may help reduce the workload of radiologists’ mentors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs may help reduce the workload of radiologists’ mentors.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this claim but reports it with lower certainty (low vs medium in the preprint), so the semantic content is unchanged but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: For datasets of large spectral data size, ResNet performed better than PCA-SVM and UMAP.
alignments.pub_claim_text: ResNet performed better than PCA-SVM and UMAP for data sets with large spectral data size.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same comparative performance claim with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using AI classification models, the mean area under receiver operating characteristic curves (AUC) for representative datasets reached 0.966, with mean sensitivity of 89.6%, mean specificity of 95.4%, and mean accuracy of 93.4%.
alignments.rationale: The specific aggregate performance metrics reported in the preprint (overall mean AUC, sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) are not present among the publication claims, which instead list per-dataset improvements.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy improved from 85.1% to 94.6% for endometrial carcinoma grading by using a data characteristic assisted AI classification model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy improved from 85.1 to 94.6% for endometrial carcinoma grading after optimizing the AI classification model.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same dataset-specific accuracy improvement with matching figures and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classification-associated biomolecules such as nucleic acid, tyrosine, tryptophan, cholesteryl ester, fatty acid, and collagen contribute to the pathological diagnosis classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims focus on model performance improvements across datasets and do not include the specific biomolecular contributors listed in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Data characteristic assisted AI classification model was demonstrated to improve the robustness and accuracy of Raman spectroscopy in pathological classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy improved from 85.1 to 94.6% for endometrial carcinoma grading after optimizing the AI classification model.
alignments.rationale: The publication supports the preprint's general conclusion via dataset-specific accuracy improvements; the model-level claim is operationalized through reported per-dataset optimization results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A fully automatic method to segment the vessels would eliminate potential subjectivity and provide a quantitative and systematic measurement of diameter reduction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AngioNet could facilitate systematic anatomical assessment of coronary stenosis in the clinical workflow.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses a similar practical benefit (facilitating systematic assessment) but phrases it more conservatively and in clinical workflow terms rather than asserting elimination of subjectivity, so the claim is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AngioNet convolutional neural network significantly improves segmentation performance on multiple network backbones.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The convolutional neural network, AngioNet, significantly improves segmentation performance on multiple network backbones.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result claim with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performance of AngioNet was achieved using Deeplabv3+, with a Dice score of 0.864, sensitivity of 0.918, and specificity of 0.987.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performance of AngioNet was achieved using Deeplabv3+, with a Dice score of 0.864, pixel accuracy of 0.983, sensitivity of 0.918, and specificity of 0.987.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds pixel accuracy to the performance metrics, clarifying and slightly expanding the reported evaluation while keeping the core performance claim intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AngioNet demonstrates interchangeability in measuring vessel diameter with Quantitative Coronary Angiography.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AngioNet demonstrates interchangeability in measuring vessel diameter with Quantitative Coronary Angiography.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this technical validation result identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AngioNet is a powerful tool for automatic angiographic vessel segmentation that could facilitate systematic anatomical assessment of coronary stenosis in the clinical workflow.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AngioNet could facilitate systematic anatomical assessment of coronary stenosis in the clinical workflow.
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same concluding statement but in a slightly more concise form; the core conclusion about facilitating clinical assessment is preserved and clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participants could identify ChatGPT-generated answers 59.5% of the time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants could identify ChatGPT-generated answers 59.5% of the time, which was outside of the non-inferiority zone. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim reports the identical measured value but adds the trial interpretation (non-inferiority zone), which clarifies the original result without changing its scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Previous users of ChatGPT answered 67.4% of the questions correctly, compared to 57.6% for non-users. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Previous ChatGPT use had the strongest association with the outcome, with an odds ratio of 1.52. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe the same association between prior ChatGPT use and performance; the publication presents it as an adjusted association (OR) rather than raw percentages, clarifying the statistical framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study suggests a stronger predictive value of linguistic features rather than the actual content in distinguishing between ChatGPT-generated and human-written answers. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or reiterate a claim favoring linguistic features over content for discrimination, so this specific conclusion appears omitted in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rigorously planned studies are needed to elucidate the risks and benefits of integrating large language models like ChatGPT in routine clinical practice. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Rigorously planned studies are needed to elucidate the risks and benefits of integrating such technologies in routine clinical practice. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published recommendation repeats the call for rigorous studies with nearly identical wording and intent, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BRAVE-NET model demonstrates superior performance for arterial brain vessel segmentation compared to other models, achieving a Dice coefficient of 0.931. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BRAVE-NET model outperformed other models for arterial brain vessel segmentation with a Dice coefficient of 0.931. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance metric and comparative outcome, so the claim is unchanged in content and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: BRAVE-NET is more resistant to false labelings than other models, as indicated by visual analysis. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: BRAVE-NET demonstrated greater resistance to false labelings compared to other models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim affirms the same comparative resistance to false labeling but with stronger certainty; the core claim is clarified and presented more confidently.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance improvement of BRAVE-NET is primarily attributed to the integration of the multiscaling context path. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance improvement of BRAVE-NET is primarily due to the integration of the multiscaling context path. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions attribute the improvement to the multiscaling context path; the publication uses slightly more cautious wording/certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework developed can serve as a biomarker extraction tool in cerebrovascular diseases. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework developed can improve clinical workflow and serve as a biomarker extraction tool in cerebrovascular diseases. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the biomarker extraction claim but broadens the claimed impact to include improvement of clinical workflow, expanding the original contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TractLearn allows a mapping between the image high-dimensional domain and the reduced latent space of brain fascicles using a Riemannian approach. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TractLearn allows for mapping between the high-dimensional image domain and the reduced latent space of brain fascicles using a Riemannian approach. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the mapping capability and Riemannian formulation verbatim, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TractLearn demonstrates the ability to detect global variation of voxel quantitative values by considering all voxel interactions in a structure. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TractLearn provides a tool to detect global variation of voxels' quantitative values, accounting for voxels' interactions in a structure. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both describe detection of global voxel variation with consideration of voxel interactions; the publication frames it as a provided tool but preserves the technical content.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed algorithm captures controls variability as a standard reference using a manifold approach instead of an atlas approach based on a Euclidean mean. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TractLearn is a unified framework for brain fascicles quantitative analyses using geodesic learning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the method as a geodesic/manifold-based unified framework; this clarifies the original claim about using a manifold reference instead of an atlas, though specific contrast to Euclidean atlas is less explicit.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TractLearn provides a statistical test that takes into account the interaction between voxels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TractLearn provides a tool to detect global variation of voxels' quantitative values, accounting for voxels' interactions in a structure. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's description encompasses providing a test/tool that accounts for voxel interactions, corresponding closely to the preprint's statistical-test claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method has been tested on a sample of 5 age-matched subjects referred with mild traumatic brain injury. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not mention the small test sample or its clinical context; this specific experimental detail is omitted in the publication's summary claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are three unique subgroups of university students who access mental health apps. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are three unique subgroups of university students who access mental health apps. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same descriptive result about three subgroups without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two of the subgroups, defined by either higher or lower mental well-being, have characteristics that are stable across COVID-19 timepoints. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two of the subgroups, defined by either higher or lower mental well-being, have characteristics that are stable across COVID-19 timepoints. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This subgroup stability claim is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The third subgroup, situational well-being, has characteristics that are timepoint-dependent and are influenced by traumatic stressors and stressful situations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The third subgroup, situational well-being, has characteristics that are timepoint-dependent and are influenced by traumatic stressors and stressful situations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the publication presents the result with higher certainty, increasing confidence in the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: User subgroups have different characteristics and likely have different mental healthcare goals. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: User subgroups have different characteristics and likely have different mental healthcare goals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion but expresses it with slightly lower certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Including questions and additional interventions targeting traumatic stressors, reasons for use, and burnout in JITAI-style mental health apps can improve engagement. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Including questions and additional interventions targeting traumatic stressors, reasons for use, and burnout in JITAI-style mental health apps may improve engagement. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published wording shifts from 'can' to 'may', softening the strength of the recommendation while keeping the content and scope the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study developed a model that accurately predicts total hemoglobin mass (Hbmass) and plasma volume (PV) based on anthropometric and complete blood count analyses. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study proposes a newly developed model that can accurately predict total hemoglobin mass (Hbmass) and plasma volume (PV) from a single complete blood count (CBC) and anthropometric variables. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both versions describe the same developed predictive model and inputs (CBC and anthropometrics); the publication clarifies 'single CBC' but does not alter the core contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predicted values for Hbmass and PV showed a root mean square error (RMSE) of 37.9 g and 50.0 g for Hbmass, and 194 ml and 268 ml for PV in women and men, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive model achieved a root mean square error of 33.2�g for Hbmass in women and 35.6�g for Hbmass in men, as well as 179�mL for PV in women and 244�mL for PV in men. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report RMSE performance for Hbmass and PV, but the published numerical values differ from the preprint, indicating a revision of reported results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Measured and predicted data for Hbmass and PV were significantly correlated, with a coefficient of determination (R��) ranging from 0.73 to 0.81. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Measured and predicted data for Hbmass and PV were significantly correlated, with a coefficient of determination (R��) ranging from 0.76 to 0.90. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim of significant correlation is maintained but the exact R�� range reported in the publication differs from the preprint, so the result has been revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model provides relevant complementary data applicable to the epidemiology of anemia, especially in high-prevalence countries or specific populations such as high-altitude communities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model has potential relevance for providing complementary data in the epidemiology of anemia and individual monitoring of hemoglobin concentration in anti-doping contexts. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the epidemiology relevance claim but broadens the potential applications to include anti-doping monitoring, expanding the original suggested use cases.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A network model constructed from the data revealed communities closely aligned with underlying clinical team structure.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Psychiatric Liaison Nurse (PLN) was identified as the most important agent in the clinical interaction network.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's general finding about community alignment is presented in the paper as a specific identification of a central agent (the PLN), which narrows and reframes the original claim to a particular node in the network.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified a vulnerability in the system with the potential to adversely impact information flow.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings suggest that overreliance on PLNs may pose risks to reliable information flow.
alignments.rationale: The vulnerability is specified in the publication as overreliance on PLNs; the publication also expresses this with lower certainty (medium), so the claim is more specific and slightly tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed an algorithmic strategy to mitigate this risk by targeted strengthening of links between clinical teams.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates a data-driven research framework to assess and reinforce important information pathways that guide clinical decision processes.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's algorithmic mitigation is presented in the paper more generally as a data-driven framework to assess and reinforce pathways; the publication is less specific about an explicit algorithmic strategy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results demonstrate a new framework for assessing and reinforcing important information pathways that guide clinical decision processes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates a data-driven research framework to assess and reinforce important information pathways that guide clinical decision processes.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this framework claim with essentially the same wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We investigated a novel application of machine learning for distinguishing specific interactions along a patient’s trajectory which were most likely to precipitate a psychiatric referral.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to the preprint's ML application for predicting interactions precipitating psychiatric referral; this claim does not appear in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM) model using proteomics outperforms traditional clinical models with an AUROC of 0.767 and an AUPRC of 0.2405.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM) model using proteomics outperforms equation-based risk scores such as PREVENT.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies a concrete comparator (PREVENT) whereas the preprint used a broader term; the substantive claim of outperforming clinical risk scores is retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adding clinical features further improves the AUROC to 0.785 and the AUPRC to 0.2835.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The EBM model achieved a receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.767 and an area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) of 0.241, which improved to 0.785 and 0.284, respectively, when clinical features were added.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same performance metrics (minor rounding differences) and the same improvement when clinical features are added.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our models demonstrate consistent performance across sexes and ethnicities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models demonstrate consistent performance across sexes and ethnicities.
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried forward verbatim in the publication with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model provides additional enriched insights into individualized disease risk predictions and in-depth biological insights into biomarkers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study presents a more accurate and explanatory framework for proteomics data analysis.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the contribution as a more accurate and explanatory framework, which clarifies and slightly reframes the preprint's description of enriched individualized and biomarker insights.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our findings support future approaches that prioritize individualized disease risk prediction and the identification of target genes for drug development.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings support future approaches that prioritize individualized disease risk prediction and identification of target genes for drug development.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this forward-looking conclusion with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model predicted mortality with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.848 for all-cause mortality and 0.829 for cardiovascular mortality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning model predicted mortality with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.862 and 0.836.
alignments.rationale: The published AUC values differ (are higher) from the preprint's reported values, so the numeric result was revised while the overall claim about predictive performance remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Stratifying participants into distinct risk groups based on machine learning scores proved effective.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not include an explicit statement about the effectiveness of stratification into risk groups, so this specific result appears absent in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All lifestyle behaviors exhibited an inverse association with all-cause and cardiovascular mortality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: All lifestyle behaviors were associated with a reduced risk of all-cause and cardiovascular mortality.
alignments.rationale: The association is retained in the publication; the certainty in the publication is presented as high (amplifying the preprint's medium certainty).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: As age increases, the impacts of dietary scores and sedentary time on mortality become increasingly apparent, while an opposite trend is observed for physical activity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: As age increases, the effects of dietary scores and sedentary time on mortality risk become more pronounced, while the influence of physical activity tends to diminish.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the age interaction patterns described in the preprint with comparable wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed machine learning model offers valuable insights for the assessment of individual lifestyle-related risks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed machine learning model offers valuable insights for the assessment of individual lifestyle-related risks.
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim about individual risk assessment is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning is a promising approach to enhance the prediction of recovery trajectories in spinal cord injury rehabilitation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning is a promising approach to enhance the prediction of recovery trajectories in spinal cord injury rehabilitation.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this conclusion identically with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Routinely acquired data, such as ISNCSCI, imaging, and demographics, are commonly utilized for predicting functional outcomes in spinal cord injury recovery.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Routinely acquired data, such as ISNCSCI, imaging, and demographics, are commonly utilized for predicting functional outcomes in spinal cord injury recovery.
alignments.rationale: The result about commonly used data sources is carried over without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Traditional machine learning architectures, including linear regression and tree-based approaches, are the overwhelmingly popular choices for implementing data-driven models in spinal cord injury recovery prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Traditional machine learning architectures, including linear regression and tree-based approaches, are the overwhelmingly popular choices for implementing data-driven models in spinal cord injury recovery prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this finding verbatim and with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are ample opportunities for exploring new machine learning architectures to address challenges in predicting spinal cord injury recovery.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are ample opportunities for exploring new machine learning architectures to address challenges in predicting spinal cord injury recovery.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about opportunities for future exploration is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model achieved a performance surpassing baseline estimates by up to 35.68% in the F1-score.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved a performance surpassing baseline estimates by up to 35.68% in the F1-score.
alignments.rationale: The reported performance improvement is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrated operational value by aligning with MS GMLOS and contributing to an 18.96% reduction in excess hospital days.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated operational value by aligning with MS GMLOS and contributing to an 18.96% reduction in excess hospital days.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this operational value claim unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed discharge date prediction model has the potential to enhance healthcare resource management and patient care planning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed discharge date prediction model has the potential to enhance healthcare resource management and patient care planning.
alignments.rationale: The potential application claim is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Additional research endeavors should prioritize the evaluation of the model’s long-term applicability across diverse scenarios.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Additional research endeavors should prioritize the evaluation of the model's long-term applicability across diverse scenarios.
alignments.rationale: The limitation/recommendation is retained verbatim and with the same emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results suggest a difference in the distribution of spatial autocorrelation between genders. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results suggest a difference in the distribution of spatial autocorrelation between genders. (certainty=low, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The textual content is the same between preprint and publication; only the stated certainty was reduced in the publication, so the claim is unchanged but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is uniqueness in network topologies among individuals. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is uniqueness in network topologies among individuals. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim text and framing are effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study indicates shared values in recurrence quantification. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are shared values in recurrence quantification. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording and meaning are the same; certainty and scope unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method of artificial intelligence-based nonlinear dynamics and network analysis can discover dissimilar and similar radiographic features of mandibles between male and female subjects. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A method of artificial intelligence-based nonlinear dynamics and network analysis can be used to discover dissimilar and similar radiographic features of mandibles between male and female subjects. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is the same in both versions with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Reducing poverty and increasing access to sanitation lessened vulnerability to increased cholera risk caused by extreme events. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Reducing poverty and increasing access to sanitation lessened vulnerability to increased cholera risk caused by extreme events. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim and certainty between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning approach evaluated the association between cholera incidence and extreme events as well as pre-existing vulnerabilities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning approach used in this study evaluated the association between cholera incidence and extreme events as well as pre-existing vulnerabilities. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Core content is the same but the publication reframes the item as a described methodological contribution and lowers stated certainty, so it is a clarified claim with tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The created traffic-light system for cholera outbreak risk highlighted potential extreme events and socioeconomic thresholds for outbreaks to occur. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The created traffic-light system for cholera outbreak risk highlighted potential extreme events and socioeconomic thresholds for outbreaks to occur. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The substantive claim is the same but the publication reports it with higher certainty and frames it as a result rather than just a contribution, so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The main limitation is the underreporting of cholera globally and the potential number of cholera cases missed in the data used. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The main limitation of the study is the underreporting of cholera globally, which may lead to missed cholera cases in the data used. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Limitation text and certainty are equivalent between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Fabry disease patients have significantly higher brain-predicted age difference values compared to healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: FD patients had significantly higher brain-PAD than healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same result expressed with abbreviated terminology (brain-PAD); certainty and meaning unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Brain-predicted age difference correlates with multi-organ disease severity and is associated with brain parenchymal fraction, white matter hyperintensities load, and tissue volume throughout the brain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Brain-PAD correlates with FD-related multi-organ damage. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The primary element (correlation with multi-organ disease severity) matches best to Q1; publication shortens wording and retains certainty. Note: some imaging-specific details in the preprint are reported separately in Q3 of the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Brain-predicted age difference might represent a sensitive quantitative biomarker of brain involvement in Fabry disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Brain-PAD offers a comprehensive biomarker of neurological disease severity in Fabry disease. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a stronger, broader formulation (offers a comprehensive biomarker) compared with the preprint's tentative 'might represent a sensitive biomarker', so the claim has been revised and broadened with amplified framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The brain-age model achieved accurate out-of-sample performance with a mean absolute error of 4.01 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific model performance (MAE = 4.01 years) appears in the preprint but is not present among the publication claims, indicating removal from the high-level claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Fabry disease is associated with older-appearing brains as indicated by the brain-age paradigm. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrated that FD patients' brains appear older than normal. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion that FD patients' brains appear older is expressed equivalently in both versions.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Brain-PAD is influenced by both global brain volume and white matter hyperintensities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim about the specific imaging influences (global volume and WMH) was not represented as a standalone high-level claim in the preprint; it may draw on preprint details but appears newly emphasized in the published claim set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method provided subject-level detection accuracy greater than 80% on all the datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method provided subject-level detection accuracy greater than 80% on all the datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim and certainty between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pipeline showed good generalisability across datasets, providing consistent accuracy of over 80% even when evaluated across different modalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pipeline showed good generalisability across datasets, providing a consistent accuracy of over 80% even when evaluated across different modalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same content and certainty retained in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed a machine learning-based, computationally light pipeline to detect CMB candidate subjects from the UK Biobank dataset. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We developed a machine learning-based, computationally light pipeline to detect CMB candidate subjects from the UK Biobank dataset. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Contribution statement is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PASC symptom lexicon included 355 symptoms consolidated from 1,520 UMLS concepts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PASCLex included 355 symptoms consolidated from 1520 UMLS concepts of 16,466 synonyms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Core numeric claim is identical, but the publication adds the detail of 16,466 synonyms, so the claim is clarified with additional detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NLP achieved an averaged precision of 0.94 and an estimated recall of 0.84. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NLP achieved an averaged precision of 0.94 and an estimated recall of 0.84. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match in metrics and certainty between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach used to derive the PASC symptom lexicon may improve identification and analysis of patient symptoms in the EHR. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach may improve identification and analysis of patient symptoms in the EHR. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conclusion verbatim (slightly reworded), so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Symptoms with the highest frequency included pain (43.1%), anxiety (25.8%), and depression (24.0%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific symptom frequency breakdown present in the preprint is not represented among the publication's listed high-level claims, indicating removal.

alignments.pub_claim_text: A comprehensive lexicon of PASC symptoms can be derived using an ontology-driven, EHR-guided and NLP-assisted approach. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an explicit methodological claim about how the lexicon can be derived that was not present as a standalone claim in the preprint's list; it appears as an added high-level conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both the Comorbidity and Text-based OUD risk scores were significantly elevated in the patients judged as High Evidence for OUD in the manual review compared to those with No Evidence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both the Comorbidity and Text-based OUD risk scores were significantly elevated in the patients judged as High Evidence for OUD in the manual review compared to those with No Evidence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text of preprint and publication claim is effectively identical in content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The risk scores were positively correlated with each other. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The risk scores were positively correlated with each other. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same correlation result with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AUCs for the Comorbidity and Text-based scores were high. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AUCs for the Comorbidity and Text-based scores were high. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical performance claim presented in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Risk scores comprising comorbidities and text offer differing but synergistic insights into characterizing problematic opioid use. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Risk scores comprising comorbidities and text offer differing but synergistic insights into characterizing problematic opioid use. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion about complementary insights is reproduced verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This pilot project establishes a foundation for more robust work in the future. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This pilot project establishes a foundation for more robust work in the future. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The forward-looking pilot conclusion is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI-enabled self-referral tool led to a 15% increase in total referrals, significantly larger than the 6% baseline increase observed in matched services using traditional self-referral methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The personalized artificial intelligence-enabled self-referral chatbot led to a 15% increase in patient referrals compared to a 6% increase in control services. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numbers and comparative framing are the same; publication restates the reported effect size.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tool was particularly effective for minority groups, with non-binary individuals experiencing a 235% increase in referrals, bisexual individuals a 30% increase, and ethnic minority individuals a 31% increase. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The increase in referrals was particularly pronounced among minorities, with a 179% increase in nonbinary individuals and a 29% increase in ethnic minority individuals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication reports different (lower) magnitudes and omits the bisexual subgroup, indicating numerical revision and narrowing of the subgroup claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The human-free nature of the tool and its ability to improve the perceived need for treatment were the main drivers for improved diversity in referrals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The chatbot's human-free nature and patients' self-realization of their need for treatment were potential drivers for the observed improvement in the diversity of access. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication reframes the asserted 'main drivers' as 'potential drivers' and rewords perceived need as self-realization, softening confidence and clarifying mechanism.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI-enabled chatbots have the potential to increase accessibility to mental health services for all and alleviate barriers faced by disadvantaged populations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Digital tools can reduce the accessibility gap in mental healthcare by addressing key barriers. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication generalizes from AI chatbots to digital tools and frames the claim more broadly about reducing access gaps; confidence remains similar in tone.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed mechanistic machine learning model predicts patient-specific evolution of atopic dermatitis severity scores on a daily basis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our proof of principle model successfully predicted the daily evolution of AD severity scores at an individual level. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication restates the same individual-level daily prediction capability as a proof-of-principle with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model improved chance-level forecast of future severity scores by 60%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model was able to predict future severity scores at the individual level and improved chance-level forecast by 60%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The 60% improvement over chance is explicitly reported in the publication alongside the individual-level prediction claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heterogeneous patterns in severity trajectories were captured with patient-specific parameters. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heterogeneous patterns in severity trajectories were captured with patient-specific parameters such as the short-term persistence of AD severity and responsiveness to topical steroids, calcineurin inhibitors and step-up treatment. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication gives more detail on the specific patient-level parameters, clarifying rather than contradicting the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model could inform the design of personalised treatment strategies for atopic dermatitis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model could inform the design of personalized treatment strategies that can be tested in future studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds the explicit next-step (testing in future studies), slightly clarifying how the model could be used.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A 9-gene panel was identified that achieved approximately 0.92 average sensitivity and approximately 0.90 specificity in discriminating PDAC from non-tumor samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A 9-gene panel was identified that achieved approximately 0.92 average sensitivity and approximately 0.90 average specificity in distinguishing pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma from healthy samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance metrics and discriminative claim are the same; wording differs only in 'non-tumor' vs 'healthy' but meaning is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classifier accurately discriminated PDAC from chronic pancreatitis with an AUC of 0.95. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding result about discrimination from chronic pancreatitis appears in the publication, so this specific claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 9-gene marker outperformed previously known markers in blood studies, achieving an AUC of 0.84. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 9-gene marker outperformed previously known markers in blood studies with an area under the curve of 0.84. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this comparative performance result identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The discrimination of PDAC from early precursor lesions in non-malignant tissue and peripheral blood may facilitate early blood-diagnosis and risk stratification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Discrimination of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma from early precursor lesions in non-malignant tissue and peripheral blood may assist in early diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the early-diagnosis implication but omits explicit mention of risk stratification, narrowing the scope of the conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The validation of these markers in proteomics and single-cell transcriptomics studies suggests their prognostic role in the diagnosis of PDAC. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 9-gene classifier can clearly discriminate between better and poor survivors of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication presents direct evidence of prognostic discrimination (survival groups), which strengthens and clarifies the preprint's suggestion of prognostic utility.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A genetic risk score based on chromosome-scale length variation can predict the severity of response to SARS-CoV-2 infection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Genetics play a role in the severity of COVID-19. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication weakens the original predictive claim to a more general statement that genetics contribute to severity, narrowing the claimed predictive utility and reducing confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost classifier was able to differentiate between patients with severe reactions to SARS-CoV-2 and those without at a significant level. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The XGBoost classifier could differentiate between patients with severe reactions to SARS-CoV-2 infection and age-matched controls at a significant level. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same classifier differentiation result, adding only the detail that controls were age-matched.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The area under the curve (AUC) of the classifier was only 0.51, indicating it is not clinically useful. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the curve (AUC) of the classifier was only 0.51, indicating it is too low for a clinically useful test. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance metric and interpretation are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost algorithm achieved excellent performance, producing an AUC-ROC of 0.813, and outperforming logistic regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The XGBoost algorithm achieved excellent performance, producing an AUC-ROC of 0.813, and outperforming logistic regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim text and reported metric are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In Brazil, socioeconomic, geographical and structural factors are more important than individual comorbidities in determining COVID-19 mortality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In Brazil, socioeconomic, geographical and structural factors are more important than individual comorbidities in determining COVID-19 mortality. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The substantive content is the same; the publication frames it as a conclusion but does not change the substance or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ethnicity is confirmed to be more important than comorbidities but less than socioeconomic, geographical and structural factors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ethnicity is confirmed to be more important than comorbidities but less than socioeconomic and structural factors. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The relative importance ordering is preserved verbatim; wording and meaning are effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Socioeconomic and structural factors are as important as biological factors in determining the outcome of COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Socioeconomic and structural factors are as important as biological factors in determining the outcome of COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim is reproduced identically in the publication with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings have important consequences for policy making, especially on vaccination/non-pharmacological preventative measures, hospital management and healthcare network organization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: An explicit policy-implication statement present in the preprint does not appear in the publication; no matching policy claim was found.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified 4407 proteins passing quality controls, with twenty-five proteins significantly associated with COPD at FDR < 0.05. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified 4,407 proteins passing quality controls in lung tissue samples from COPD cases and controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The protein count and QA statement match directly; the publication expands slightly on sample context but does not change the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multiple previously reported plasma protein biomarkers for COPD were not significantly associated with proteomic analysis of COPD in lung tissue. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multiple previously reported plasma protein biomarkers for COPD were not significantly associated with proteomic analysis of COPD in lung tissue. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Content is the same; publication frames it as a conclusion but preserves the substantive negative association finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models using Random Forests with the top 5% of protein biomarkers demonstrated reasonable accuracy (0.766) and AUC (0.702) for COPD prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
