alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the clinical guideline varied across demographic subgroups. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The statement about subgroup variability appears unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Additional work is needed to develop a simple yet accurate screener for youth diabetes risk, potentially by using advanced ML methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Additional work is needed to develop a simple yet accurate screener for youth diabetes risk, potentially by using advanced machine learning methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion calling for further work and ML approaches is preserved in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A general-purpose, end-to-end, clinically integrated ML model deployment and validation system was implemented at UCSF. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The paper presents a general-purpose, end-to-end, clinically integrated ML model deployment and validation system implemented at UCSF. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the core contribution of an implemented end-to-end system at UCSF with equivalent framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Completed validation studies provided expert clinical feedback on model performance and usability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Completed validation studies provided expert clinical feedback on model performance and usability, plus system reliability and performance metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same clinical feedback and additionally includes system reliability and performance metrics, broadening the reported evaluation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The present work provides a template for institutions looking to translate and clinically validate ML models in the clinic. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The present work provides a template for institutions looking to translate and clinically validate ML models in the clinic. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The claim is preserved but the publication presents it with stronger certainty, amplifying the asserted contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Building a system that generalizes and scales across multiple ML models requires collaboration among software engineers, clinicians, data scientists, and system administrators. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly include this collaboration-as-requirement statement, so the specific claim about needed multidisciplinary collaboration appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed LSTM-based model achieved an average AUC of 0.892 in predicting sepsis onset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved an average area under the curve of 0.892. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical average AUC is reported identically in the publication, matching the preprint result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model outperformed traditional models such as logistic regression, random forest, and LightGBM. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model was selected as one of the winners of the 2019 DII National Data Science Challenge for both prediction accuracy and clinical interpretability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Rather than explicitly reporting direct comparisons to named traditional models, the publication reports competition-winning status for accuracy/interpretability, which modifies and narrows the original comparative claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model effectively handles irregular time intervals by incorporating time encoding. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A long short-term memory (LSTM)-based model with event embedding and time encoding was developed to predict the onset of sepsis 4 hours before its diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the methodological element of time encoding and places it in the concrete context of a 4-hour prediction horizon, clarifying the applied contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The attention mechanism and global max pooling techniques enhance the interpretability of the model. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model was selected as one of the winners of the 2019 DII National Data Science Challenge for both prediction accuracy and clinical interpretability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication asserts the model's interpretability at a high level (competition recognition) but omits the specific technical attribution (attention and pooling), so the claim is modified and narrower in mechanism detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model was evaluated on two use cases of sepsis onset prediction, achieving AUC scores of 0.940 and 0.845. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved an average area under the curve of 0.892. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the average AUC rather than the two separate use-case AUCs, reducing per-case detail while preserving the overall performance summary.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The MultiCOVID algorithm demonstrates an overall accuracy of 84% in diagnosing COVID-19, heart failure, non-COVID pneumonia, and healthy patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The MultiCOVID algorithm demonstrates an overall accuracy of 84% in diagnosing COVID-19, heart failure, non-COVID pneumonia, and healthy patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the identical accuracy claim with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean AUC for the MultiCOVID algorithm is 0.92, indicating strong predictive performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean AUC for the MultiCOVID algorithm is 0.92, indicating strong predictive performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The AUC value and interpretation are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy of the MultiCOVID algorithm (69.6%) is significantly higher than that of individual radiologists (43.7%-58.7%) and their consensus (59.3%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of MultiCOVID (69.6%) is significantly higher than that of individual radiologists (43.7–58.7%) and the consensus of all five radiologists (59.3%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative accuracy figures and conclusion with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MultiCOVID was developed using a multimodal approach that combines chest X-rays and blood tests. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MultiCOVID was developed using a multimodal approach that incorporates both Chest X-rays and blood tests. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The development approach is stated identically in the publication, with no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The dual-objective adversarial learning framework reconstructs higher quality brain magnetic resonance images (MRIs) that retain disease-specific imaging features critical for predicting progression from mild cognitive impairment (MCI) to Alzheimer’s disease (AD). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The dual-objective adversarial learning framework reconstructs higher quality brain magnetic resonance images (MRIs) that retain disease-specific imaging features critical for predicting progression from mild cognitive impairment (MCI) to Alzheimer's disease (AD). (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-level claim about reconstruction quality and retention of disease-specific features with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Generated images had better image quality than the diced scans, with a structural similarity index (SSIM) of 0.553 ± 0.116 compared to 0.348 ± 0.108. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Generated images had better image quality than the diced scans, as indicated by a higher Structural similarity (SSIM) index (0.553 ± 0.116 versus 0.348 ± 0.108). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The SSIM numbers and comparison are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A classifier utilizing the generated images distinguished progressive MCI (pMCI) from stable MCI (sMCI) more accurately than with the diced scans, achieving an F1-score of 0.634 ± 0.019 versus 0.573 ± 0.028. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A classifier utilizing the generated images distinguished progressive MCI (pMCI) from stable MCI (sMCI) more accurately than with the diced scans, as shown by a higher F1-score (0.634 ± 0.019 versus 0.573 ± 0.028). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The classifier performance metrics are reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Competitive deep learning has potential to facilitate disease-oriented image reconstruction in those at risk of developing Alzheimer’s disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Competitive deep learning has potential to facilitate disease-oriented image reconstruction in those at risk of developing Alzheimer's disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding claim about the potential of competitive deep learning is carried forward without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated deep learning algorithm achieved an AUC of 0.950 in the internal test dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm achieved an AUC of 0.950 in the internal test and AUCs of 0.970 and 0.971 in two external datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the internal AUC claim but adds external validation AUCs, broadening the evidence base and strengthening the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm demonstrated robust sensitivity and specificity of approximately 0.95 for detecting intracranial ICA or M1-MCA occlusion. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm demonstrated a sensitivity and specificity of approximately 0.95 in both internal and external datasets for cases involving intracranial ICA or M1-MCA occlusion. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports similar sensitivity/specificity but extends the result to external datasets, increasing generalizability and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm's performance showed a slight reduction for isolated M2-MCA occlusion compared to other occlusions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The diagnostic performance of the algorithm was somewhat reduced for isolated M2-MCA occlusion, with AUCs of 0.903 and 0.916 for the internal and combined external datasets, respectively. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides numerical AUCs that quantify the previously stated slight reduction, clarifying but not changing the scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This validated algorithm has potential applications in identifying thrombectomy amenable vessel occlusion (TAVO). (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state potential to identify TAVO; instead it focuses on validation and clinician assistance, so the specific TAVO-identification claim is not carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm could aid less-experienced clinicians and potentially expedite the treatment process for eligible patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This validated algorithm has the potential to assist early-career physicians, thereby streamlining the treatment process for patients who can benefit from endovascular treatment. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses a similar conclusion about aiding less-experienced clinicians, adding the explicit link to endovascular treatment beneficiaries, which clarifies the intended clinical impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The manual tracing protocol produced ground-truth data with high intra-rater and inter-rater reliability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The manual tracing protocol produced reference standards with high intrarater and interrater reliability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim about tracing reliability using equivalent terminology.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The nnU-Net framework performed best among the initial deep learning architecture explorations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state that nnU-Net performed best among initial architectures; this specific exploratory comparison is not reported.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two-step localization plus segmentation pipeline achieved a Dice score of 0.898±0.031 for left DN and 0.894±0.036 for right DN. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The two-step localization plus segmentation pipeline achieved a Dice score of 0.90 ± 0.03 for left DN segmentation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a rounded left-DN Dice score (0.90 ± 0.03) but omits the explicit right-DN value, narrowing the reported scope to the left DN.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm outperformed the leading existing automated tool in external validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed algorithm outperformed the current leading automated tool in external testing with mean Dice scores significantly higher for both left and right DN. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides statistical significance and explicitly reports higher mean Dice for both sides, clarifying and strengthening the external validation claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model can be readily deployed for use in observational, natural history, and treatment trials for biomarker discovery. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes generalizability and correlation with manual annotations but does not assert immediate readiness for deployment in trials, so the deployment-for-trials claim was not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CNNs have the potential to accurately classify street images into levels of COVID-19 risk. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CNNs have the potential to classify street images according to levels of COVID-19 risk. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same high-level conclusion about CNNs classifying street images by COVID-19 risk.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NASNetLarge outperformed other CNNs in classifying bus stops into moderate or extreme COVID-19 risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The NASNetLarge CNN outperformed other CNNs in classifying bus stop images, except in specific recall and precision metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication qualifies the outperforming result by noting exceptions in certain recall/precision metrics, tempering the original unqualified superiority claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tuned NASNetLarge achieved a training accuracy of 75% and precision, recall, and F1 score for the moderate label of 80%, 83%, and 82%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report these specific training accuracy and class-level metric numbers for the moderate label, so the exact numeric claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CNNs and street images could advance the epidemiology of COVID-19 at the population level. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CNNs and street images could advance the epidemiology of COVID-19 at the population level. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about the potential epidemiological utility of CNNs and street images.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of brain atrophy and clinical characteristics can serve as an imaging biomarker for early disease phenotyping in frontotemporal dementia (FTD).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of brain atrophy and clinical characteristics can serve as a biomarker for disease phenotyping in frontotemporal dementia (FTD).
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion but removes the word "imaging," making the claim slightly broader in scope while keeping the substantive finding unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PLS-based atrophy and behavioral patterns predicted the FTD phenotypes with a cross-validated accuracy of 89.12%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Partial least squares-based atrophy and cognitive patterns predicted the FTD phenotypes with a cross-validated accuracy of 89.12%.
alignments.rationale: The core result and accuracy are identical; the publication uses the term "cognitive" instead of "behavioral," a minor wording clarification without changing scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Including only atrophy or behavior patterns in the analysis led to prediction accuracies of 69.76% and 76.38%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Including only atrophy or behaviour patterns in the analysis led to prediction accuracies of 69.76% and 76.54%, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The two claims report the same comparisons but the second accuracy is slightly different in the publication (76.54% vs 76.38%), indicating a minor numerical revision.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model using only MRI measures and two behavioral tests achieved a classification accuracy of 83.62%, demonstrating clinical feasibility.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using only MRI measures and two behavioural tests, the model achieved a prediction accuracy of 87.18%, demonstrating clinical feasibility.
alignments.rationale: The published result reports a noticeably higher accuracy (87.18% vs 83.62%), representing a substantive numeric revision and an amplified performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep neural networks have achieved physician-level accuracy in classifying retinal images for medical diagnostics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep neural networks have achieved physician-level accuracy in classifying retinal images for medical diagnostics.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result verbatim with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The quality of saliency maps is significantly influenced by the choice of DNN architecture and explanation method.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quality of saliency maps is significantly influenced by the choice of DNN architecture and explanation method.
alignments.rationale: Identical claim in preprint and publication with no substantive changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Guided Backprop consistently demonstrates good performance across different disease scenarios and DNN architectures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Guided Backprop consistently demonstrates good performance across different disease scenarios and DNN architectures.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is reproduced exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Saliency maps should be validated against clinicians to facilitate the adoption of automated diagnostic systems.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Saliency maps should be validated against clinicians to facilitate the adoption of automated diagnostic systems.
alignments.rationale: The contribution recommendation is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification network was on average within one 5 mm slice for both the superior and inferior borders when compared with 100 manually annotated cases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification network was on average within one 5-mm slice for both the superior and inferior borders when compared with 100 manually annotated cases.
alignments.rationale: The result is restated identically (minor hyphenation only) with no change to meaning or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The segmentation network demonstrated excellent performance with interclass correlation coefficients of 0.99 for both subcutaneous and visceral fat.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The segmentation network demonstrated excellent performance with intraclass correlation coefficients of 1.00 for both subcutaneous and visceral fat.
alignments.rationale: The publication corrects the reported reliability metric from 0.99 to 1.00 (and standardizes terminology to "intraclass"); this is a numerical/terminology revision without changing scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Integrative analyses of abdominal fat revealed highly significant associations with diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and renal failure.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Integrative analyses of abdominal fat revealed highly significant associations with diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and renal failure.
alignments.rationale: The integrative analysis result is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work presents a fully automated and highly accurate method for the quantification of abdominal fat.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work presents a fully automated and highly accurate method for the quantification of abdominal fat.
alignments.rationale: The overall conclusion is reproduced without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrate insights into the progress of fear-sentiment over time as COVID-19 approached peak levels in the United States.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate insights into the progress of fear-sentiment over time as COVID-19 approached peak levels in the United States.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with no substantive changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We observe a strong classification accuracy of 91% for short Tweets, with the Naïve Bayes method.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We observe a strong classification accuracy of 91% for short Tweets, with the Naïve Bayes method.
alignments.rationale: Identical performance claim reproduced in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The logistic regression classification method provides a reasonable accuracy of 74% with shorter Tweets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The logistic regression classification method provides a reasonable accuracy of 74% with shorter Tweets.
alignments.rationale: The logistic regression result is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both classification methods showed relatively weaker performance for longer Tweets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both classification methods showed relatively weaker performance for longer Tweets.
alignments.rationale: The comparative performance statement is identical in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We discovered several novel associations between clinical variables, including correlations between being male and having higher levels of serum lymphocytes and neutrophils.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We discovered several novel associations between clinical variables, including correlations between being male and having higher levels of serum lymphocytes and neutrophils.
alignments.rationale: The discovery claim is repeated verbatim with no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We found that COVID-19 patients could be clustered into subtypes based on serum levels of immune cells, gender, and reported symptoms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found that COVID-19 patients could be clustered into subtypes based on serum levels of immune cells, gender, and reported symptoms.
alignments.rationale: Clustering result unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We trained an XGBoost model to achieve a sensitivity of 92.5% and a specificity of 97.9% in discriminating COVID-19 patients from influenza patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We trained an XGBoost model to achieve a sensitivity of 92.5% and a specificity of 97.9% in discriminating COVID-19 patients from influenza patients.
alignments.rationale: The model performance metrics are identical in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrated that computational methods trained on large clinical datasets could yield ever more accurate COVID-19 diagnostic models to mitigate the impact of lack of testing.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrated that computational methods trained on large clinical datasets could yield ever more accurate COVID-19 diagnostic models to mitigate the impact of lack of testing.
alignments.rationale: The concluding claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We also presented previously unknown COVID-19 clinical variable correlations and clinical subgroups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We also presented previously unknown COVID-19 clinical variable correlations and clinical subgroups.
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement is reproduced exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best-performing model accurately estimated gestational age within about 6 days of early pregnancy ultrasound estimates in both cohorts when applied to heel prick data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best-performing model accurately estimated gestational age within about 6 days of ultrasound estimates in both cohorts when applied to heel prick data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording and numeric accuracy identical between preprint P0 and publication Q0; claim content unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrated an accuracy of about 7 days when applied to cord blood data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model performance was superior in heel prick data compared to cord blood data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Preprint gives a specific numeric accuracy for cord blood; publication omits the numeric cord-blood estimate and instead emphasizes relative superiority of heel-prick data, so the specific claim was revised/omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Algorithms developed in Canada provided accurate estimates of gestational age when applied to external cohorts from Zambia and Bangladesh. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Algorithms developed in Canada provided accurate estimates of gestational age when applied to external cohorts from Zambia and Bangladesh. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim content is effectively identical between preprint P2 and publication Q2.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model performance was superior in heel prick data as compared to cord blood data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model performance was superior in heel prick data compared to cord blood data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim itself is the same, but the publication expresses it with higher certainty (medium -> high) and frames it as a conclusion.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated a mean absolute error of 0.79 weeks for Zambia and 0.81 weeks for Bangladesh when using heel prick data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication Q1 provides country-specific MAE details for heel-prick data that are not present in the preprint; this is an added, more granular result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning classification from features extracted by a curated nonexpert crowd achieves clinical performance for pediatric autism videos. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning classification from features extracted by a certified nonexpert crowd achieves high performance for ASD detection from natural home videos. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication restates the result with slightly different wording ("certified/curated", "clinical/high", "pediatric autism videos" -> "natural home videos") but the core result is the same and clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The curated crowd's ratings on unstructured home videos were evaluated using two previously validated binary autism logistic regression classifiers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A trustworthy crowd of non-experts can efficiently annotate behavioral features needed for accurate machine learning detection of Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD). (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Preprint P1 describes the evaluation approach; publication Q0 frames the evaluated outcome as a general conclusion about crowd annotation ability—related but expressed at a higher-level.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sensitivity is preserved at 96.0% while maintaining specificity at 80.0% and accuracy at 88.0% with privacy-preserving modifications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of privacy-preserving modifications maintains high sensitivity (96.0%) while achieving specificity (80.0%) and accuracy (88.0%) comparable to prior classification methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric performance metrics and the effect of privacy-preserving modifications are the same in preprint P2 and publication Q2.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Privacy-based crowdsourcing of short videos can be leveraged for rapid and mobile assessment of behavioral health. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Privacy-safeguarded crowdsourced analysis of short home videos can help enable rapid and mobile machine-learning detection of developmental delays in children. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication refines the claim to emphasize ML detection of developmental delays in children (more specific application) and uses slightly different phrasing about privacy-safeguarding; scope is somewhat broadened/clarified from general 'behavioral health'.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed machine-learning algorithm can identify whether a patient is more likely to survive or die with high accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine-learning algorithm can identify whether a patient is more likely to survive or die from COVID-19 with high accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication makes the disease context explicit (COVID-19), narrowing the original more general survival/death claim to that patient population.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm improves hospital capacity planning and timely treatment by detecting high-risk patients in each of four identified treatment stages. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method improves hospital capacity planning and timely treatment by detecting high-risk patients in four identified clinical stages. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim about improving capacity planning and detecting high-risk patients in four stages is effectively identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method can be extended to provide optimal estimators for hypothesis-testing techniques commonly used in biological and medical statistics. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method can be extended to provide optimal estimators for hypothesis-testing techniques commonly used in biological and medical statistics. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication repeats the methodological extension claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The work could assist medical professionals with real-time assessments to determine health care priorities during the COVID-19 pandemic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The work could assist medical professionals with real-time assessments to determine health care priorities during the COVID-19 pandemic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion about assisting clinicians during the COVID-19 pandemic is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The average inter-rater reliability of atopic dermatitis segmentation in digital images is 0.45, indicating a 'poor' agreement between raters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The average intraclass correlation coefficient for AD segmentation among dermatologists was 0.45, indicating poor agreement between raters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric ICC and interpretation are the same in preprint P0 and publication Q0.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The degree of agreement for atopic dermatitis segmentation varies from image to image. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The degree of agreement for AD segmentation varied from image to image. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Statement about per-image variability of agreement is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Atopic dermatitis segmentation in digital images is highly rater-dependent, even among dermatologists. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AD segmentation in digital images is highly rater dependent even among dermatologists. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion about high rater-dependence is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The limitations of inter-rater reliability must be considered when using atopic dermatitis segmentation data to train machine learning algorithms. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Limitations in inter-rater reliability need to be considered when using AD segmentation data to train machine learning algorithms. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Limitation statement is carried forward unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predictor performance is most strongly dependent on the size of training data, with smaller gains from algorithmic improvements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Predictor performance is most strongly dependent on the size of training data, with smaller gains from algorithmic improvements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Core claim about dependence on training set size is identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LASSO generally performs as well as the best methods, judged by a variety of metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LASSO generally performs as well as the best methods, judged by a variety of metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Statement about LASSO performance is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A predictor trained in the Taiwan Precision Medicine Initiative for asthma can achieve an AUC of 0.63 (0.02) for a Taiwanese population. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A future predictor trained in the Taiwan Precision Medicine Initiative for asthma can achieve an AUC of 0.63(0.02) and for height a correlation of 0.648(0.009) for a Taiwanese population. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication repeats the asthma AUC but also adds a height correlation result not present in the preprint, broadening the set of reported outcomes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Performance metrics and predictor sparsity for LASSO are in agreement with theoretical predictions from the Donoho-Tanner phase transition. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Performance metrics and predictor sparsity for LASSO are in agreement with theoretical predictions from the Donoho-Tanner phase transition. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion about agreement with Donoho-Tanner predictions is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: UK Biobank trained predictors applied to a European population have measured values of AUC of 0.61 (0.01) and correlation of 0.631 (0.008). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication claims list does not include the UK Biobank/European-population performance numbers present in the preprint; this preprint claim appears omitted in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Anti-VEGF therapy has reduced the risk of legal blindness in neovascular age-related macular degeneration (nAMD). (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No statement in the publication corresponds to the clinical claim about anti-VEGF reducing legal blindness; the publication focuses on ML prediction rather than treatment impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning techniques can be used to predict fibrosis and atrophy development in nAMD patients at 36 months from the start of anti-VEGF treatment. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates the potential of ML techniques in predicting the development of fibrosis and atrophy in nAMD patients receiving long-term anti-VEGF treatment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the ML-predictive claim but frames it as demonstration of potential and emphasizes patients on long-term anti-VEGF (narrowing the clinical context compared with the preprint's explicit 36-month horizon).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ML analysis found ETDRS to be relevant for the prediction of atrophy and fibrosis, while genetic variables did not show statistical relevance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ML analysis consistently identified ETDRS as a relevant factor for predicting the development of atrophy and fibrosis in nAMD patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the ETDRS finding directly; note that the genetic-variable non-relevance is reported separately in Q1 of the publication (closely related to this preprint claim).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best case balance accuracy achieved in predicting macular degeneration was 63% with an AUC of 0.72. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved a balance accuracy of 63% and an AUC of 0.72 when predicting the development of atrophy or fibrosis at 36 months. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same performance metrics and prediction target/timeframe as the preprint, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The lessons learnt from this work can guide future ML-based prediction tasks in the ophthalmology field. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates the potential of ML techniques in predicting the development of fibrosis and atrophy in nAMD patients receiving long-term anti-VEGF treatment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the demonstrated potential of ML in this specific clinical context, which is a more concrete framing of the preprint's broader lessons for future ML tasks.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tweets annotated as misinformation showed specific word patterns and were liked and reshared more often compared to the true and neutral ones. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tweets annotated as misinformation showed specific word patterns and were liked and reshared more often compared to the true and neutral ones. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The validation of the machine learning algorithms yielded enhanced results in terms of Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve Score (0.744-0.843) when evaluating the Support Vector Classifier. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The validation of the machine learning algorithms yielded enhanced results in terms of Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve Score (0.744-0.843) when evaluating the Support Vector Classifier. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical result reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive model estimates in a well calibrated manner the probability that a specific Twitter post is true, neutral or fake. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive model estimates in a well calibrated manner the probability that a specific Twitter post is true, neutral or fake. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same calibration conclusion/result with no meaningful change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The current study offers important insights regarding vaccine related online content written in an Eastern European language. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The current study offers important insights regarding vaccine related online content written in an Eastern European language. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is restated unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future studies must aim at building an online platform for rapid identification of vaccine misinformation and raising awareness for the general population. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future studies must aim at building an online platform for rapid identification of vaccine misinformation and raising awareness for the general population. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation appears intact in the publication, with only a minor genre shift (limitation -> conclusion) that does not alter content.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine Learning and Cloud Computing can be effectively deployed to track COVID-19 and manage its spread. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine Learning and Cloud Computing can be effectively deployed to track COVID-19 and manage its spread. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates this high-level conclusion without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An ML-based improved model can predict the potential threat of COVID-19 in countries worldwide. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An ML-based improved model can predict the potential threat of COVID-19 in countries worldwide. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct correspondence between preprint and publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using iterative weighting for fitting Generalized Inverse Weibull distribution provides a better fit for developing a prediction framework. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using iterative weighting for fitting Generalized Inverse Weibull distribution provides a better fit for developing a prediction framework. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The methodological result is preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A data-driven approach with higher accuracy can facilitate a proactive response from governments and citizens. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A data-driven approach with higher accuracy can facilitate a proactive response from governments and citizens. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is carried over intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study proposes a set of research opportunities and grounds for further practical applications. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study proposes a set of research opportunities and grounds for further practical applications. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the proposed future research directions and applications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Three biomarkers were identified that predict the survival of individual patients with more than 90% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Three biomarkers have been identified that predict the mortality of individual COVID-19 patients more than 10 days in advance with more than 90% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication alters the claim by specifying prediction of mortality (rather than survival) and adds a lead time (>10 days), which changes the original claim's focus and temporal scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Relatively high levels of lactic dehydrogenase (LDH) alone play a crucial role in distinguishing cases that require immediate medical attention. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Relatively high levels of lactic dehydrogenase (LDH) alone play a crucial role in distinguishing the vast majority of cases that require immediate medical attention. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens the LDH finding by stating it distinguishes the 'vast majority' of urgent cases, increasing the claim's emphasis (amplified) while otherwise preserving content.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings support the current medical knowledge that high LDH levels are associated with tissue breakdown in various diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding statement about LDH and tissue breakdown appears in the publication; the publication instead focuses on prediction and decision rules.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study suggests a simple and operable formula to quickly predict patients at the highest risk, potentially reducing the mortality rate. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings support a simple and operable decision rule to quickly predict patients at the highest risk, potentially reducing the mortality rate. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the preprint's suggested formula as a supported decision rule and presents it with somewhat higher certainty, clarifying and strengthening the original suggestion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MultiSurv is a multimodal deep learning method that predicts long-term pan-cancer survival. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MultiSurv is a multimodal deep learning method for long-term pan-cancer survival prediction. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the method and its high-level contribution without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MultiSurv achieved the best results in long-term patient survival prediction according to different time-dependent metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A quantitative comparison with previous methods showed that MultiSurv achieves the best results according to different time-dependent metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same comparative performance conclusion after quantitative comparison with prior methods.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performance of MultiSurv was obtained when combining clinical information with either gene expression or DNA methylation data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly restate this specific modality-combination performance claim; instead it focuses on broader results and added details, so the specific preprint claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MultiSurv can handle missing data, including missing values and complete data modalities. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MultiSurv can handle missing data, including single values and complete data modalities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim that MultiSurv handles missing data; wording is effectively equivalent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Simpler modeling approaches can achieve results rivaling those of more complex methods for certain unimodal data modalities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the preprint's comparative observation about simpler unimodal methods, so this claim does not appear in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The difference in macular structure between male and female is not statistically large enough for sex distinction.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not reiterate this negative/null claim; instead it emphasizes a specific sexual difference (foveal contour), so the preprint's claim that macular structure differences are insufficient was dropped.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The thickness of choroid decreases with age.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The whole layers of retina differ with aging.
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes age-related changes to whole retinal layers rather than stating a specific choroid thickness decrease, broadening the scope and slightly tempering certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning achieved a sex prediction accuracy of 85.6 ± 2.1% and an age prediction error of 5.78 ± 0.29 years from macular OCT images.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the sex-prediction accuracy value verbatim; the preprint's combined metrics were split across publication claims but this part is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning achieved a sex prediction accuracy of 85.6 ± 2.1% and an age prediction error of 5.78 ± 0.29 years from macular OCT images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean absolute error for age prediction using deep learning from macular OCT was 5.78 ���������������������������������������������������������
alignments.rationale: The publication also reproduces the age prediction error from the preprint; the preprint combined metrics were presented separately in the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The foveal contour leads to a better sex distinction than the macular thickness.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The main sexual difference of macula lies in the foveal contour.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's comparative conclusion but frames it more definitively as the main sexual difference, increasing the strength of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The age-related characteristics of the macula are present in the whole layers of the retina, not just the choroid.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The whole layers of retina differ with aging.
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert that age-related changes affect whole retinal layers rather than only the choroid; the publication presents the same conclusion.

alignments.pub_claim_text: These novel findings provide useful information for further investigation in the pathogenesis of sex- and age-related macular structural diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a forward-looking contribution about implications for pathogenesis that was not present among the listed preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Progressive forms of multiple sclerosis remain resistant to treatment, presenting a high barrier to drug development.
alignments.rationale: The publication's highlighted claims focus on predictive enrichment and trial power rather than restating this background claim about progressive MS treatment resistance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning can estimate conditional average treatment effect using baseline clinical and imaging features to predict individual responses to anti-CD20 antibodies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep-learning predictive enrichment strategy can increase statistical power for short proof-of-concept trials in multiple sclerosis.
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasized a method estimating individual treatment effect for a specific therapy (anti-CD20); the publication frames the contribution more generally as a predictive-enrichment strategy to increase trial power, broadening the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In a left-out test set of primary progressive patients, the average treatment effect was significantly greater for the 50% and 30% predicted to be most responsive compared to the entire group.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The average treatment effect was significantly larger for the 50% and 30% of patients predicted to be most responsive compared to the entire group.
alignments.rationale: This result is reproduced verbatim in the publication, with no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The same predictive model can identify responders to laquinimod, which has a different mechanism of action.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model successfully identified responders to laquinimod in a separate test set of primary progressive MS patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the preprint's finding and presents it with higher certainty (publication marks it high confidence), amplifying the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of this model for predictive enrichment could lead to important increases in power, enabling short proof-of-concept trials.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using the predictive enrichment model results in important increases in power for clinical trials.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's conclusion about gains in trial power through predictive enrichment with similar scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed deep learning method called HeartNet improves the overall performance of ECG classification by 5-10% on each insufficient data label category.
alignments.pub_claim_text: HeartNet improves the overall performance of ECG classification by 5-10% on each insufficient data label category.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the quantitative improvement claim exactly, with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HeartNet achieves an accuracy of 99.67 ± 0.11 and an MCC of 89.24 ± 1.71 when trained with an adversarial data synthesized dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method achieved an accuracy of $99.67\text{±}{0.11}$ and an MCC of 89.24± 1.71 when trained with the adversarial data synthesized dataset.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the reported accuracy and MCC values exactly, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method are validated by extensive experiments, comparison, and analysis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The effectiveness and robustness of HeartNet were validated through extensive experiments and comparisons with other datasets.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that validation included comparisons with other datasets, clarifying how robustness was demonstrated while keeping the overall conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method addresses the challenge of insufficient data labels by using adversarial data synthesis with a generative adversarial network (GAN).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed generative adversarial network-based deep learning method called HeartNet effectively addresses the problem of data insufficiency in ECG classification.
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert that a GAN-based approach addresses label/data insufficiency; the publication frames this as an effective solution consistent with the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The paper highlights some limitations of the proposed work.
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include an explicit statement about limitations of the proposed method; instead the publication critiques prior work, so the preprint's explicit limitations claim is not present.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The recent works on ECG classification did not adequately address the challenge of solving the data insufficiency label problem.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a critical statement about recent works that was not present among the listed preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DLS method maintained moderate agreement with a bias of -2.33% in the external validation set.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DLS method demonstrated moderate agreement with 2-dimensional GLS, with an intraclass correlation coefficient of 0.56.
alignments.rationale: Both claims describe moderate agreement; the publication reports agreement via ICC rather than bias, so the claim is presented with a different metric (clarified).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The absolute difference in measurements was independent of subjective image quality.
alignments.rationale: The publication's summary claims do not report this independence from image quality, so the preprint's specific statement about image-quality independence is not carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DLS method has reduced variability compared to human measurements.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The open-source DLS provides lower variation than human measurements and similar quantitative results.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly states lower variation vs humans and similar quantitative results, matching the preprint's claim about reduced variability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DLS measurement provides similar quantitative results to human measurements.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The open-source DLS provides lower variation than human measurements and similar quantitative results.
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates that DLS produces similar quantitative results to human measurements, directly matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DLS method is rapid, consistent, vendor-agnostic, and robust across a wide range of imaging qualities.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes claims about variation and agreement but does not assert the broad operational attributes (rapid, vendor-agnostic, robust) listed in the preprint, so that contribution-level claim was not retained.

alignments.pub_claim_text: In patients scanned by 2 sonographers using 2 vendors, GLS had an intraclass correlation of 0.29 between vendor measurements and 0.63 between sonographers.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a detailed comparison of vendor and sonographer variability not present among the enumerated preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The DLS method showed significant differences between populations with cardiac hypertrophy.
alignments.rationale: This population-specific result about hypertrophy appears in the publication but was not listed among the preprint claims, so it is an added result-level claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model trained to segment the left lung using ps-KDE achieved a Dice score of 0.780, while that trained on CLAHE achieved a Dice score of 0.717.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model trained to segment the left lung using ps-KDE achieved a Dice score of 0.780, which is significantly higher than the 0.717 Dice score achieved by the model trained on CLAHE.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the exact Dice scores and statistical comparison, so the result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ps-KDE appears to be more robust than CLAHE, as CLAHE-based models misclassified right lungs in select test images for the left lung model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ps-KDE appears to be more robust than CLAHE, as CLAHE-based models misclassified right lungs in select test images for the left lung model.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this robustness observation verbatim; there is no change in meaning or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ps-KDE offers advantages over current preprocessing techniques when segmenting certain lung regions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ps-KDE offers advantages over current preprocessing techniques when segmenting certain lung regions.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this conclusion about ps-KDE's advantages without substantive change.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The integration of ps-KDE with deep learning algorithms could be beneficial in subsequent analyses such as disease classification and risk stratification.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a forward-looking application-level claim about integrating ps-KDE with downstream analyses that was not present among the provided preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning approach based on Neural Image Compression with Attention (NIC-A) can pre-screen (pre)malignant lesions in colorectal and cervical biopsies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach has the potential to assist pathologists in prescreening workflows by reducing workload in routine digital pathology diagnostics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the preprint's claim as a potential workflow-assist tool and emphasizes workload reduction; this preserves the claim but with a more cautious, application-focused wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NIC-A classifies normal tissue, low-grade dysplasia, high-grade dysplasia, and cancer in colon and uterine cervix, and identifies celiac disease in duodenal biopsies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed NIC-A approach classifies colon and cervical tissue slides into cancer, high-grade dysplasia, low-grade dysplasia, and normal tissue and detects celiac disease in duodenal biopsies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same classification and disease-detection claims with essentially identical scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach reaches pathologist-level performance at detecting and classifying abnormalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NIC-A reaches pathologist-level performance in detecting and classifying abnormalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert pathologist-level performance with no substantive change in wording or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of NIC-A has the potential to assist pathologists in pre-screening workflows by reducing workload in digital pathology routine diagnostics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach has the potential to assist pathologists in prescreening workflows by reducing workload in routine digital pathology diagnostics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same potential benefit and caveated conclusion with matching scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural network can detect patients who developed chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy (CCC) from the ECG. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated weaker performance for early-stage Chagas disease cases compared to chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes relatively better performance for CCC versus early-stage cases, which clarifies and supports the preprint's assertion about detecting CCC but places it in comparative context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model attains an AUC-ROC of 0.82 for REDS-II and 0.77 for ELSA-Brasil when considering only patients with Chagas cardiomyopathy as positive. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model's performance was evaluated on external datasets, yielding an AUC-ROC of 0.68 for REDS-II and 0.59 for ELSA-Brasil. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports substantially lower AUC values on the named external datasets, indicating a revision (tempering) of the preprint's numeric performance claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model has moderate diagnostic performance in recognition of Chagas disease and better accuracy in detecting Chagas cardiomyopathy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated weaker performance for early-stage Chagas disease cases compared to chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both texts convey the same conclusion that detection is stronger for chronic cardiomyopathy than for earlier-stage disease, with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Detecting Chagas disease patients without CCC is still insufficient, indicating that further developments are needed for higher performance. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The reliance on self-reported labels in the CODE dataset limits the model's performance for non-chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy patients. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies a concrete dataset limitation (self-reported labels) that explains the preprint's more general limitation about insufficient detection in non-CCC patients.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings constitute the first step towards building tools for more efficient detection and treatment of Chagas disease, especially in high-prevalent regions. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest that improving Chagas disease detection and treatment is possible, especially in high-prevalence areas. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the contribution-oriented message but presents it with slightly less certainty and framed as a suggestion rather than a strong claim of being a 'first step.'

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.941 in identifying the primary outcome of OMI, demonstrating superior performance compared to STEMI criteria. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model achieved an area under the curve of 0.938 in identifying the primary OMI outcome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric AUC differs only slightly (rounding/measurement), and the publication maintains the same primary performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The average time from presentation to a correct diagnosis of OMI was significantly shorter when relying on the AI model compared to STEMI criteria. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel ECG AI model suggests potential to improve ACS triage and ensure timely referral for immediate revascularization. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes the preprint's finding about faster diagnosis into a lower-certainty suggestion about improved triage and timely referral, tempering the direct time-to-diagnosis claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The present novel ECG AI model demonstrates superior accuracy and earlier diagnosis of OMI compared to the STEMI criteria. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model demonstrated superior performance compared to STEMI criteria in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly supports the preprint's conclusion by reporting superior diagnostic metrics compared to STEMI criteria with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The OMI AI model has the potential to improve ACS triage and clinical decision-making by enabling timely and accurate detection of OMI in NSTEMI patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel ECG AI model suggests potential to improve ACS triage and ensure timely referral for immediate revascularization. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the potential clinical benefit but expresses it with lower certainty and more tentative language.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prospective studies are essential to evaluate the efficacy of the OMI AI model and its impact on patient outcomes in real-world settings. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The final publication does not explicitly state the need for prospective studies or similar future-work limitations in the provided claims, so this preprint limitation appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GenECG is the first synthetic image-based ECG dataset to pass a clinical Turing test. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GenECG is the first synthetic image-based ECG dataset to pass a clinical Turing test, enabling image-based AI-ECG algorithm development. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the claim that GenECG passed the Turing test and expands its stated implication (enabling algorithm development), broadening the impact statement while preserving certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Synthetic images were created from all PTB-XL ECGs leading to the creation of GenECG, an image-based dataset containing 21,799 ECGs with artefacts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GenECG, an image-based dataset containing 21,799 ECGs with visual imperfections, was created. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms creation of the same-sized dataset with equivalent description of imperfections; this is a direct match.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of image-based AI-ECG algorithms improved through fine-tuning with GenECG data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Algorithm fine-tuning with GenECG data improved real-world ECG classification accuracy to an AUC of 0.821, demonstrating its potential to augment image-based algorithm development. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides quantitative evidence (AUC 0.821) supporting the preprint's claim that fine-tuning with GenECG improves performance, clarifying with concrete results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GenECG will facilitate the development of image-based AI-ECG algorithms, expanding their application to traditional hospital settings and non-hospital environments. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Algorithm fine-tuning with GenECG data improved real-world ECG classification accuracy to an AUC of 0.821, demonstrating its potential to augment image-based algorithm development. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication not only affirms potential to facilitate development but gives stronger, high-certainty evidence of improvement (quantitative AUC), thereby amplifying and broadening the preprint's forward-looking claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model predicts COVID-19 test results with high accuracy using only eight features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model predicted COVID-19 test results with high accuracy using only eight binary features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the high-accuracy, eight-feature claim and clarifies that the features are binary; otherwise the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model was trained on records from 51,831 tested individuals, including 4,769 confirmed COVID-19 cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We established a machine-learning approach that trained on records from 51,831 tested individuals. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same training dataset size and the publication preserves this contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed a model that detects COVID-19 cases by simple features accessed by asking basic questions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model predicted COVID-19 test results with high accuracy using only eight binary features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the preprint's methodological/conclusion statement as a concrete predictive result and clarifies the nature of the simple features (binary).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our framework can be used to prioritize testing for COVID-19 when allocating limited testing resources. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our framework can be used to prioritize testing for COVID-19 when testing resources are limited. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about prioritizing testing under limited resources with matching scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Overall, 98.39% of COVID-19 cases recovered while 1.61% died.
alignments.pub_claim_text: 98.39% of COVID-19 cases in the Philippines recovered while 1.61% died.
alignments.rationale: The numbers are identical but the publication adds geographic specificity (Philippines), which clarifies rather than changes the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Delta wave reported the most deaths at 43.52%, while the Omicron wave reported the least at 10.36%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest case fatality rate (CFR) was observed during the ancestral wave at 2.49%, while the lowest was during the Omicron wave at 0.61%.
alignments.rationale: The preprint reports proportions of deaths by wave, while the publication reports CFR values and identifies a different wave (ancestral) as highest; the claim was revised and narrowed to CFR estimates.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher age groups generally had higher case fatality rates (CFRs) across all infection waves.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher age groups had higher case fatality rates across all four infection waves.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same relationship with more specific wording (all four waves) and higher certainty, clarifying and strengthening the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Omicron variant caused fewer deaths despite being more transmissible.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Omicron variant caused fewer deaths despite its increased transmissibility.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about Omicron's lower mortality despite higher transmissibility with essentially identical wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Age remained a significant predictor of death regardless of infection wave.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher age groups had higher case fatality rates across all four infection waves.
alignments.rationale: Both assert age is a consistent predictor of mortality across waves; the publication frames this as higher CFRs across defined waves, which clarifies the preprint statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All models achieved an accuracy greater than 0.8, with the LGBM model outperforming the others with an accuracy of 0.830.
alignments.pub_claim_text: All models achieved an accuracy greater than 0.8, with the LGBM model outperforming the others with an accuracy of 0.830.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact model accuracy claims from the preprint without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CatBoost model had the highest sensitivity (0.804), while the LGBM model showed the highest specificity (0.884) and F1-Score (0.821).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CatBoost model had the highest sensitivity (0.804), while the LGBM model showed the highest specificity (0.884) and F1-Score (0.821).
alignments.rationale: Identical performance metrics are presented in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Feature importance analysis revealed that EQ-5D was the most critical variable across all models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Feature importance analysis revealed that EQ-5D was the most critical variable across all models.
alignments.rationale: The finding about EQ-5D's prominence is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An efficient LBP prediction model was developed using easily accessible variables.
alignments.pub_claim_text: An efficient LBP prediction model was developed using easily accessible variables.
alignments.rationale: The development claim is repeated without alteration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using this model, it may be helpful to identify the risk of LBP in advance or establish prevention strategies in subjects who have difficulty accessing medical facilities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using this model, it may be helpful to identify the risk of LBP in advance or establish prevention strategies in subjects who have difficulty accessing medical facilities.
alignments.rationale: The application/implication statement is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Random Forest classifier demonstrated the best performance in predicting hyperglycemia among 27 model classifiers, with an accuracy score of 0.894.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest classifier achieved the best performance in predicting hyperglycemia with an accuracy score of 0.89.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same result with negligible rounding difference (0.894 vs 0.89), so the claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Uric acid and age were identified as pivotal variables associated with hyperglycemia in the cohort.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Uric acid and age were identified as important variables associated with hyperglycemia.
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the same key-variable finding with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study revealed an inverse relationship between normotension and age, while hypertension increased with age, peaking between 50-59 years.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this age–blood-pressure pattern and instead adds a different hypertension-related finding, indicating the preprint claim was removed or not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings provide valuable insights for early detection of diabetes and inform the design and deployment of therapeutic interventions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides valuable insights for the early detection of diabetes and informs the design of therapeutic interventions.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same translational implication with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Modeling additional relevant clinical features in larger datasets could enhance the understanding of each feature’s contribution to blood glucose levels.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Modeling additional relevant clinical features in larger datasets could enhance understanding of blood glucose levels.
alignments.rationale: The limitation/suggestion is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning models demonstrate impressive performance in categorizing leg injuries, achieving high accuracy with macro-F1 scores of around 0.8.
