alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sixteen effective factors were identified for spatial modeling of COVID-19 outbreak risk in Fars Province.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified sixteen effective factors for spatial modeling of outbreak risk in Fars Province.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution about identifying sixteen spatial modeling factors with equal certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study's results may assist in better programming for COVID-19 disease prevention and control.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings of this study may assist in better programming for COVID-19 disease prevention and control.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about potential utility for prevention/control programming is present and unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ARIMA models were generated for Brazil and its 27 federative units to forecast Covid-19 cases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ARIMA models identified statistically significant increasing temporal patterns of Covid-19 cases in several federative units in Brazil.
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasized generation of ARIMA models for forecasting; the publication emphasizes the substantive finding (significant increasing patterns) detected by those models, shifting from a methodological statement to an outcome-focused statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The percentage error between the predicted values and the actual values in Brazil varied between 2.56% and 6.50%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The overall percentage error between the forecasted values and the actual values varied between 2.56 and 6.50%.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same forecasting accuracy/error range with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method for dynamic forecasting can guide social policies and plan direct interventions effectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method for dynamic forecasting may be used to guide social policies and plan direct interventions in a cost-effective manner.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same policy-guidance claim but clarifies/extends it by adding that it may be cost-effective, which refines the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The forecasting system can be adapted to different realities by utilizing information from multiple databases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The COVID-SGIS application provides real-time surveillance, forecast, and spatial visualization of Covid-19 for Brazil.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's adaptability claim is expressed in the paper as a concrete application (COVID-SGIS) offering real-time surveillance and visualization; the publication specifies the implemented system, clarifying the general preprint contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models that included structural connectomes significantly outperformed others when classifying healthy controls and people with multiple sclerosis, having a median accuracy of 0.86.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The regional structural connectivity (SC) model significantly outperformed other models in classifying healthy controls (HC) vs. people with multiple sclerosis (pwMS) with a median AUC of 0.89.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding but with more specific model labeling (regional SC) and AUC metric/number; this clarifies and slightly revises the performance statistic while preserving the core claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models that included dynamic functional connectome metrics significantly outperformed others when distinguishing people with multiple sclerosis by impairment level, having a median accuracy of 0.63.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The regional dynamic functional connectivity (dFC) model significantly outperformed other models in classifying pwMS by disability status with a median AUC of 0.65.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result with more specific terminology (regional dFC) and an AUC metric/number, slightly adjusting the performance value while keeping the main claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Damage to the structural connectome, particularly in the subcortical, visual and somatomotor networks, is a hallmark of multiple sclerosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Damage to structural connectivities is a hallmark of multiple sclerosis and is the most accurate connectomic measure in classifying patients and controls.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the hallmark status of structural damage and strengthens the claim by asserting it is the most accurate connectomic measure, thus amplifying confidence in that conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Increased functional coordination between subcortical, visual and somatomotor networks may be related to severity of motor disability in multiple sclerosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Increased regional dFC in dorsal attention and visual networks and decreased regional dFC in frontoparietal and cerebellar networks in certain dFC states was associated with being in the group of pwMS with evidence of disability.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports associations with slightly different networks and state-dependent increases/decreases in regional dFC; this revises the preprint's network-specific claim and narrows the implicated network set and conditions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of multi-modal connectome imaging has the potential to shed light on mechanisms of disease and compensation in multiple sclerosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dynamic functional connectivity metrics are most important for determining disability level in pwMS and could represent functional compensation in response to white matter pathology.
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the general multi-modal potential to a specific emphasis on dFC metrics as important for disability determination and suggests a compensatory interpretation, thereby clarifying and amplifying the specific contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The majority of the samples analyzed were primary non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC), with some examples of metastases and granulomas.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not describe sample composition; none state that the majority were primary NSCLC, so the preprint sample-composition statement is not present in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sterol abundances were consistently higher in diseased lung tissues compared to non-diseased lung tissues at statistically significant levels.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Significant lipid profile differences were found between cancerous and non-cancerous lung tissue samples.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports significant lipid-profile differences between cancerous and non-cancerous tissue but frames the result more generally (lipids overall) rather than the preprint's sterol-specific statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The increase in sterol abundances in primary NSCLC suggests a possible therapeutic role for statins and nitrogenous bisphosphonates.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Targeting the shared affected pathways may be beneficial for a variety of genetically distinct NSCLC subtypes.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains a therapeutic implication but generalizes to targeting affected pathways rather than recommending the specific drug classes named in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A large fraction of NSCLC samples displayed a metabolic phenotype associated with increased SCD1 and ACAT1 activity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A high abundance sterol, high abundance and high m/z sphingolipid, and low abundance glycerophospholipid metabolic phenotype was identified across the NSCLC samples.
alignments.rationale: Both documents identify the same metabolic phenotype across NSCLC samples; the publication describes the lipid-class pattern consistent with the preprint's phenotype description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Inhibitors of SCD1 and other enzymes involved in the production of this metabolic phenotype could be useful in treating many genetic subtypes of NSCLC.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Targeting the shared affected pathways may be beneficial for a variety of genetically distinct NSCLC subtypes.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same therapeutic implication but in broader terms (targeting pathways) rather than explicitly naming enzyme inhibitors, thus slightly clarifying/cauterizing the suggestion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model allows accurate real-time prediction of sepsis within 5 hours preceding.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning model developed allows accurate real-time prediction of sepsis within 5 hours preceding.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance claim for the model with the same time window and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transfer learning technique effectively enhanced the AUCs for LightGBM and MLP models on the target cohort, HDRJH.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transfer learning can effectively improve the feasibility to deploy the prediction model in the target cohort and ameliorate the model performance for external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the positive conclusion about transfer learning but frames it more generally in terms of deployment and external validation rather than naming the specific models and cohorts from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SHAP analysis illuminated the importance of optimizing antibiotic use and restricting fluid management.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SHAP analysis illustrates the importance of age, antibiotics, net balance, and ventilation for sepsis prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents SHAP as identifying predictive variables (including antibiotics and net fluid balance) and lists additional predictors (age, ventilation), and the paper frames these as feature importances rather than directly prescribing clinical management, tempering the original actionable wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transfer learning can improve the feasibility to deploy the prediction model to the target cohort.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transfer learning can effectively improve the feasibility to deploy the prediction model in the target cohort and ameliorate the model performance for external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication directly restates this conclusion about transfer learning improving deployment feasibility and performance for external cohorts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significantly smaller total cerebellar volume was observed in the msTBI group.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Significantly smaller total cerebellum volume and subregional cerebellum volumes were observed in the msTBI group.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same significant reduction in total cerebellar volume (and additionally notes subregional reductions), consistent with the preprint finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Smaller cerebellum volumes were associated with more parent-reported executive function problems.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Smaller cerebellum volumes were associated with higher scores on the Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Functioning.
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe the same association between smaller cerebellar volume and worse parent-reported executive function scores.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Changes in total cerebellum volume over time were associated with white matter microstructural organization in the first weeks and months post-injury.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Poorer white matter organization in the first months postinjury was associated with decreases in cerebellum volume over time.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same longitudinal association between early white matter microstructure and later decreases in cerebellum volume, matching the preprint's temporal relationship.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pediatric msTBI was characterized by smaller cerebellar volumes, primarily in the posterior lobe and vermis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Significantly smaller total cerebellum volume and subregional cerebellum volumes were observed in the msTBI group.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports subregional cerebellar volume reductions consistent with the preprint's identification of posterior-lobe and vermis involvement, though the publication's claim is less region-specific.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Evidence for robust brain-behavior relationships underscore the potential cognitive and behavioral consequences of cerebellar disruption during a critical period of brain development.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results provide further understanding of secondary injury mechanisms and may point to new opportunities for intervention.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses similar implications for mechanisms and interventions but is more cautious about clinical translation, tempering the stronger phrasing about robust brain–behavior relationships in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The University of Maryland Global COVID Trends and Impact Survey (UMD-CTIS) is the largest remote global health monitoring system.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The University of Maryland Global COVID-19 Trends and Impact Survey (UMD-CTIS) has collected data from over 31 million responses across 114 countries/territories.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides concrete size and coverage metrics that support the preprint's claim about large scale, but it does not restate the superlative 'largest,' so the claim is presented more factually.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using limited self-reported data, the UMD-CTIS demonstrates validity and utility for tracking COVID-19 impact trends.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The UMD-CTIS demonstrates that it is possible to define COVID-19 impact trajectories using a remote platform independent of local government resources.
alignments.rationale: The publication demonstrates the platform's ability to define impact trajectories and independence from local resources, which supports the preprint's claim about utility for tracking, phrased somewhat differently.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The survey predicts COVID-19 cases in the absence of testing and characterizes perceived COVID-19 risk versus risk-lowering measures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: COVID-like illness (CLI) signals from the UMD-CTIS survey were correlated with government benchmark data.
alignments.rationale: Correlation of CLI survey signals with government benchmarks supports the preprint's claim that the survey can indicate cases when testing is absent; the publication documents this tracking validity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Risk mitigation behavior trends are correlated with, but sometimes lag, risk perception trends.
alignments.rationale: None of the publication claims address timing (lag) between risk perception and mitigation behaviors, so this specific finding does not appear in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The UMD-CTIS has the potential to support existing monitoring systems for the COVID-19 pandemic and other global health threats.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The UMD-CTIS demonstrates that it is possible to define COVID-19 impact trajectories using a remote platform independent of local government resources.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the platform's ability to define impact trajectories independently of local resources, which is a closely aligned but somewhat different phrasing of the preprint's claim about supporting monitoring systems.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vaccinated and unvaccinated individuals were seen in the clinic at similar rates within 21 days of vaccination.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals vaccinated with BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273 had a higher rate of return to the clinic after both doses compared to unvaccinated controls.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the opposite result (higher return-to-clinic rates among vaccinated individuals), so the preprint's claim that rates were similar is revised in the published findings.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The incidence rates of all surveyed adverse effects were similar or lower in vaccinated individuals compared to unvaccinated individuals after either vaccine dose.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most frequently documented adverse effects within 7 days of each vaccine dose included myalgia, headache, and fatigue.
alignments.rationale: The publication documents which adverse effects were most frequently recorded but does not support the preprint's broader claim that all surveyed AEs were similar or lower in vaccinated individuals; the published data conflict with or do not confirm that generalized assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Myalgia was modestly increased within 7 days of the second dose when considering only matched individuals with clinical notes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most frequently documented adverse effects within 7 days of each vaccine dose included myalgia, headache, and fatigue.
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms myalgia as one of the most commonly documented AEs within 7 days, which is consistent with the preprint's specific observation, though the publication does not explicitly report the same matched-comparison increase.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The frequencies of adverse event documentation in EHR notes are significantly lower than those recorded via active solicitation during clinical trials or post-marketing surveillance.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not mention a direct comparison between EHR-documented AE frequencies and actively solicited trial or surveillance rates, so this specific methodological comparison is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This analysis highlights the rarity of vaccine-associated adverse effects requiring clinical attention and reaffirms the tolerability of the FDA-authorized COVID-19 vaccines in practice.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This analysis reaffirms the safety and tolerability of the FDA-authorized mRNA COVID-19 vaccines in practice.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly restates the same overall conclusion about safety and tolerability, matching the preprint's assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The high-resolution features collectively have greater predictive value for modifiable clinical markers associated with cardiometabolic disease risk compared to typical summary statistic measures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: High-resolution features derived from wearable data have higher predictive value than typical baselines for clinical markers of cardiometabolic disease risk.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with equivalent meaning and comparable certainty, only minor wording differences ("typical baselines" vs "summary statistic measures").

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heart rate dynamics from different activity states contain distinct information about the type of cardiometabolic risk.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heart rate dynamics in sedentary states are most predictive of lipid abnormalities and obesity, while patterns in active states are most predictive of blood pressure abnormalities.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides specific associations between activity states and particular cardiometabolic outcomes, making the original general claim more specific (narrower) but consistent in intent and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Subtle heart rate dynamics in wearable recordings serve as physiological correlates of genetic predisposition for cardiometabolic disease, lifestyle habits, and health perceptions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher resolution patterns in wearable heart rate recordings are better able to represent subtle physiological dynamics related to genomic risk for cardiometabolic disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the link to genomic (genetic) risk but omits lifestyle and health-perception correlates, so the claim is narrower and revised in scope though consistent regarding genomic associations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High resolution digital phenotypes recorded by consumer wearables in free-living states have the potential to enhance prediction of cardiometabolic disease risk.
alignments.pub_claim_text: High-resolution digital phenotypes recorded by consumer wearables have the potential to enhance the prediction of cardiometabolic disease risk.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion but reports it with lower stated certainty (medium vs high), so the semantic content is unchanged while confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 4,913 genes, 1,478 isoforms, 386 exons, and 881 proteins were significantly associated with emphysema.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A total of 3,829 genes, 942 isoforms, 260 exons, and 714 proteins were significantly associated with emphysema.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same type of result but with substantially different (reduced) numerical counts, indicating a revision of the reported findings rather than a change in conceptual scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 75% of the genes and 77% of the proteins associated with emphysema showed evidence of mediation by BMI.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Seventy-four percent of the identified genes and 62% of the identified proteins showed mediation by body mass index (BMI).
alignments.rationale: The publication gives similar mediation results for genes but a notably different percentage for proteins, so the claim is revised numerically though addressing the same mediation question.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The highest-performing predictive model distinguished the top from the bottom tertile of emphysema with an AUROC of 0.92.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest-performing prediction model used clinical, blood cell, and protein data, achieving an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.90 in the COPDGene testing sample.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports model performance in a specific testing sample with a slightly lower AUROC and specifies inputs, which revises and narrows the original performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Blood transcriptome and proteome-wide analyses reveal key biological pathways of emphysema and enhance the prediction of emphysema.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Blood transcriptome and proteome-wide analyses revealed key biological pathways of emphysema and enhanced the prediction of emphysema.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same overall conclusion about pathway discovery and predictive enhancement with comparable certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our supervised machine learning modeling demonstrated the utility of incorporating multi-omics data in enhancing the prediction of emphysema.
alignments.rationale: The specific claim about supervised machine learning demonstrating utility is not explicitly present in the publication text (it instead emphasizes models and inputs), so the explicit methodological claim appears removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The host-response varied between disease groups, with different combinations of proteins distinguishing infected patients from healthy controls.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Characterization of the inflammatory profile of individuals with fever can help to identify disease-specific host responses.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the general observation as a conclusion about using inflammatory profiling to identify disease-specific responses; this is a clarifying restatement rather than a contradiction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Malaria displayed the most unique protein signature, indicating a strong immunoregulatory response characterized by high levels of IL10, sTNFRI and II, and sCD25 but low levels of sCD40L.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Malaria displayed the most unique protein signature, indicating a strong immunoregulatory response with high levels of IL10, sTNFRI and II, and sCD25 but low levels of sCD40L.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this specific disease-result verbatim in content and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Characterization of the inflammatory profile of individuals with fever can help to identify disease-specific host responses.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Characterization of the inflammatory profile of individuals with fever can help to identify disease-specific host responses.
alignments.rationale: This preprint conclusion appears verbatim in the publication with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Dengue fever was associated with a mix of pro- and anti-inflammatory proteins, while bacterial gastroenteritis had a mainly pro-inflammatory profile.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Bacterial gastroenteritis had high levels of sCD40L, APRIL, and IFN-γ.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports specific pro-inflammatory markers for bacterial gastroenteritis but presents a different, more specific signature for dengue elsewhere; the composite preprint statement is therefore revised and narrowed in the published results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Comparing the protein profiles between diseases indicated unique patterns that could potentially be further developed for clinical use.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings can be used to guide future research on diagnostic strategies and therapeutic interventions.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly frames the translational potential as guiding future diagnostic and therapeutic research, clarifying the preprint's more tentative statement about clinical development.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The age-specific prevalence of traditional atherosclerotic risk factors (TARFs), except for chronic kidney disease, formed an inverted U-shape with significantly different peaks and percentages.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The age-specific prevalence of traditional atherosclerotic risk factors (TARFs), except for chronic kidney disease, formed an inverted U-shape with significantly different peaks and percentages.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this descriptive epidemiologic finding directly and with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Smoking was most strongly associated with acute myocardial infarction and premature coronary artery disease, with relative contributions of 47.1% and 42.2%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Smoking was most strongly associated with acute myocardial infarction and premature coronary artery disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the directional association but omits the specific percentage-attribution values reported in the preprint, revising the quantitative detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Diabetes was most strongly associated with multivessel disease and the presentation of non-acute myocardial infarction, with relative contributions of 59.4% and 21.9%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Diabetes was most strongly associated with multivessel disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the key association with multivessel disease but drops the additional association with non-acute MI and the numerical contributions, thereby narrowing and revising the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The absence of dyslipidemia was most strongly associated with the presentation of cardiogenic shock and in-hospital mortality, with relative contributions of 34.2% and 39.8%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The absence of dyslipidemia was most strongly associated with the presentation of cardiogenic shock and in-hospital mortality.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the association but omits the numerical contribution details, so the core claim is revised in quantitative detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The relative importance of TARFs differed significantly by clinical profile and in-hospital mortality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The contribution of TARFs to clinical profiles and mortality varied widely.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same overall conclusion about variable contributions of risk factors to clinical profiles and outcomes with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ENDPAC model employs patients’ age, blood glucose, and weight changes to provide pancreatic cancer risk scores.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ENDPAC model employs patients' age, blood glucose, and weight changes to provide pancreatic cancer risk scores.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the model description verbatim in content and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study aims to determine the feasibility of applying ENDPAC to data held by UK primary care practices.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study aims to determine the feasibility of applying ENDPAC to data held by UK primary care practices.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the stated aim exactly, so the claim remains unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The extraction software will permit validation of the extracted data by primary care staff prior to transfer to the research team.
alignments.rationale: The publication describes software to calculate ENDPAC scores for anonymised data but does not contain the preprint's explicit claim that extraction software will enable primary-care staff validation prior to transfer, so this operational detail appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using HbA1c results only to define new-onset diabetes means this study is not impacted by the quality of diabetes diagnosis coding in primary care.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate this specific methodological limitation about relying on HbA1c-only definition, so the claim is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study period includes the COVID-19 pandemic, thus the data within this period may not reflect data obtained before or after the pandemic.
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not mention this pandemic-period limitation; instead they add operational details and future trial implications, so the preprint's specific limitation is absent in the publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Software to calculate ENDPAC scores will be developed to process anonymised data from 20 primary care providers.
alignments.rationale: This operational detail about developing software to process anonymised data from 20 providers is not in the preprint claims and thus represents an added publication claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Findings from this study will inform the development of a future UK clinical trial to test ENDPAC's effectiveness for the early detection of pancreatic cancer.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a future-trial implication that was not stated in the preprint claims, so this is an added conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 15% of the surveyed population used alcohol harmfully, with prevalence varying by country from 8.7% in Malawi to 26.1% in Namibia.
alignments.pub_claim_text: 15% of the surveyed individuals engaged in harmful alcohol use.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same overall 15% prevalence; the preprint combined overall prevalence and country ranges whereas the paper splits these into separate statements, so the core result is clarified but unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Females were less likely to use alcohol in a harmful way, with an adjusted odds ratio (AOR) of 0.32.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Being female or HIV-positive and on ART is associated with a lower likelihood of harmful alcohol consumption.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same association for females (lower likelihood); the framing groups this with another covariate but does not change the substantive result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HIV-positive individuals on ART were less likely to use alcohol harmfully compared to HIV-negative individuals, with an AOR of 0.65.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Being female or HIV-positive and on ART is associated with a lower likelihood of harmful alcohol consumption.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes the same association for HIV-positive individuals on ART (lower likelihood); the claim is preserved though reported together with the female association.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HIV-positive individuals not on ART were more likely to use alcohol harmfully, with an odds ratio (OR) of 1.49.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals who are HIV-positive and not on ART have a higher likelihood of harmful alcohol use (OR = 1.49, 95% CI: 1.32-1.69).
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same direction and numeric effect (OR 1.49) with confidence interval added, so the result is unchanged and slightly more detailed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings indicate that targeted interventions are necessary to address the multiple factors influencing harmful alcohol use within the surveyed populations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a need to introduce or enforce harmful alcohol use control policies in SSA considering various demographic characteristics.
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts the recommendation from targeted interventions to broader policy-level action (control policies), expanding the scope of the recommended response.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: BlockLASSO is generally as effective for training polygenic scores as global LASSO and other approaches.
alignments.pub_claim_text: blockLASSO is generally as effective for training polygenic scores (PGS) as a global LASSO.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same high-level effectiveness claim for blockLASSO versus global LASSO.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: BlockLASSO produces similar polygenic scores for phenotypes while training with just a fraction of the variants per block.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Within the All of Us (AoU) study, block PGS for T1D reaches an AUC of 0.63±0.02 and for BMI a correlation of 0.21±0.01.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides phenotype-specific empirical performance for block PGS (T1D, BMI), which clarifies and supports the preprint's general claim about obtaining similar PGS with fewer variants but is more specific to datasets/phenotypes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The block approach is more computationally efficient and scalable than global machine learning approaches.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The blockLASSO approach is more computationally efficient and scalable than naive global machine learning approaches.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion that the block approach is more efficient and scalable, matching the preprint's computational-efficiency claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: BlockLASSO typically uses approximately 200 times less memory and runs in approximately 500 times less time compared to traditional LASSO training.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The blockLASSO approach is more computationally efficient and scalable than naive global machine learning approaches.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the efficiency conclusion but omits the specific multiplicative memory/time figures from the preprint, so the numeric claim is revised/tempered to a more general statement.

alignments.pub_claim_text: An accurate PGS can be trained for type 1 diabetes (T1D) using approximately 100 single nucleotide variants (SNVs), while a PGS for body mass index (BMI) requires more than 10,000 SNVs.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds phenotype-specific statements about the number of SNVs required for accurate PGS that were not present in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two robust subgroups of individuals with delayed ASD diagnosis were detected, one with lower support needs and the other with higher support needs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study used various machine learning approaches to identify groups of individuals with delayed autism diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports using ML to identify groups with delayed diagnosis, which corresponds to the preprint's detection of subgroups; the essential result is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individuals in the lower support needs subgroup had fewer comorbidities compared to those with a timely ASD diagnosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study comparatively characterized groups of individuals with delayed autism diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication's comparative characterization covers subgroup differences such as comorbidities, so the specific subgroup comparison is retained in the paper-level claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The identification of opposite subgroups of individuals with delayed autism diagnosis improves our understanding of autism heterogeneity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study comparatively characterized groups of individuals with delayed autism diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's interpretive claim about improving understanding of heterogeneity is softened in the publication to a comparative characterization; the explanatory emphasis is clarified and somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A Random Forest classifier trained on the groups’ characteristics achieved an AUC of 0.94.
alignments.rationale: The publication notes machine learning methods were used but does not report the specific Random Forest AUC of 0.94 presented in the preprint; the numeric result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cognitive functioning three months after surgery cannot be reliably predicted from pre-operative cognitive functioning and the included clinical predictors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Post-operative cognitive functioning could not reliably be predicted from pre-operative cognitive functioning and the included clinical predictors.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same negative predictive finding about post-operative cognition using the same set of predictors, so the conclusion is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pre-operative cognitive functioning was the most important predictor of post-operative cognitive functioning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pre-operative cognitive functioning was the most influential predictor of post-operative cognitive functioning.
alignments.rationale: Both documents identify pre-operative cognitive functioning as the dominant predictor; wording differs minimally but the substantive claim is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Clinicians should not rely on the included predictors to infer individual patients’ cognitive functioning after surgery.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Post-operative cognitive functioning could not reliably be predicted from pre-operative cognitive functioning and the included clinical predictors.
alignments.rationale: The publication's statement that reliable prediction is not possible supports the preprint's clinical recommendation not to rely on these predictors.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a need to collect additional predictors and larger cross-center multimodal datasets to improve predictions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a need to collect larger cross-center multimodal datasets to obtain more certain predictions for individual patients.
alignments.rationale: Both texts call for larger, cross-center multimodal datasets to improve certainty of individual predictions; the publication reiterates this contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individual predictions resulting from Bayesian models may ultimately be used in clinical practice, including their uncertainty estimates.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a need to collect larger cross-center multimodal datasets to obtain more certain predictions for individual patients.
alignments.rationale: The preprint proposes eventual clinical use of Bayesian individual predictions; the publication instead emphasizes the need for more data to achieve certain predictions, which tones down the immediate clinical-application claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Acute kidney injury, anemia, and cardiac arrhythmia were significantly enriched in the physician notes of the hospitalized post-clearance cohort.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Anemia, cardiac arrhythmias, and acute kidney injury were significantly enriched in the physician notes of the hospitalized post-clearance cohort.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same set of enriched conditions in the hospitalized post-clearance cohort; the result is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pre-COVID anemia amplifies the risk of post-COVID hospitalization.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study highlights specific pre-existing conditions that are associated with higher hospitalization rates in COVID-19 patients despite viral clearance.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same association more generally as pre-existing conditions linked to higher post-clearance hospitalization; the substantive conclusion is retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study is the first to analyze risk factors for post-viral clearance hospitalization of COVID-19 patients at a major healthcare center.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert novelty/firstness; the preprint's claim of being the first is not reflected in the provided publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The dataset used for this study is limited to a single healthcare system, which may bias the clinical characteristics of the study population.
alignments.rationale: The provided publication claims do not include this limitation statement about a single-system dataset, so the preprint's limitation text is not present in the given publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The true viral clearance date for each patient is unknown, as the study uses the first of two consecutive negative PCR tests to estimate this date.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims provided do not report this caveat about uncertainty in viral clearance dating; the preprint limitation is absent from the listed publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings motivate follow-up prospective research into the associated risk factors for hospitalization post-viral clearance.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a forward-looking statement motivating prospective follow-up research that was not presented among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ABCD classifications improve longitudinally, commensurate with the degree of behavioural recovery. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ABCD classifications improve longitudinally, commensurate with the degree of behavioral recovery. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The wording and meaning are effectively identical between preprint and publication, asserting longitudinal improvement aligned with behavioural recovery.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Acutely, 95% of patients demonstrated ‘D’ signals in at least one channel but exhibited heterogeneity in the proportion of different channel-level ABCD classifications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the acute 95% 'D' signal prevalence or the channel-level heterogeneity, so this specific acute result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ABCD classification distinguished patients with and without command-following in the subacute-to-chronic phase of recovery. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ABCD classification distinguished patients with and without command-following in the subacute-to-chronic phase of recovery. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about distinguishing command-following in the subacute-to-chronic phase with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients demonstrated significant longitudinal improvement in EEG dynamics along the ABCD scale. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients demonstrated longitudinal improvement in ABCD classifications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report longitudinal improvement along the ABCD scale; the publication's wording is slightly more concise but conveys the same result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ABCD classification did not correspond with behavioural level of consciousness acutely, where patients demonstrated substantial within-session temporal variability in ABCD classifications. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ABCD classifications correlate with behavioral level of consciousness. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint reported no acute correspondence (and temporal variability), whereas the publication asserts a correlation with behavioral level of consciousness overall, representing a substantive change in direction and generalization of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The real-time machine learning framework achieved high predictive performance for hospital-onset COVID-19 infections with an AUC-ROC of 0.89 using all variables. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed machine-learning framework was highly predictive of hospital-onset COVID-19 infections with an AUC-ROC of 0.89. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numerical performance claim (AUC-ROC 0.89) for the framework using all variables.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework demonstrated that dynamic contact network variables were almost as predictive as all variables combined, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.88. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dynamic contact networks are robust predictors of individual patient risk of hospital-onset COVID-19 infections. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint gave a specific numeric result for dynamic-contact-only performance; the publication expresses the conclusion more generally that dynamic contact networks are robust predictors, broadening the original specific result into a general conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The addition of contact network variables improved predictive performance on both non-UK and UK validation datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating contact-network variables improved prediction performance across validation datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the same finding that adding contact-network variables improved prediction across validation datasets, including UK and non-UK sets.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The top three risk factors identified included background hospital COVID-19 prevalence and two contact network variables, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.85. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A model using only three variables achieved an AUC-ROC of 0.85, indicating effective prediction with minimal data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both describe a three-variable model achieving AUC-ROC 0.85; the publication frames it as showing effectiveness with minimal data, matching the preprint's core finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Dynamic patient contact networks can be a robust predictor of respiratory viral infections spreading in hospitals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dynamic contact networks are robust predictors of individual patient risk of hospital-onset COVID-19 infections. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's medium-certainty conclusion about robustness is echoed and stated more strongly in the publication as a high-certainty conclusion about individual patient risk, representing clarification and an amplification of confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The overall accuracy for generative AI models across 54 studies was 56.9% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 51.0–62.7%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analysis of 83 studies revealed an overall diagnostic accuracy of 52.1%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication updates the analysis (larger evidence base: 83 vs 54 studies) and reports a lower overall accuracy, constituting a substantive revision and a broader dataset with a tempered summary accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: On average, physicians exceeded the accuracy of the generative AI models by 14.4% (95% CI: 4.9–23.8%, p-value =0.004). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: No significant performance difference was found between AI models and physicians overall (p�=�0.10) or non-expert physicians (p�=�0.93). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication overturns the preprint's significant physician advantage by reporting no significant overall difference, representing a direct revision of the earlier comparative finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Generative AI models have the potential to enhance healthcare delivery and medical education, provided they are integrated with caution and their limitations are well-understood. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings suggest potential for enhancing healthcare delivery and medical education when implemented with appropriate understanding of its limitations. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions express cautious optimism about potential utility; the publication repeats the conclusion but with lower certainty, so the claim is clarified and presented with tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Some generative AI models are comparable to non-expert physicians in diagnostic accuracy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: No significant performance difference was found between AI models and physicians overall (p�=�0.10) or non-expert physicians (p�=�0.93). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's finding of no significant difference with non-expert physicians supports the preprint's assertion of comparability; the publication frames this as a statistical result but the substantive claim remains consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diagnostic performance of generative AI models varies significantly by model and medical specialty. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Generative AI demonstrates promising diagnostic capabilities with accuracy varying by model. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both acknowledge variability by model; the publication retains variability by model but omits explicit mention of differences by specialty, narrowing the original scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ARIA system was able to classify referable vs. non-referable cases with an AUROC of 98% for international patient-cases and 98.3% for Mexican patient-cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ARIA system was able to classify referable vs nonreferable cases with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 98% for international patient cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the high AUROC for international cases; the Mexican-specific AUROC is not stated verbatim in the publication's Q0 text, so the claim is slightly clarified in framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity of the ARIA system for Mexican patient-cases was 95.2%, which outperformed the average performance of the 17 ophthalmologist participants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ARIA system's sensitivity for Mexican patient cases was 95.2% and specificity was 90%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same sensitivity value for Mexican cases and adds specificity; the core preprint claim about sensitivity is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant sensitivity improvements were observed when participants were exposed to the ARIA system's answer as a second opinion, increasing from 87.3% to 93.3%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ARIA system can be useful as an assistive tool, as sensitivity was significantly higher when ophthalmologists were exposed to the ARIA system's answer prior to their own classification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both report increased sensitivity when clinicians see the ARIA output; the publication frames this as an assistive (prior-to-classification) use case rather than explicitly calling it a second-opinion post-hoc, clarifying the use context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results demonstrate that ARIA systems present a substantial opportunity for efficient expansion of monitoring capacity for early detection of diabetes-related blindness in Latin American countries. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results demonstrate that both independent and assistive use cases of the ARIA system present a substantial opportunity for expanding the monitoring capacity for the early detection of diabetes-related blindness in Latin American countries such as Mexico. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the preprint conclusion to explicitly include both independent and assistive use cases and names Mexico as an example, broadening and clarifying the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed 'white-box' Bayesian network model achieves accurate and interpretable predictions of immunotherapy responses against non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed 'white-box' Bayesian network model achieves accurate and interpretable predictions of immunotherapy responses against nonsmall cell lung cancer (NSCLC). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same claim about accuracy and interpretability for NSCLC immunotherapy response prediction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This Tree-Augmented naïve Bayes model (TAN) precisely predicted durable clinical benefits and distinguished two clinically significant subgroups with distinct prognoses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tree-augmented naïve Bayes (TAN) model accurately predicted durable clinical benefits and distinguished two clinically significant subgroups with distinct prognoses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same performance and subgroup-discovery claim for the TAN model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The state-of-the-art white-box TAN approach achieved greater accuracy than previous methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The state-of-the-art white-box TAN approach achieved greater accuracy than previous methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the claim that the TAN approach outperformed previous methods.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model will guide clinicians in selecting NSCLC patients who genuinely require immunotherapy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model is expected to guide clinicians in selecting NSCLC patients who truly require immunotherapy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same expectation that the model will aid clinician selection of patients for immunotherapy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach is expected to be easily applied to other types of cancer. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach is anticipated to be easily applied to other types of cancer. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions express the same expectation about transferability to other cancers with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effective population sizes for each country indicated by Maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimates are below 20% of the total population size. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Maximum a posteriori estimates of the initial susceptible population indicated effective population sizes of below 20% for each of the seven European countries. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q0 restates the same MAP-based result for effective population sizes across the seven countries with no substantive change in wording or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An estimated 6.4% of the total population across all seven countries would be immune following the first wave of infection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We estimated that 6.4% of the total population would be immune across all seven countries. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q1 repeats the identical population-level immunity estimate with matching certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A large proportion of the total population will remain without immunity after the current wave. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our results indicate that after the current wave, a large proportion of the total population will remain without immunity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Q3 conveys the same conclusion but with reduced stated certainty (medium vs high), so the claim is preserved but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In the absence of strong seasonal effects, new medications, or more comprehensive contact tracing, further epidemic waves in different geographic centres are likely. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to the forecast about likely future epidemic waves; this scenario-based conclusion does not appear in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Males were more likely to develop HFrEF than HFpEF, with a hazard ratio of 2.07 for HFrEF compared to 1.11 for HFpEF. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Males were more likely to develop HFrEF than HFpEF, with a hazard ratio of 2.07 for HFrEF compared to 1.11 for HFpEF. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q0 restates the same hazard ratio result for male sex and heart failure subtype with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Atrial fibrillation and pulmonary hypertension had stronger associations with the risk of HFpEF, while cardiomyopathy and myocardial infarction were more strongly associated with HFrEF. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Atrial fibrillation and pulmonary hypertension had stronger associations with the risk of HFpEF, while cardiomyopathy and myocardial infarction were more strongly associated with HFrEF. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q1 matches the differential associations between comorbidities and HF subtypes exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Age was identified as the strongest predictor across all heart failure subtypes, with a relative influence from the gradient boosted model exceeding 35%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Age was identified as the strongest predictor across all heart failure subtypes, with a relative influence from the gradient-boosted model greater than 35%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q2 conveys the same model-based result about age's influence with equivalent magnitude and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study highlights the heterogeneous relationships between various risk factors and heart failure types, suggesting potential differences in the etiology of HFpEF and HFrEF. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings highlight the heterogeneous relationships between several important risk factors and heart failure types, suggesting potential differences in the etiology of HFpEF and HFrEF. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Q4 reiterates the same concluding interpretation about heterogeneity and possible etiologic differences with equivalent nuance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Employing an advanced machine learning technique allowed for the assessment of the relative importance of each risk factor on the development of HFpEF and HFrEF. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not explicitly state the methodological contribution about using an advanced machine-learning approach to assess relative importance; that contribution is not present among Q0–Q4.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using principal components analysis (PCA) for feature extraction provides higher classification accuracies compared to a region-of-interest based approach. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using principal components analysis for feature extraction provides higher classification accuracies compared to a region-of-interest based approach. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q0 restates the same methodological result about PCA vs ROI-based feature extraction with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The differences between the two feature extraction methods were significantly reduced when an independent sample was used for validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The differences between the two feature extraction methods were significantly reduced when an independent sample was used for validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q1 matches the preprint claim about reduced differences with independent validation exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both T1-weighted and diffusion MRI data can successfully differentiate between subject groups, with features from diffusion MRI resulting in significantly higher classification accuracies in independent validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Features obtained from diffusion magnetic resonance imaging data resulted in significantly higher classification accuracies when an independent validation cohort was used. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q2 conveys the same result emphasizing diffusion MRI features gave higher accuracies in independent validation; wording is equivalent in substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classification accuracy can be affected by group size, age, sex, and movement artifacts. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classification accuracy can be affected by group size, age, sex and movement artefacts. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Q3 repeats the same limitation about factors affecting classification accuracy with equivalent detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: With appropriate controls and out-of-sample cross validation, MRI-based classifiers can be an important adjunct to clinical evaluation for the differential diagnosis of parkinsonian disorders. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Magnetic resonance imaging based classifiers may be an important adjunct to clinical evaluation for the differential diagnosis of parkinsonian disorders. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Q4 makes the same concluding claim but with slightly more cautious phrasing and lower stated certainty (may/medium vs can/high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Project for Objective Measures Using Computational Psychiatry Technology (PROMPT) aims to develop objective, noninvasive, and easy-to-use biomarkers for assessing the severity of depressive and neurocognitive disorders. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Project for Objective Measures Using Computational Psychiatry Technology (PROMPT) aims to develop objective, noninvasive, and easy-to-use biomarkers for assessing the severity of depressive and neurocognitive disorders. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Q0 restates the PROMPT project's aims identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Advances in technology allow for the quantification of features such as facial expressions, speech information, body motion, daily activity, and sleep, which are reflective of disorder severity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not include the explicit enumeration of specific technology-derived features (facial expressions, speech, body motion, etc.); that specific descriptive claim is absent in Q0–Q3.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of machine learning approaches in the study is intended to predict the presence of symptoms, severity, and the improvement or deterioration of symptoms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of machine learning in this study is intended to predict the presence of symptoms, severity, and the improvement/deterioration of symptoms in participants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q1 conveys the same planned use of machine learning to predict symptom presence and trajectories with equivalent intent and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The variability of samples presents challenges in extracting features that commonly reflect disorder severity. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Challenges in the study may include the large variability of samples, which complicates the extraction of features that reflect disorder severity. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Q3 restates the same limitation about sample variability and its effect on feature extraction with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The annual number of published RCTs has substantially increased over four decades. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim explicitly reports the long-term increase in the annual number of published RCTs; that specific bibliometric result is omitted in Q0–Q4.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Risk of bias in RCTs has generally decreased over the last decades, particularly in the domains of allocation concealment, random sequence generation, and blinding of outcome assessment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The risk of bias in randomized controlled trials (RCTs) has generally decreased over the last decades. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Q0 presents the same overarching conclusion that risk of bias has decreased over time; the publication phrasing is slightly more general but consistent in substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Trial registration and adherence to CONSORT reporting guidelines have rapidly increased in the latest period. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Trial registration and the use of the CONSORT Statement have rapidly increased over time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q2 conveys the same observation about increased trial registration and CONSORT use over time with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher impact factor journals consistently show lower risk of bias and higher levels of trial registration and CONSORT adherence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The likelihood of bias in RCTs is lower in journals with a higher impact factor. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Q1 retains the element about lower bias in higher-impact journals but omits the preprint's additional claim about higher registration and CONSORT adherence, so the claim has been narrowed/revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further improvement of RCT registration, conduct, and reporting is still urgently needed due to the relatively high probabilities of bias that remain. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further improvement of RCT registration, conduct, and reporting is still urgently needed. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Q4 repeats the same call for continued improvement in registration, conduct, and reporting with equivalent urgency.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Unsupervised clustering identified distinct sub-groups of COVID-19 patients based on symptom patterns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Unsupervised clustering identified distinct sub-phenotypes of COVID-19 symptoms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording differs slightly (sub-groups vs sub-phenotypes) but the result is the same identification of distinct symptom clusters.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients presenting with gastrointestinal symptoms were more commonly female, had a longer duration of symptoms before presentation, and had lower 30-day mortality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients presenting with gastrointestinal symptoms were more commonly female, had a longer duration of symptoms before presentation, and had lower 30-day mortality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim, with no substantive change in meaning or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients presenting with confusion, with or without core symptoms, were older and had a higher unadjusted mortality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients presenting with confusion, with or without core symptoms, were older and had a higher unadjusted mortality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried over unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study proposes that four patterns of symptoms are usefully distinct from the core symptom groups, each associated with different in-hospital mortality rates. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Four sub-phenotypes are usefully distinct from the core symptom group: gastro-intestinal disease, productive cough, confusion, and pauci-symptomatic presentations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the four named sub-phenotypes, clarifying the original statement while keeping the same overall conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings from the ISARIC-4C study will influence clinical diagnosis, risk prediction, and future mechanistic and clinical studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No equivalent forward-looking claim about influencing diagnosis, risk prediction, or future studies appears in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: cfTrack can sensitively detect minimal residual disease (MRD) and cancer recurrence by integrating signals across known clonal tumor mutations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: cfTrack can sensitively detect minimal residual disease and cancer recurrence by integrating signals across known clonal tumor mutations of a patient. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same capability with a small wording addition clarifying the patient-specific nature of clonal mutations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: cfTrack detects tumor evolution and second primary cancers by de novo identifying emerging tumor mutations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: cfTrack detects tumor evolution and second primary cancers by de novo identifying emerging tumor mutations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim carried into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: cfTrack achieved an average AUC of 99% on the validation dataset and 100% on the independent dataset in detecting recurrence in samples with tumor fraction ≥0.05%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: On the simulation data, cfTrack achieved an average AUC of 99% on the validation dataset and 100% on the independent dataset in detecting recurrence in samples with tumor fractions ≥0.05%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies that these AUC results refer to simulation data, narrowing the context though not changing the reported metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: cfTrack accurately monitors tumor evolution during treatment, which cannot be accomplished by previous methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: cfTrack accurately monitors tumor evolution during treatment, which cannot be accomplished by previous methods. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same claim about monitoring evolution and the novelty relative to prior methods.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: cfTrack uses whole-exome sequencing (WES) of cfDNA to track the full range of cancer treatment outcomes, including MRD, recurrence, evolution, and second primary cancer. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state that WES of cfDNA is used to track the full range of outcomes as a single claim; the explicit methodological/coverage statement from the preprint is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Fifty genes were identified to be significantly increased in expression with the severity of diabetic retinopathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Fifty genes were identified to be significantly increased in expression with the severity of diabetic retinopathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is presented identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Functional enrichment analyses suggested these severity-associated genes are related to inflammation and immune responses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Functional enrichment analyses suggested that these severity-associated genes are related to inflammation and immune responses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this functional interpretation with essentially the same framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proportions of memory B cells, M2 macrophages, and Müller glia were significantly increased with the progression of diabetic retinopathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proportions of memory B cells, M2 macrophages, and Müller glia were significantly increased with the progression of diabetic retinopathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This cell-proportion result is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CCND1 and FCGR2B are further identified as key regulators that interact with many other severity-associated genes and are crucial to inflammation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CCND1 and FCGR2B are identified as key regulators that interact with many other severity-associated genes and are crucial to inflammation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same, but the publication presents this as a higher-certainty/result-level finding (certainty increased from medium to high).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EEG-features predicted both 3-month and 12-month outcomes in ICU patients with acute disorders of consciousness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EEG features predicted both 3-month and 12-month outcomes in ICU patients with acute disorders of consciousness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this predictive finding verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: fMRI-features predicted 3-month outcomes but not 12-month outcomes in ICU patients with acute disorders of consciousness. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: fMRI features appeared to predict 3-month outcomes but not 12-month outcomes in the same patient population. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same tentative finding using similar language.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Independent clinical predictors of time to favourable outcome include younger age, TBI, command-following abilities at admission, initial brain-imaging without severe pathology, improving consciousness in the ICU, and favourable visual-graded EEG. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Independent clinical predictors of time to favorable outcome include younger age, traumatic brain injury, command-following abilities at admission, initial brain imaging without severe pathological findings, improving consciousness in the ICU, and favorable visual-graded EEG. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same list of independent clinical predictors presented unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study indicates that EEG- and fMRI-features along with clinical data can reliably predict short-term outcomes in patients with acute disorders of consciousness. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EEG and fMRI features, along with readily available clinical data, can predict short-term outcomes of patients with acute disorders of consciousness. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this practical conclusion with minimal wording change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hypertension group is on average 0.3098 years older in WM brain age than the non-hypertension group of the same chronological age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The hypertension group is on average 0.31 years older in white matter brain age than the nonhypertension group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric value and meaning are preserved with rounding in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Females are on average 0.8143 years younger in WM brain age than males of the same chronological age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Women are on average 0.81 years younger in white matter brain age than men. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same finding with rounding/wording differences only.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Every 10 mm Hg increase in diastolic blood pressure can lead to a 0.371 years increase in brain age. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Mendelian randomization analyses showed an overall significant positive causal effect of DBP on white matter BAG of 0.37 years per 10 mmHg. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the effect specifically as coming from Mendelian randomization analyses (clarifying method) and reports similar magnitude with increased certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The significant causal effect of diastolic blood pressure on brain age is most prominent among women aged 50-59 and 60-69. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The causal effect of blood pressure on white matter brain aging is most prominent among women aged 50-59 and aged 60-69. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The subgroup specificity is preserved verbatim in the publication.
