alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hypertension and genetic predisposition to higher blood pressure can accelerate WM brain aging specifically targeting late middle-aged women. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: High blood pressure can accelerate white matter brain aging among late middle-aged women. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same overall conclusion about high blood pressure accelerating WM brain aging in late middle-aged women.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is significant evidence for the generalisability of patterns from interictal to ictal time windows across patients, particularly reflected in signal power and high-frequency network-based features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Evidence strongly supported generalisability from interictal to ictal time windows across patients, particularly in signal power and high-frequency network-based features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with near-identical wording and emphasis on the same feature classes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The majority of patients showed consistent patterns of epileptogenicity across interictal and ictal time windows, with a mean area-under-curve (AUC) of 0.6. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Consistent patterns of epileptogenicity were observed across time windows within most patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core observation of consistent patterns but omits the numeric AUC, simplifying the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Signal features of epileptogenic regions could generalise across patients in both interictal and ictal time windows, with higher generalisability observed in the ictal time window (mean AUC 0.75 vs. 0.59). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Signal features of epileptogenic regions generalised across patients, with higher generalisability in the ictal window. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same directional result (higher ictal generalisability) but omits the numerical AUCs, so the core claim is clarified without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Signal power and moment features were the most contributory to the cross-patient generalisation in the interictal window, while signal complexity features were the most contributory in the ictal window. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Signal complexity features were particularly contributory in cross-patient generalisation across patients. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes signal complexity as contributory but omits the preprint's claim about power and moment features in the interictal window, narrowing the feature-level claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The explainable machine-learning pipeline developed can guide future developments in epilepsy investigations. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings offer insights into generalisable features of epileptic neural activity across time and patients, with implications for future automated approaches to supplement other EZ localisation methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the pipeline's contribution more explicitly as informing automated approaches for EZ localisation, clarifying the intended translational implications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Liver-Quant is an open-source Python package designed for quantifying fat and fibrosis in liver whole slide images. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Liver-Quant provides an open-source solution for rapid and precise quantification of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) in whole slide images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the tool as a rapid, precise solution targeted to MASLD specifically, adding performance framing and narrowing the disease focus compared to the preprint's broader description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method demonstrated a substantial correlation (ρ=0.92) for steatosis quantification and a moderate correlation (ρ=0.51) for fibrosis quantification with human pathologist scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Liver-Quant demonstrates a substantial correlation (ρ = 0.92) in steatosis quantification compared to pathologist scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the steatosis correlation value exactly but separates the fibrosis result into a distinct statement, so this part of the preprint claim is restated more specifically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method demonstrated a substantial correlation (ρ=0.92) for steatosis quantification and a moderate correlation (ρ=0.51) for fibrosis quantification with human pathologist scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Fibrosis quantification using Liver-Quant yields a moderate correlation (ρ = 0.51) with pathologist scores. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the reported fibrosis correlation but downgrades its stated certainty from high to medium, tempering confidence in that component.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Stain normalisation resulted in excellent agreement in Collagen Proportionate Area measurements among three different staining dyes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Stain normalisation in Liver-Quant results in excellent agreement in Collagen Proportionate Area (CPA) measurements across multiple staining dyes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result about stain normalisation and CPA agreement across dyes with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High resolution images at 0.25 microns per pixel are essential for accurate Steatosis Proportionate Area measurement. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: High-resolution images (0.25 or 0.50 μm per pixel) are necessary for accurate Steatosis Proportionate Area (SPA) measurement using Liver-Quant. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication broadens acceptable resolutions to include 0.50 μm, raises the certainty to high, and frames the requirement as necessary for accurate measurement, amplifying confidence and widening scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LG cohort highlighted a diverse disease spectrum of obstructive jaundice, including cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic adenocarcinoma, and common bile duct stones as leading causes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The large general cohort highlighted a diverse disease spectrum of obstructive jaundice, including cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic adenocarcinoma, and common bile duct stones as leading causes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same descriptive finding about the cohort and leading causes with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Traditional serum markers such as CA 19-9 and CEA lacked standalone diagnostic accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Traditional serum markers such as carbohydrate antigen 19-9 and carcinoembryonic antigen lacked stand-alone diagnostic accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim with identical meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two ML-based models, collectively termed the MOLT model, were developed to differentiate benign from malignant causes and further stratify malignant and benign diseases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two machine learning-based models were developed, including a classifier to differentiate benign from malignant causes with an AUROC of 0.862 and a multiclass model with an accuracy of 0.777. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same models but adds specific performance metrics, clarifying and strengthening the reported performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The MOLT model provides a rapid and reliable primary assessment for patients with obstructive jaundice, enabling clinicians to identify potential etiologies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides a practical and interpretable machine learning-based diagnostic tool that enhances transparency in the decision-making process for obstructive jaundice. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the model as practical and interpretable, emphasizing transparency rather than the preprint's phrasing of rapid/reliable primary assessment.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Interpretable ML tools provided clarity on critical features, enhancing transparency in the decision-making process. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides a practical and interpretable machine learning-based diagnostic tool that enhances transparency in the decision-making process for obstructive jaundice. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements emphasise interpretability and improved transparency in clinical decision-making, with consistent framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Diffusion Histology Imaging (DHI) accurately detected and classified various MS lesion types. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DBSI-DNN improves the classification of different MS lesion subtypes, which could aid clinical decision making. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the technology as improving classification with potential clinical aid and uses slightly more cautious wording/certainty compared to the preprint's stronger accuracy claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DHI achieved a 93.6% overall concordance with neurologist determinations of all five MS lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DBSI-DNN derived lesion classification achieved 93.4% overall concordance with predetermined lesion types. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same overall-concordance result with a very similar numeric value and equivalent certainty, a minor numerical revision.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DHI showed greater performances on detecting individual MS lesion types compared to other models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DBSI-DNN produced the highest specificity, sensitivity, and accuracy compared to DTI-DNN, MTR-DNN, and cMRI-DNN models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies the preprint's comparative-performance claim by specifying that DBSI-DNN attained the highest specificity, sensitivity, and accuracy versus comparator models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DHI significantly improves the detection and classification accuracy for various MS lesion types. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The efficacy and efficiency of DBSI-DNN shows great promise for clinical applications in automatic MS lesion detection and classification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adopts a more cautious/forward-looking phrasing ('shows great promise') and a lower certainty, tempering the preprint's stronger definitive conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Single pairs of T1- and T2-weighted images acquired at low field showed high correspondence to high-field-strength images for estimates of total intracranial volume, surface area cortical volume, subcortical volume, and total brain volume. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Single pairs of T1- and T2-weighted images acquired at low field showed high correspondence to high-field-strength images for estimates of total intracranial volume, surface area cortical volume, subcortical volume, and total brain volume. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this core result verbatim with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Processing images with SynthSR yielded significant improvements in correspondence for total brain volume, white matter volume, total surface area, subcortical volume, cortical volume, and total intracranial volume. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Processing images with SynthSR yielded significant improvements in correspondence for total brain volume, white matter volume, total surface area, subcortical volume, cortical volume, and total intracranial volume. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same processing improvement claims with identical scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Applying super-resolution approaches to low-field imaging improves regional brain volume and surface area accuracy in young people. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Applying super-resolution approaches to low-field imaging improves regional brain volume and surface area accuracy in young people. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion regarding benefits of super-resolution methods for regional measurements.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Finer-scale brain measurements, such as cortical thickness, remain challenging with the limited resolution of low-field systems. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Finer-scale brain measurements, such as cortical thickness, remain challenging with the limited resolution of low-field systems. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this limitation statement verbatim with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The median completion rate of applicable atoms in the model documentation for 12 commonly used Epic models was 39%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Documentation for 12 commonly used models from a single vendor reported a median of 39% of items from the collective reporting requirements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative result (median 39% for 12 vendor models) with the same scope and implication, so the claim is essentially unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most commonly requested atoms had reporting rates of 90% or above, particularly those concerning outcome definition, preprocessing, AUROC, internal validation, and intended clinical use. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Many of the commonly requested items had 100% reporting rates, including items concerning outcome definition and internal validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a stronger (100%) reporting rate and lists a narrower subset of items (outcome definition, internal validation) compared with the preprint's broader 90%+ list, so the claim has been revised and narrowed but presented with higher reported completeness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Half or less of the reviewed documentation reported information related to usefulness, reliability, transparency, and fairness of models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Areas for improvement were identified in reporting items related to model reliability and fairness. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core concern (reliability and fairness) but omits some preprint categories (usefulness, transparency) and frames it less quantitatively, so the claim is clarified and somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Existing model documentation provides less than half of applicable atoms, indicating low adherence rates to entire reporting guidelines. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Documentation for 12 commonly used models from a single vendor reported a median of 39% of items from the collective reporting requirements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's conclusion about low adherence is supported in the publication by the same 39% median finding; the publication frames it as a reported result rather than as an interpretive conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a need for better operationalization of reporting recommendations for predictive models in healthcare. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Consistent reporting recommendations for clinical predictive models are needed for model developers to share necessary information for model deployment. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both the preprint and publication call for improved/consistent reporting recommendations for clinical predictive models; wording is slightly different but the meaning and scope are the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Elevated blood glucose is a key facilitator in the progression of COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Elevated blood glucose is a key facilitator in the progression of COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the core conclusion verbatim (elevated glucose facilitates progression), so this is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Elevated blood glucose levels can explain much of the variance in disease severity seen across the population. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Elevation in glucose levels can facilitate the progression of the disease through multiple mechanisms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the claim more mechanistically (multiple mechanisms) and drops the stronger population-level explanatory claim, so this is a revision with tempered emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Elevated blood glucose increases glucose in the pulmonary airway surface liquid (ASL), which breaks down the primary innate antiviral defenses of the lungs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Elevations of glucose provide ideal conditions for the virus to evade and weaken the immune defense system in the lungs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same lung-immune weakening idea but in broader terms (immune defense weakening) rather than the specific ASL mechanism, so the claim is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Elevated blood glucose causes dysregulations of the immune response that facilitates the cytokine storm and acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Elevation in glucose levels can facilitate the progression of the disease through multiple mechanisms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication groups this mechanism with other mechanisms under a broader statement about facilitation of disease progression, so the more specific claim is presented in clarified, broader terms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Elevated glucose levels act synergistically with SARS-CoV-2-dependent inactivation of angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) to escalate the disease to multi-organ failure and thrombotic events. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Elevation in glucose levels can facilitate the progression of the disease through multiple mechanisms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains a general statement that glucose facilitates progression but omits the specific ACE2-synergy mechanism and downstream sequelae; thus the specific mechanistic claim was revised/tempered in the published text.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The incidence of myocarditis following mRNA vaccines is low but probably highest in males aged 12-29 years. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The incidence of myocarditis after mRNA vaccines is highest in male adolescents and young adults, with rates of 50-139 cases per million for ages 12-17 and 28-147 per million for ages 18-29. (certainty=low, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides more granular incidence estimates for the same demographic group; the core finding is preserved but certainty is stated as lower, so the claim is clarified with tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Evidence for pericarditis following mRNA vaccination was of very low certainty. (certainty=low, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not explicitly restate or quantify the certainty for pericarditis as in the preprint; that specific statement about very low certainty is not present in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most cases of myocarditis involved males, often over 90%, in their 20s, with a short symptom onset of 2 to 4 days after a second dose. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The incidence of myocarditis after mRNA vaccines is highest in male adolescents and young adults, with rates of 50-139 cases per million for ages 12-17 and 28-147 per million for ages 18-29. (certainty=low, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on incidence estimates by age/sex rather than reporting the >90% male proportion and onset timing; the clinical description is therefore clarified and narrowed to incidence metrics with different certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Existing evidence does not strongly support preference of one mRNA vaccine over another, even in young males. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incidence of myocarditis is probably higher after vaccination with mRNA-1273 (Moderna) compared to Pfizer for individuals aged 18-29 years. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents evidence favoring a higher incidence with Moderna in 18-29 year-olds, which revises the preprint's conclusion of no strong vaccine preference by providing a more specific preference for a defined age group.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Continued active surveillance of myocarditis incidence out to 30 days from dosing is recommended. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The clinical course of mRNA-related myocarditis appears to be benign, although longer-term follow-up data are limited. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint recommends active 30-day surveillance; the publication comments on limited longer-term follow-up and benign short-term course but does not explicitly reiterate the 30-day surveillance recommendation, so the recommendation is softened/clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: At baseline, EOC patients with higher relative abundances of peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) may experience greater overall survival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Survival duration in patients with relapsed epithelial ovarian cancer is impacted by baseline abundances of total peripheral blood mononuclear cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the same association between baseline PBMC abundance and survival, preserving scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients with faster disease progression exhibited a shift in CD14 + CD16 − myeloid cells towards a more immunosuppressive phenotype. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A significant increase in CD14+CD16- myeloid cells occurs during treatment, with expansion of monocytic myeloid-derived suppressor cells linked to shorter progression-free survival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication describes the same myeloid expansion and links it to shorter PFS, giving more specific terminology (MDSC) but preserving the substantive result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher expression of CD73 and IDO1 on select leukocyte subsets at baseline was significantly positively correlated with longer progression-free survival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher CD73 and IDO1 expression on certain leukocytes at baseline significantly positively correlates with longer progression-free survival. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the specific biomarker association with PFS; wording and implication are the same, though reported certainty is slightly reduced in the publication metadata.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study proposes blood-based biomarkers for predicting and monitoring responses to immunotherapy in EOC. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study proposes potential biomarkers for the personalization of immunotherapy and response monitoring in epithelial ovarian cancer. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions propose blood-based biomarkers for personalization and monitoring of immunotherapy response; the publication uses slightly different phrasing but the claim is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Validation of these biomarkers through larger-scale studies is required. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The explicit statement calling for larger-scale validation is not present in the published claim set provided, so this specific limitation/recommendation appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The error classification system identifies five different error types with three different severities across five anatomical regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The error classification system identifies five different error types with three different severities across five anatomical regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim is identical to the preprint statement about the error classification system, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DALL-E 3 performed consistently better than Stable Diffusion in generating photorealistic images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DALL-E 3 performed consistently better than Stable Diffusion in generating photorealistic images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance comparison is stated the same way in publication and preprint, so this is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method developed enables systematic comparison of AI-generated photorealistic images of humans. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The error classification system enables systematic comparison of AI-generated photorealistic images of humans. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both texts claim the classification system/method enables systematic comparisons; the core contribution is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Inter-rater agreement was relatively poor, reflecting the subjectivity of the error classification task. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inter-rater agreement was relatively poor, reflecting the subjectivity of the error classification task. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same observation about inter-rater agreement, so this claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study presents the first systematic approach to evaluate AI-generated images of humans that enables model and prompt comparisons. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The error classification system enables systematic comparison of AI-generated photorealistic images of humans. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the system's enabling function but drops the explicit claim of being the 'first' systematic approach; thus the originality claim is revised/tempered while the methodological contribution remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Coded clinical data available in electronic health records facilitated prediction of general treatment response in major depressive disorder.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Coded clinical data available in electronic health records may facilitate prediction of general treatment response in patients with major depressive disorder.
alignments.rationale: The publication softens the claim language from a high-certainty affirmative statement to a tentative 'may facilitate' and lowers certainty; the scope (general prediction from EHR data) remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In a cohort study of 81,630 adults, 55,303 reached a stable antidepressant treatment regimen.
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not report the cohort counts or the explicit result about 55,303 patients reaching stable regimens, so this specific numerical result was omitted from the publication claims list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predictions using generalized linear models or ensembles of decision trees achieved area under receiver operating characteristic curve values of approximately 0.62-0.65.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) for discrimination of the general stability outcome was 0.627 for the supervised topic model with 10 covariates in held-out patients from site A.
alignments.rationale: The general performance range reported in the preprint is replaced by a specific AUC (0.627) for a particular model in the paper; the performance claim is preserved but the reported metric is revised to a precise model/result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Treatment-specific models did not perform better than general treatment outcome models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Building models to predict stability specific to a particular drug did not improve prediction of general stability.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding that drug-specific models did not improve prediction of general stability with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Greater discrimination is likely required for clinical application of the predictions made from coded clinical data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results provide a transparent baseline for future studies aimed at improving prediction of treatment response in major depressive disorder.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the implication as providing a baseline for future improvement (clarifying the next step) rather than explicitly stating a limitation, but both convey the need for improved prediction for clinical use.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Frequent, longitudinal workplace disease surveillance effectively controls SARS-CoV-2 transmission among employees and their household members.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study's scalable and transferable surveillance plan met its primary objectives and represents an innovative public health initiative.
alignments.rationale: The preprint asserts effective control of transmission broadly; the publication emphasizes meeting primary objectives and scalability/innovation without directly restating broad control over transmission, which narrows and tempers the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only two study participants were found to be infected by SARS-CoV-2 during the study, with one being a household member who tested positive prior to enrollment.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific count of two infected participants nor the enrollment timing detail, so this particular numerical result is absent from the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model predicted that without workplace surveillance, up to 7 employees would have become infected, with at most 1 requiring hospitalization.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A COVID-19 infection model predicted that without surveillance intervention, up to 7 employees would have become infected, with at most 1 requiring hospitalization.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the model prediction verbatim in scope and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No other employees or household members contracted COVID-19 over the course of the study.
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not include an explicit statement that no other employees/household members contracted COVID-19, so this specific negative-result claim was not carried into the publication claims list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The surveillance plan is scalable and transferrable, representing an innovative public health initiative that can be integrated with scientific discovery.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study's scalable and transferable surveillance plan met its primary objectives and represents an innovative public health initiative.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the scalability/transferability and innovation language and further states that the plan met primary objectives, increasing confidence in the contribution compared with the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The clinical study successfully reduced the economic burden and loss of productivity associated with standard isolation methods.
alignments.rationale: This economic/productivity reduction claim appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint claims list; it is an added outcome-focused claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: One household member remained SARS-CoV-2 RNA positive for at least 71 days and had quantifiable serum virus-specific antibody concentrations for over 1 year.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a detailed virological/serological observation about a household member that was not in the preprint claims, so this is an added specific result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We estimate 463,000 articles describing RCTs were published from 1990 to July 2020.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We estimate 463,000 articles describing RCTs were published from 1990 to July 2020.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative estimate with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: As DALYs increased for a particular health condition by 10%, the number of RCTs in the same year increased by 5%, but the association was weak.
alignments.pub_claim_text: As DALYs increased for a particular health condition by 10%, the number of RCTs in the same year increased by 5%.
alignments.rationale: The core numeric association (10% DALYs -> 5% more RCTs) is retained; the publication omits the preprint's explicit qualifier about the association being weak, slightly clarifying the wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Conditions disproportionately affecting countries with lower socio-economic development appear relatively under-researched for their disease burden.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Conditions disproportionately affecting countries with lower socioeconomic development appear relatively under-researched for their disease burden.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about under-research relative to burden for lower socioeconomic countries with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Research priorities are not well optimized to reduce the global burden of disease, with most RCTs produced by highly developed countries.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Research priorities are not well optimised to reduce the global burden of disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core conclusion that research priorities are misaligned with global burden, but shortens the phrasing and omits the explicit mention that most RCTs are produced by highly developed countries; the meaning is preserved but slightly condensed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Each 10% shift in DALYs towards countries with low and middle socio-economic development was associated with a 4% reduction in RCTs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Each 10% shift in DALYs towards countries with low and middle socioeconomic development was associated with a 4% reduction in RCTs.
alignments.rationale: The numerical association is reproduced exactly in the publication with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) are associated with significant reductions in human mobility.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NPIs are associated with significant reductions in human mobility.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result attributing reduced mobility to NPIs with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Changes in mobility can be used to forecast COVID-19 infections.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Changes in mobility can be used to forecast COVID-19 infections.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same forecasting claim about mobility and infections directly and without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mobility data substantially improves 10-day case rates forecasts at the county, state, and global levels.
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not include the specific quantitative claim that mobility data substantially improves 10-day forecasts across county/state/global scales, so this specific forecasting-strength claim was not carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A shelter-in-place policy targeting a 10% increase in the amount of time spent at home would decrease the propagation of new cases by 32% by the end of a 10-day period.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not present this precise policy-effect estimate (10% increase -> 32% reduction over 10 days), so the specific quantified policy result appears to have been removed from the published claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Publicly available data on human mobility can be used to evaluate the effectiveness of NPIs.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly frames mobility data as usable to evaluate NPI effectiveness, a claim about application/utility that was not present among the preprint claims and thus is added.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach requires only publicly-available data and involves minimal assumptions about disease dynamics.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a methodological/implementation claim about data availability and assumptions that was not in the preprint's claims list, so this is an added contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Drugs from antihypertensive agents, urologicals, and antithrombotic agents form a similar class with a significantly higher incidence of pulmonary adverse drug events (pADEs).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Drugs from antihypertensive agents, urologicals, and antithrombotic agents form a similar class with a significantly higher incidence of pulmonary ADEs.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same class-level finding about higher pulmonary ADE incidence for the listed drug groups with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Macitentan and bosentan were associated with 64% and 56% of pADEs, respectively, validating the methods utilized in this study.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Macitentan and bosentan were associated with 64% and 56% of pulmonary ADEs, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same numerical associations for these two drugs; the validation interpretation is omitted but the core result is identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study confirmed the hypothesis that drugs from the same drug class could have very different pADE profiles affecting outcomes in acute respiratory illness.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Drugs from the same drug class could have very different pulmonary ADE profiles affecting outcomes in acute respiratory illness.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same conclusion about within-class heterogeneity in pulmonary ADE profiles; wording and certainty are consistent with the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Several individual drugs have significant differences between their drug classes and compared to other drug classes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Several individual drugs have significant differences between their drug classes and compared to other drug classes.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the preprint's claim about significant differences for individual drugs relative to their classes and others.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Doxazosin and rilmenidine were found to have the least pulmonary ADEs in selected drugs from hypertension patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a specific finding about the lowest-risk drugs (doxazosin and rilmenidine) that was not present in the preprint claims, so this is an added specific result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lung auscultation and reliable interpretation of lung sounds of children are usually not feasible in first-level facilities in Bangladesh and other low- and middle-income countries.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quality of lung sound recordings by primary healthcare workers using the Feelix Smart Stethoscope will be assessed against a predefined quality threshold.
alignments.rationale: The preprint asserts broad infeasibility at first-level facilities; the paper instead frames the issue as an empirical assessment of recording quality with a specific device, changing the claim from a general infeasibility statement to an evaluated, context-specific one.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating automated lung sound classification within the current IMCI pneumonia diagnostic algorithm may improve childhood pneumonia diagnostic accuracy at LMIC first-level facilities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Including lung auscultation in the IMCI algorithm could improve the specificity of pneumonia diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: Both claims argue that adding auscultation improves IMCI diagnosis, but the publication specifies improvement in specificity rather than the broader 'diagnostic accuracy,' narrowing and clarifying the effect.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study will assess the feasibility of recording lung sounds by frontline community health workers who do not usually use conventional stethoscopes during clinical care.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quality of lung sound recordings by primary healthcare workers using the Feelix Smart Stethoscope will be assessed against a predefined quality threshold.
alignments.rationale: Both describe assessing recording quality/feasibility by frontline/primary healthcare workers; the publication adds device name but retains the same substantive contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine-learning algorithm developed by Johns Hopkins and Sonavi Labs will detect abnormal lung sounds and be compared with classifications by human listeners/paediatricians.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The reliability and performance of recorded lung sound interpretations by an automated algorithm will be compared with reference paediatrician interpretations.
alignments.rationale: Both describe comparing an automated algorithm's interpretations of lung sounds with paediatrician/reference human interpretations; the change is largely wording, not meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study will not have chest radiography findings of enrolled children, which is considered by many a gold standard for pneumonia diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims and results list do not mention absence of chest radiography data or this limitation, so the preprint limitation was omitted in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An LPS map was defined using 4DCT-derived features of LV mechanics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: An LPS map was defined using 4-dimensional computed tomography-derived features of left ventricular mechanics.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution using equivalent wording (4DCT spelled out); there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LPS correlated with CRT response, reclassifying 25% of the subjects into low-probability of response and 25% into high-probability of response.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LPS correlated with CRT response, reclassifying 25% of the subjects into low probability of response, 25% into high probability of response, and 50% unchanged.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the explicit breakdown that the remaining 50% were unchanged, clarifying the original reclassification claim without changing its meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Subjects with an LPS≥ Q 3 had a posttest probability of responding of 90%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Subjects with an LPS≥ Q3 (upper-quartile) had a posttest probability of responding of 90%.
alignments.rationale: This result is restated identically in the publication (minor notation clarification only).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AUC of the ROC curve for identifying responders with an LPS threshold was 87%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the curve of the receiver operating characteristic curve for identifying responders with an LPS threshold was 87%.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same quantitative performance metric without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The present findings need verification in larger independent data sets and prospective trials.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The present findings need verification in larger independent data sets and prospective trials.
alignments.rationale: The limitation/next-steps statement appears identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Random forest-based models and LASSO regression-based models effectively leverage multiple data sources to produce accurate predictions of dengue incidence.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Random forest-based models and LASSO regression-based models effectively leverage multiple data sources to produce accurate predictions of dengue incidence.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this methodological/result claim directly; no substantive change is present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Real-time internet search data are the strongest predictors of dengue incidence for predictions assuming long delays in epidemiological data availability.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Real-time internet search data are the strongest predictors of dengue incidence for predictions assuming long delays in epidemiological data availability.
alignments.rationale: The content is the same, but the publication reports this with lower stated certainty (medium vs high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed methodological framework achieves meaningful and actionable estimates of dengue incidence across cities with different demographic, geographic, and epidemic characteristics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed methodological framework achieves meaningful and actionable estimates of dengue incidence across cities with varying demographic, geographic, and epidemic characteristics.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the claim about framework generalizability with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of random forest and LASSO regression models is comparable on average, but the random forest method produces fewer extreme outliers, making it more robust.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest method produces fewer extreme outliers compared to LASSO regression, making it more robust for dengue incidence predictions.
alignments.rationale: Both claims note RF's robustness via fewer extreme outliers; the publication states this with higher certainty (certainty high), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A prediction model using surrogates developed for predicting any-onset preeclampsia achieved an AUROC of 0.85 and was well-replicated in an independent dataset with no intervention.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A prediction model using surrogates developed for predicting any-onset preeclampsia achieved an AUROC of 0.85 and was well-replicated in an independent dataset with no intervention.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the model performance and replication result verbatim; no substantive change is present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The blood biomarkers identified in the prediction model included transcripts of integrin-α5 (ITGA5), interferon regulatory factor-6 (IRF6), and P2X purinoreceptor-7 (P2RX7).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Blood biomarkers identified in the prediction model included transcripts of integrin-α5 (ITGA5), interferon regulatory factor-6 (IRF6), and P2X purinoreceptor-7 (P2RX7).
alignments.rationale: Both versions list the same biomarkers as model features without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most predicted events among any-onset preeclampsia were classified as cluster A based on ITGA5, while only a minority were found among positives in the COVID-19 dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most predicted events among any-onset preeclampsia were classified as cluster A defined by ITGA5, while only a minority were found among positives in the COVID-19 dataset.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the clustering observation with equivalent content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model predicts preeclampsia but not COVID-19 infection, indicating that the important predictors are maternal-blood genes that are not extremely expressed.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model successfully identified blood biomarkers that predict preeclampsia without overlap with COVID-19 infection biomarkers.
alignments.rationale: The publication phrases the distinction more directly and with higher certainty (high vs medium), clarifying the overlap issue and amplifying confidence in the separation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive performance and biological relevance of the identified biomarkers should be validated in future experiments.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive performance and biological relevance of the identified biomarkers should be validated in future experiments.
alignments.rationale: The recommendation for further validation is preserved but reported with higher stated certainty in the publication (medium vs low), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: COVID-19 in UK school-aged children due to SARS-CoV-2 Delta strain B.1.617.2 resembles illness due to the Alpha variant B.1.1.7, with short duration and similar symptom burden.
alignments.pub_claim_text: COVID-19 in UK school-aged children due to SARS-CoV-2 Delta strain B.1.617.2 resembles illness due to the Alpha variant B.1.1.7.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same overall conclusion that Delta resembles Alpha in this population; wording is slightly briefer but the substantive claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Symptom burden over the first 28 days was slightly greater with Delta compared with Alpha infection in older children.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Symptom burden over the first 28 days was slightly greater with Delta compared with Alpha infection.
alignments.rationale: The publication drops the 'in older children' qualifier, presenting the effect more generally; this broadens the scope from an age subgroup to a wider population and revises the claim wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The odds of several symptoms, including headache and fever, were higher with Delta than Alpha infection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The odds of presenting several symptoms were higher with Delta than Alpha infection, including headache and fever.
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the same symptom-specific increased odds with Delta; the publication wording is equivalent in meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Illness duration was short with either variant, with a median of 5 days for both Alpha and Delta infections.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Median illness duration was short with either variant, at 5 days for both Alpha and Delta infections.
alignments.rationale: The median illness-duration result is reproduced verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study adds quantitative information to the debate on whether there are meaningful clinical differences in COVID-19 due to Alpha vs. Delta variants.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this meta-level contribution statement about adding to the debate, so this preprint contribution was omitted in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only two variables, population density and proportion of African Americans, significantly impact COVID-19 severity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only two variables, population density and proportion of African Americans, significantly impact COVID-19 severity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim is effectively identical in content and certainty to the preprint claim, so there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study employs advanced feature selection techniques from machine learning to analyze predictors of COVID-19 severity. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study employs advanced feature selection techniques from machine learning to analyze predictors of COVID-19 severity. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The methods/contribution statement is carried forward verbatim with no change in scope or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach used in this study may be useful for selecting significant determinants of disease progression across diverse geographic regions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach used in this study may be useful for selecting significant determinants of disease progression across diverse geographic regions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same concluding scope and level of confidence as the preprint without substantive alteration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adolescent and young adult males are at the highest risk of myocarditis after mRNA vaccination. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The incidence of myocarditis after mRNA vaccines is highest in male adolescents and young adults, with rates of 50-139 cases per million for ages 12-17 and 28-147 per million for ages 18-29. (certainty=low, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same central conclusion but adds numeric incidence estimates and downgrades certainty, so it is a clarification with tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incidence of myocarditis in children aged 5-11 may be very rare but certainty was low. (certainty=low, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or reference the 5-11 age group incidence claim, indicating the specific claim was not carried into the published paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incidence of myocarditis is probably higher after vaccination with Moderna compared to Pfizer for 18-29 year-old males and females. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incidence of myocarditis is probably higher after vaccination with mRNA-1273 (Moderna) compared to Pfizer for individuals aged 18-29 years. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same comparative incidence finding for the 18-29 age group with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Waiting more than 30 days between doses may be preferred to lower the incidence of myocarditis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Waiting for more than 30 days between doses of mRNA vaccines might lower the incidence of myocarditis or pericarditis in individuals aged 12-39 years. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claim preserves the same recommendation but broadens the specified age range and lowers the stated certainty, so it is a clarification with broader scope and tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most cases of mRNA related myocarditis appear to be mild and self-limiting, although longer term follow-up data is limited. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Longer term follow-up data suggested that more than 50% of patients with mRNA-related myocarditis experienced persistent echocardiogram abnormalities or ongoing symptoms. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents follow-up data suggesting persistent abnormalities in a substantial fraction of cases, which revises the preprint's characterization of most cases as mild and self-limiting.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One dose of BNT162b2 vaccine reduced risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection for at least 90 days in CYP aged 12-17 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: One dose of BNT162b2 vaccine reduced risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection for at least 90 days in children and adolescents aged 12-17 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vaccination provided lower protection against the Omicron variant compared to the Delta variant. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vaccine protection varied for SARS-CoV-2 variant type, being lower for the Omicron variant than for the Delta variant. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements make the same comparative point about lower protection against Omicron; the publication frames it as a conclusion but retains the claim and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vaccinated CYP who contracted SARS-CoV-2 during the Delta period experienced milder disease than unvaccinated CYP. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vaccinated children and adolescents who contracted SARS-CoV-2 during the Delta period had milder disease than unvaccinated children and adolescents. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the published report assigns a lower certainty rating, so the claim content is unchanged but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Post-vaccination local side-effects were common, while systemic side-effects were uncommon and resolved quickly. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Post-vaccination local side-effects were common, while systemic side-effects were uncommon and resolved within a few days. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same finding but adds a short timeframe for resolution, making the description slightly more specific (clarified) without changing certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Overall disease profile was similar in both vaccinated and unvaccinated CYP. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report a general claim that overall disease profiles were similar; instead it reports variant- and vaccination-specific differences, so this broader preprint conclusion is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified three clusters of IPF patients with statistically significant differences in lung function and mortality between groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified three clusters of patients with IPF with statistically significant differences in lung function and mortality between groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the clustering result and its statistical significance exactly as in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gene enrichment analysis implicated dysregulation of mitochondrial homeostasis, apoptosis, cell cycle and innate and adaptive immunity in the pathogenesis underlying these groups. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gene enrichment analysis implicated mitochondrial homeostasis, apoptosis, cell cycle and innate and adaptive immunity in the pathogenesis underlying these groups. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The biological pathways implicated are the same in the publication; wording and certainty are effectively maintained, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed and validated a 13-gene cluster classifier that predicted mortality in IPF with a hazard ratio of 4.25. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We developed and validated a 13-gene cluster classifier that predicted mortality in IPF. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the classifier result but omits the specific hazard ratio reported in the preprint, making the published claim slightly less detailed (clarified) while preserving the main result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The clusters identified in this study could provide valuable insight into the underlying biological processes driving the considerable clinical heterogeneity in IPF. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These clusters could be representative of distinct pathophysiological states, which would support the theory of multiple endotypes of IPF. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the implication more explicitly in terms of distinct pathophysiological states and endotypes, which is a clarification of the preprint's inference.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our gene expression-based classifier could be a useful tool for patient stratification and outcome prediction in IPF. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our classifier could be a useful tool in patient stratification and outcome prediction in IPF. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same proposed utility for the classifier with similar wording and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Older adults with frailty and individuals living in most deprived areas have increased odds of post-vaccination SARS-CoV-2 infection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Frailty was associated with post-vaccination infection in older adults (≥60 years) after their first vaccine dose. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the frailty association but specifies older adults (≥60) and first-dose timing, narrowing the scope relative to the broader preprint phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vaccination is associated with reduced odds of hospitalisation and high acute-symptom burden in individuals who contract COVID-19 post-vaccination. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vaccination was associated with reduced odds of hospitalisation or having more than five symptoms in the first week of illness following the first or second dose. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the protective association but provides a specific symptom-count threshold and notes applicability after first or second dose, clarifying the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vaccinated individuals with post-vaccination infection report fewer symptoms compared to unvaccinated individuals, with some being entirely asymptomatic. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vaccinated participants were more likely to be completely asymptomatic, especially if they were 60 years or older. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes asymptomatic status with a stronger certainty and adds an age-specific amplification (≥60), so the claim is clarified and confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Frail older adults and those living in more deprived areas are at higher risk of contracting COVID-19 post-vaccination, but illness appears less severe in most individuals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals living in highly deprived areas had increased odds of post-vaccination infection following their first vaccine dose. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the deprivation association as a specific result (first-dose timing) and does not explicitly restate the preprint's comment about illness severity; mapping narrows the original combined claim to the deprivation association.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Strategies such as timely prioritisation of booster vaccination and optimised infection control could be considered for at-risk groups. (certainty=low, type=implication)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At-risk populations must be targeted in efforts to boost vaccine effectiveness and infection control measures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication advances the preprint's suggestion into a firmer recommendation with stronger language and higher certainty, so the implication is clarified and confidence amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Delta variant is more transmissible than the Alpha variant. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Delta variant appeared more transmissible than the Alpha variant, with a transmissibility factor of 1.49. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion about greater transmissibility but quantifies it (factor 1.49), so the claim is clarified rather than changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: COVID-19 from Delta or Alpha infections is clinically similar in terms of symptom profile and illness duration. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: COVID-19 from Delta or Alpha infections is similar in terms of symptoms and hospitalisation rates. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps an overall similarity conclusion but frames it with different endpoints (symptoms and hospitalisation) and at lower certainty, thus clarifying and tempering the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vaccination markedly reduces the risk of Delta infection by 69-84%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vaccination markedly reduced the risk of Delta infection by 69-84%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative vaccine effectiveness estimate against Delta with the same conclusion and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The odds of any given symptom lasting ≥7 days was either lower or unchanged with Delta infection compared to Alpha infection. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Illness duration of 28 days or more was lower with Delta infection compared to Alpha infection, although it remained unchanged in unvaccinated individuals. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports an effect on a different duration threshold (≥28 days) and provides subgroup nuance, so the original 7-day claim was revised and narrowed in scope with tempered certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hospitalization for COVID-19 was unchanged between Delta and Alpha infections. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: COVID-19 from Delta or Alpha infections is similar in terms of symptoms and hospitalisation rates. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reaches the same conclusion about similar hospitalisation rates but at lower certainty, so the semantic content is unchanged while confidence is tempered.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Symptom burden in the first week was higher with Delta infection compared to Alpha infection. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific finding about higher first-week symptom burden was not present in the preprint and therefore appears as an added result in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive performance of models exceeded age-sex-based predictions in all but one cancer type. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive performance of the pan-cancer risk models built on Danish data showed good discrimination with a concordance index of 0.81. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a specific performance metric (c-index) supporting the claim of good predictive performance, clarifying the original comparative statement though not restating the exact age-sex comparison.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models trained on Danish data perform similarly on the UK Biobank without any additional retraining. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models demonstrated similar performance in the UK Biobank, with a concordance index of 0.66 when controlling for shifts in the age distribution. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms similar performance on UK Biobank and adds a performance metric and note about controlling for age shifts, clarifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Data available in national electronic health databases can be used to approximate cancer risk factors and enable risk predictions in most cancer types. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Data available in national electronic health databases can be used to approximate cancer risk factors and enable risk predictions in most cancer types. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim repeats the preprint conclusion almost verbatim with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ability to identify subsets of the population earlier compared to age-sex-based screening may improve the efficiency of current screening programs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Electronic health record-based risk models could supplement screening efforts with the emergence of multi-cancer early detection tests. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the potential screening benefit as a supplement to screening specifically in the context of emerging multi-cancer tests, narrowing and revising the original broader suggestion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study shows that national electronic health databases can help to identify individuals of increased risk of cancer across many organ sites. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cancer risks were associated with a broad range of preceding diagnoses and health factors, in addition to heritable components. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides more detailed characterization of the types of predictors (diagnoses, health factors, heritable components), which clarifies the mechanism by which EHRs can identify higher-risk individuals.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Climate and atmospheric variables are the strongest predictors of the distributions of Buruli ulcer and Mycobacterium ulcerans. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Proximity to waterbodies was identified as the strongest predictor of suitability for Buruli ulcer (BU). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication identifies different, more specific predictors (e.g., proximity to waterbodies) than the preprint's more general climate/atmospheric claim, revising the predictor attribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The estimated population living in areas predicted suitable for Mycobacterium ulcerans and Buruli ulcer is 29.1 million. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The estimated population living in areas predicted suitable for Mycobacterium ulcerans and Buruli ulcer is 46.1 million. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same type of population-estimate claim but reports a substantially different (higher) numeric estimate, so the claim is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ecological niche for Buruli ulcer extends beyond the range of known endemic areas, indicating potential undetected cases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patchy foci of suitability for BU were identified throughout West and Central Africa, including areas with no previous evidence of the disease. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication supports the same conclusion about suitability beyond known endemic areas and potential undetected cases, using similar geographic phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The high-resolution suitability maps can guide case search activities to identify endemic areas beyond the known endemic range. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generated maps could inform burden estimations and case searches, enhancing the understanding of the spatial distribution of BU in Africa. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the case-search guidance claim and additionally highlights use for burden estimation, broadening the stated applications of the maps.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Suitability for Mycobacterium ulcerans is predicted to be wider than, but geographically overlapping with, that for Buruli ulcer. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report an explicit claim comparing the relative geographic extents of suitability for M. ulcerans and BU, so this specific comparative claim appears to be omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Deforestation and potential evapotranspiration were the strongest predictors of suitability for Mycobacterium ulcerans. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces specific predictor findings for M. ulcerans (deforestation and evapotranspiration) that were not present in the preprint, so this is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Plasma HA was closely relevant to clinical parameters, including lymphocytes, CRP, D-dimer, and fibrinogen, as well as the mass and volume of pulmonary lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Hyaluronic acid (HA) is closely relevant to clinical parameters in COVID-19 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the central result that HA correlates with clinical parameters in COVID-19; the wording is slightly condensed but meaning is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hymecromone remarkably reduced HA via downregulating HAS2/HAS3 expression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Hymecromone remarkably reduced HA by downregulating HAS2/HAS3 expression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This mechanistic result is repeated verbatim in the publication with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The number of lymphocytes recovered more quickly in hymecromone-treated patients compared to the control group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lymphocytes recovered more quickly in hymecromone-treated patients than in the control group. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The direction and substance of the finding are the same, but the publication reports it with lower certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 89% of patients with hymecromone treatment had pulmonary lesion absorption while only 42% of patients in the control group had pulmonary lesion absorption. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 89% of patients treated with hymecromone had pulmonary lesion absorption compared to 42% in the control group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this numeric efficacy result identically, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hymecromone could significantly improve the clinical manifestations of severe COVID-19 patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Hymecromone is a promising drug for COVID-19 and deserves further investigation. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens the clinical conclusion to a promising candidate warranting further study, revising the stronger therapeutic claim and lowering confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Insufficient sleep is associated with long-lasting adverse effects on mental health, cognition, and brain function in adolescents. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Insufficient sleep negatively affects children's mental health, cognition, brain function, and brain structure over a two-year period. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same overall negative effects claim but clarifies timescale (two years) and broadens the phrasing to children/early adolescence, refining the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sufficient sleep is linked to fewer behavioral problems and better cognitive performance in adolescents. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Insufficient sleep negatively affects children's mental health, cognition, brain function, and brain structure over a two-year period. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the inverse relationship (insufficient sleep harms outcomes) with similar implications for sufficient sleep; this reframing clarifies the effect rather than contradicting it.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Identified brain measures at baseline mediate the effect of sufficient sleep on behavioral assessments over time. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cortico-basal ganglia functional connections mediate the effects of insufficient sleep on depression, thought problems, and crystallised intelligence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies which brain measures and which behavioral outcomes are mediated, providing a clearer and broader mechanistic account of the mediation claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides evidence for potential neural mechanisms that underlie the adverse effects of insufficient sleep in adolescents. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These results provide population-level evidence for the long-lasting effect of insufficient sleep on neurocognitive development in early adolescence. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens and frames the conclusion as population-level evidence with higher certainty about long-lasting neurocognitive effects, amplifying confidence relative to the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The effect sizes of between-group differences in behavioral measures at baseline and two-year follow-up were significantly correlated. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific statistical finding about correlated effect sizes across time points was not present in the preprint and therefore appears as an added result in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Repeated rounds of mass drug administration (MDA) are essential for controlling transmission of five recognized neglected tropical diseases (NTDs).
